tEn: Balanced Life and Continuous Learning: A Conversation with a Tech Investor
tZh: 平衡生活与持续学习：与一位科技投资者的对话
sEn: A tech investor discusses the importance of a balanced life, embracing multiple interests, and the joy of continuous learning and starting over.
sZh: 一位科技投资者探讨了平衡生活、拥抱多元兴趣的重要性，以及持续学习和重新开始的乐趣。

consensus.en: Specialization is for insects; humans should try everything in life.
consensus.zh: 专业化是昆虫的事；人类应该尝试生活中的一切。

consensus.en: Reading should satisfy genuine curiosity, not complete books as a vanity metric.
consensus.zh: 阅读应满足真正的好奇心，而非以读完书作为虚荣指标。

consensus.en: Happiness is a choice; desire is a contract to be unhappy until you get what you want.
consensus.zh: 幸福是一种选择；欲望是让自己不快乐直到得到想要的契约。

consensus.en: To get rich, own a piece of a business; renting your time won't make you wealthy.
consensus.zh: 要致富，必须拥有一部分企业；出租时间无法让你富有。

consensus.en: Modern struggle is resisting addiction to social media, sugar, and other abundance diseases.
consensus.zh: 现代斗争是抵制社交媒体、糖等富足疾病的成瘾。

consensus.en: Meditation is the art of doing nothing; peace comes from within, not solving external problems.
consensus.zh: 冥想是无为的艺术；平静来自内心，而非解决外部问题。

contrarian.en: Universal Basic Income is a non-solution to a non-problem; automation creates new jobs.
contrarian.zh: 全民基本收入是对非问题的非解决方案；自动化创造新就业。

contrarian.en: AGI is not coming in our lifetimes; narrow AI is pattern recognition, not general intelligence.
contrarian.zh: AGI（通用人工智能）不会在我们有生之年到来；狭义AI是模式识别，而非通用智能。

contrarian.en: Socialism only works in small groups; with strangers, capitalism aligns incentives better.
contrarian.zh: 社会主义只在小群体中有效；面对陌生人，资本主义能更好地协调激励。

contrarian.en: Income inequality is about different choices, not oppression; equal opportunity matters, not equal outcome.
contrarian.zh: 收入不平等源于不同选择，而非压迫；机会平等重要，而非结果平等。

contrarian.en: Social media platforms are committing slow-motion suicide by picking sides in culture wars.
contrarian.zh: 社交媒体平台在文化战争中选边站，正在缓慢自杀。

contrarian.en: The meaning of life has no single answer; you get to make up your own, which is true freedom.
contrarian.zh: 生命的意义没有单一答案；你可以自己创造，这才是真正的自由。

turns.en: Two, one, boom. All right, we're live. Thank you very much for doing this, man. I really appreciate it. I've been absorbing your information and listening to you talk for quite a while now, so it's great to actually meet you.
turns.zh: 二、一，开拍。好了，我们开始了。非常感谢你来做这个节目，老兄，我真的很感激。我一直在吸收你的信息，听你讲话已经有一段时间了，所以能真正见到你真是太好了。

turns.en: Thanks for having me.
turns.zh: 谢谢你邀请我。

turns.en: My pleasure. You are one of the rare guys that is a big investor, deep in the tech world, but yet you seem to have a very balanced perspective in terms of how to live life, as opposed to just being entirely focused on success and financial success and tech investing, but rather how to live your life in a happy way.
turns.zh: 我的荣幸。你是少数几个既是大型投资者、深入科技界，却又似乎对如何生活有着非常平衡视角的人，而不是完全专注于成功、财务成功和科技投资，而是如何快乐地生活。

turns.en: That's a... it's not balance. You know, I think the reason why people like hearing me is because if you go to a circus and you see a bear, that's kind of interesting but not that much. If you see a unicycle, that's interesting. But you see a bear on a unicycle, that's really interesting. So when you combine things you're not supposed to combine, people get interested. It's like Bruce Lee: striking thoughts, philosophy plus martial arts. And I think it's because at some level, all humans are broad. We're all multivariate, but we get summarized in pithy ways in our lives, and at some deep level we know that's not true. Every human is capable of every experience and every thought. You're a UFC commentator, comedian, podcaster, but you're also more than that. You're also a father, lover, thinker, etc. So I like the model of life that the ancients had, the Greeks, the Romans, where you would start out young, go to school, then go to war, then run a business, then serve in the senate or the government, then become a philosopher. There's an arc to life where you try your hand at everything. As one of my friends says, specialization is for insects. So everyone should be able to do everything. I don't believe in this model anymore of trying to focus your life down on one thing. You've got one life, just do everything you're going to do.
turns.zh: 那不是平衡。你知道，我认为人们喜欢听我说话的原因是，如果你去马戏团看到一只熊，那有点意思，但没那么有意思。如果你看到一辆独轮车，那很有意思。但如果你看到一只熊骑独轮车，那就非常有意思了。所以当你把本不该组合在一起的东西组合起来时，人们就会感兴趣。就像李小龙：引人深思的思想、哲学加上武术。我认为这是因为在某种程度上，所有人类都是广阔的。我们都是多变量的，但在生活中被简化为精炼的方式，而在内心深处我们知道那不是真的。每个人都能经历每一种体验和每一种思想。你是一个UFC评论员、喜剧演员、播客主持人，但你也不止于此。你也是一个父亲、爱人、思考者等等。所以我喜欢古人，希腊人、罗马人，所拥有的生活模式：你年轻时开始，上学，然后去打仗，然后经营生意，然后服务于元老院或政府，然后成为哲学家。生活有一个弧线，你尝试做所有事情。正如我的一位朋友所说，专业化是给昆虫的。所以每个人都应该能做所有事情。我不再相信这种试图将生活聚焦于一件事的模式了。你只有一次生命，就去做你将要做的所有事情吧。

turns.en: I couldn't agree more. I think that sometimes people find certain success in whatever the endeavor is, and then they think that that is their niche and they stick with it and they never change, almost out of fear.
turns.zh: 我完全同意。我认为有时候人们在某项努力中取得了一定的成功，然后他们认为那就是他们的 niche，他们坚持它，从不改变，几乎是出于恐惧。

turns.en: Well, it's hard. There's the analogy around mountain climbing: if you find a mountain and you start climbing, you spend your whole life climbing it, and you get say two-thirds of the way, and then you see the peak is way up there, but you're two-thirds of the way up, you're still really high up. But now to go the rest of the way, you're going to have to go back down to the bottom and look for another path. Nobody wants to do that. People don't want to start over. And it's the nature of later in life that you just don't know the time, so it's very painful to go back down and look for a new path, but that may be the best thing to do. That's why when you look at the greatest artists and creators, they have this ability to start over that nobody else does. Like Elon will be called an idiot and start over doing something brand new that he supposedly is not qualified for. Or when Madonna or Paul Simon or U2 come out with a new album, their existing fans usually hate it because they've adopted a completely new style that they've learned somewhere else. And a lot of times they'll just miss completely. So you have to be willing to be a fool and have that beginner's mind and go back to the beginning to start over. If you're not doing that, you're just getting older.
turns.zh: 嗯，这很难。有一个关于登山的类比：如果你找到一座山并开始攀登，你花了一辈子爬它，你爬到了比如三分之二的地方，然后你看到顶峰还在上面，但你已经爬了三分之二，你仍然很高。但现在要完成剩下的路程，你必须回到山底寻找另一条路。没有人想那样做。人们不想重新开始。而且这是晚年生活的本质：你只是不知道时间，所以回去寻找新路非常痛苦，但那可能是最好的做法。这就是为什么当你看到最伟大的艺术家和创作者时，他们拥有别人没有的重新开始的能力。比如埃隆会被叫做白痴，然后重新开始做一些他本不具备资格的全新的事情。或者当麦当娜、保罗·西蒙或U2发行新专辑时，他们现有的粉丝通常讨厌它，因为他们采用了从别处学来的全新风格。很多时候他们会完全失败。所以你必须愿意做个傻瓜，拥有初学者的心态，回到起点重新开始。如果你不这样做，你只是在变老。

turns.en: I mean, I don't even know if it's willing to be a fool. To me, the most exciting thing is to try to get better at something, to learn things. It's really exciting when you have incremental progress in something you're completely new to.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我甚至不知道这是否是愿意做个傻瓜。对我来说，最令人兴奋的事情是尝试在某件事上变得更好，学习东西。当你在完全陌生的事情上取得渐进式进步时，真的很令人兴奋。

turns.en: Yeah, I live for the aha moment, that moment when you connect two things together that you hadn't connected before, and it fits nicely and solidly, and it helps form a steel framework of understanding in your mind that you can then hang other ideas off of. That's what I live for.
turns.zh: 是的，我为顿悟时刻而活，那个你把你以前没有联系过的两件事联系在一起的时刻，它们完美而牢固地契合，帮助在你的脑海中形成一个钢铁般的理解框架，然后你可以把其他想法挂在上面。那就是我为之而活的。

turns.en: Yeah, it's like curiosity fulfilled. And it's what little children do too. My little son is always asking why, why, why, why, why. And I always try to answer him, and half the times I realize actually I don't really understand why, I just have a memorized answer for you, but that's not really understanding.
turns.zh: 是的，就像好奇心得到了满足。这也是小孩子做的事情。我的小儿子总是问为什么、为什么、为什么、为什么、为什么。我总是试着回答他，但有一半时间我意识到实际上我并不真正理解为什么，我只是有一个背下来的答案给你，但那不是真正的理解。

turns.en: Yeah, those are weird conversations when you're talking to your kids and you say, look, the reality is I don't know a lot of things. I've just memorized a lot of things. And there's certain things that you just can't know. You realize that you have answers for a few things that you've thought through, then you sort of have cover-ups, like trap doors: don't go here, this is just a cover-up, I don't really know the answer to what the meaning of life is or how we got here. And then you've got a whole bunch of memorized stuff, because a lot of intelligence these days is just the external brain pack of civilization. I know it's out there, I know the answers are out there, I know how to look them up, and I've memorized some of them. I kind of understand how money works and the Federal Reserve prints it and what this government thing is, but not really, not good enough to teach it in university.
turns.zh: 是的，当你和你的孩子说话时，那些奇怪的对话：你说，看，现实是我不知道很多事情。我只是记住了很多事情。有些事情你就是无法知道。你意识到你对一些你深思熟虑过的事情有答案，然后你有一些掩饰，就像活板门：别来这里，这只是掩饰，我并不真正知道生命的意义是什么或者我们怎么来到这里的答案。然后你有一大堆背下来的东西，因为现在很多智力只是文明的外部大脑包。我知道它在那里，我知道答案在那里，我知道如何查找它们，而且我记住了一些。我有点理解钱是如何运作的，美联储印钱，以及这个政府的东西是什么，但并不是真正理解，不足以在大学里教它。

turns.en: Yeah, I think people do that with almost everything in life these days. They have a one-page cheat, a brief summary of what the explanation for this very complex subject might be. TL;DR: don't give me the lecture, give me the book; don't give me the book, give me the blog post; don't give me the blog post, give me the tweet; don't give me the tweet, I just already know.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为现在人们对生活中的几乎所有事情都这样做。他们有一页纸的 cheat sheet，一个关于这个非常复杂的主题可能是什么解释的简短摘要。TL;DR：不要给我讲座，给我书；不要给我书，给我博客文章；不要给我博客文章，给我推文；不要给我推文，我已经知道了。

turns.en: I got really fascinated by the way you read, because I thought there was something wrong with me by doing that, but you don't really just read a book to completion. You read and then you pick something else up and you just go based on your whims, whatever you're interested in.
turns.zh: 我对你的阅读方式非常着迷，因为我以为我那样做有问题，但你并不是真的把一本书读完。你读着读着就拿起另一本，然后根据你的兴致，无论你对什么感兴趣，就那样做。

turns.en: Well, I was raised by a single mom in New York, and she used the local library as a daycare center because it was a very tough neighborhood. So she would basically say, when you get back from school, go straight to the library and don't come out until I pick you up late at night. So I used to basically live in the library, and I read everything. I read every magazine, every pictograph, every book, every map. I just ran out of stuff to read. I just read everything. So I got over this idea that reading a large number of books or reading a book to completion is a vanity metric, because really when people are putting up photos on Twitter or Instagram of 'look at my pile of books that I'm reading,' it's a show-off thing, it's a signaling thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我是在纽约由单亲妈妈抚养长大的，她把当地的图书馆当作日托中心，因为那是一个非常艰难的社区。所以她基本上会说，放学后直接去图书馆，直到我深夜来接你才出来。所以我基本上就住在图书馆里，我读了一切。我读了每一本杂志、每一本象形文字书、每一本书、每一张地图。我只是没有东西可读了。我读了所有东西。所以我克服了这种想法，即读很多书或把一本书读完是一种虚荣指标，因为当人们在 Twitter 或 Instagram 上晒出‘看我正在读的一堆书’的照片时，那是一种炫耀，一种信号。

turns.en: Reality is, I would rather read the best hundred books over and over again until I absorb them, rather than read all the books. Your brain has finite information in a finite space. You get enough advice, it all cancels to zero. There's a lot of nonsense in books out there too. So I don't read anymore to complete books; I read to satisfy my genuine intellectual curiosity. And it can be anything—it could be nonsense, it could be history, fiction, science, sci-fi. These days it's mostly sci-fi, philosophy, science, because that's just what I'm interested in. But I will read for understanding. So a really good book, I will flip through; I won't actually read it consecutively in order, and I won't even finish it. I'm looking for ideas, things that I don't understand. And when I find something really interesting, I'll reflect on it, I'll research it, and then when I'm bored of it, I'll drop it or I'll flip to another book. Thanks to electronic books, I've got 50 to 70 books open at any time in my Kindle or iBooks, and I'm just bouncing around between them.
turns.zh: 现实是，我宁愿把最好的100本书反复读直到吸收，而不是读遍所有书。你的大脑信息容量有限，空间也有限。你得到足够多的建议，它们就会相互抵消为零。书里也有很多废话。所以我不再为了读完一本书而读书；我读书是为了满足真正的好奇心。可以是任何东西——可以是废话，可以是历史、小说、科学、科幻。现在主要是科幻、哲学、科学，因为那是我感兴趣的。但我会为了理解而读。所以一本真正的好书，我会翻阅，不会按顺序连续读，甚至不会读完。我在寻找想法，寻找我不理解的东西。当我发现真正有趣的东西时，我会反思、研究，然后当我厌倦了，就会放下或翻到另一本书。多亏了电子书，我的Kindle或iBooks里随时都有50到70本书打开着，我在它们之间跳来跳去。

turns.en: You brought up two awesome points. First of all, the social media aspect of books and basically anything—it's such a weird way to display your life because you're displaying the best aspects of your life in some sort of a glass case. It's an unrealistic version of your life that you cultivate and curate. I'm as guilty of that as anybody. Everybody's guilty of it. I'm guilty of it too.
turns.zh: 你提到了两个很棒的点。首先，书籍以及几乎所有事物的社交媒体方面——这是一种展示生活的奇怪方式，因为你把生活中最好的方面放在某种玻璃柜里展示。这是你精心培养和策划的不真实的生活版本。我和任何人一样有罪。每个人都有罪。我也有罪。

turns.en: Yeah, you know, I mean, I pose with my dog every time I run. Yeah, we're always signaling. It's like rather than really looking at yourself, you're looking at how other people look at you. So it's this one-remove mental image, and it's kind of a disease. Because social media is making celebrities of all of us, and celebrities are the most miserable people in the world, right? Because they have this strong self-image that gets built up, built up by compliments. Every time somebody pays you or me a compliment and we're like, 'Oh well, thank you,' right? Then that builds up an image of who we are. And then one idiot comes along—one out of ten, one out of a hundred—and they can easily tear it down, because it doesn't take many insults to cancel out a lot of compliments. And now you're carrying around this big weighty self-image, and it's just very easy to be attacked. And because you're famous or well-known, people want to attack you. So being a celebrity is no good; it's actually a problem. Like one of my tweets is—and these are all reminders to myself—is 'You want to be rich and anonymous, not poor and famous.'
turns.zh: 是啊，你知道，我的意思是，我每次跑步都会和我的狗摆拍。是的，我们总是在炫耀。这就像不是真正审视自己，而是看别人怎么看你。所以这是一种间接的心理形象，这有点像一种病。因为社交媒体让我们所有人都成了名人，而名人是世界上最痛苦的人，对吧？因为他们有这种强烈的自我形象，被赞美建立起来。每次有人赞美你或我，我们就会说'哦，谢谢'，对吧？那就建立了一个我们是谁的形象。然后一个白痴出现了——十分之一，百分之一——他们可以轻易地摧毁它，因为不需要很多侮辱就能抵消很多赞美。现在你背负着这个沉重的自我形象，很容易受到攻击。因为你出名或知名，人们想攻击你。所以当名人不好；这实际上是个问题。就像我的一条推文——这些都是提醒我自己的——是'你想富有且匿名，而不是贫穷且出名。'

turns.en: There are benefits to it, of course. But we wouldn't do it; it has unusual problems that you don't get trained for, and you really will not understand unless you experience it. You know, I was having this conversation with my wife. We were talking about people that just come up to you and they don't care what you're doing. They don't care if I'm with my daughter, if I'm holding her, if I'm feeding her. You know, we're in the middle of an intense conversation, she's crying—she could be crying—and some bro will come over and just immediately have to take a picture. Doesn't care; his needs supersede the daughter. And my wife was saying that before she knew me, she used to think that's just part of the price of being famous, that people like you, that's just part of the price. And now when it interrupts her life and you know it interrupts the children and interrupts friends, she now she's like, 'This is annoying. This is not healthy. This is not a smart way to interact with people.' And people have this weird challenge that if you become famous, people just want to come to you, especially today, because if they can get a photo of you, then that boosts their social media profile. Like, 'Hey, I'm sitting here with Naval, look at that smile.'
turns.zh: 当然，这有好处。但我们不会去做；它有你不曾受过训练的不寻常问题，除非你经历过，否则你真的不会理解。你知道，我和我妻子有过这样的对话。我们在谈论那些直接走过来的人，他们不在乎你在做什么。他们不在乎我是否和女儿在一起，是否抱着她，是否在喂她。你知道，我们正在激烈交谈，她在哭——她可能在哭——然后某个哥们会走过来，立刻就要拍照。不在乎；他的需求凌驾于女儿之上。我妻子说，在她认识我之前，她曾认为那是成名的代价的一部分，人们喜欢你，那只是代价的一部分。现在当它打扰她的生活，你知道它打扰孩子和朋友，她现在觉得，'这很烦人。这不健康。这不是与人互动的明智方式。'人们有这种奇怪的挑战，如果你出名了，人们就想接近你，尤其是今天，因为如果他们能拍到你的照片，就能提升他们的社交媒体形象。比如，'嘿，我和Naval坐在一起，看那个笑容。'

turns.en: Anonymity is a privilege. On the other hand, it's self-inflicted. I mean, we brought it on ourselves. Yeah, we didn't think we knew what it was, though. We did, but we carry on. So yeah, it tells us we are getting something out of it. So you know, there are times when someone will approach me in public and I'm a little resentful, and there are other times I'm just like, actually I'm really grateful that you know I worked for this, I got this right, this is the payoff. Just embrace it, smile, grit and bear it.
turns.zh: 匿名是一种特权。另一方面，这是自找的。我的意思是，是我们自己招来的。是的，不过我们当时没想过我们知道那是什么。我们确实知道，但我们继续了。所以是的，这说明我们从中得到了什么。所以你知道，有时候有人在公共场合接近我，我会有点不满，而其他时候我只是想，实际上我真的很感激，你知道我为此努力过，我做对了，这是回报。拥抱它，微笑，咬紧牙关忍受。

turns.en: But you have a different sort of celebrity too, right? You're a hero amongst investors and amongst—I mean you've just—I'm a hero among young male geeks, which are some of my favorite people. But that's not the kind of celebrity I think most people set out to get, especially most guys.
turns.zh: 但你也有一种不同的名人效应，对吧？你是投资者中的英雄，在——我的意思是，你只是——我是年轻男性极客中的英雄，他们是我最喜欢的一些人。但这不是我认为大多数人想要的那种名人，尤其是大多数男人。

turns.en: Right, right, right. You want the cute females.
turns.zh: 对对对。你想要可爱的女性。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. I look at my brief little YouTube clips; I have a tiny little podcast going now, and it's like 95% male. Oh sure, yeah, this is very highly 18 to 35.
turns.zh: 是啊，是啊。我看我那些简短的YouTube片段；我现在有一个小小的播客，观众大概95%是男性。哦当然，是的，这非常集中在18到35岁。

transcript.sec: Introduction to the Podcast and Wealth Creation Principles

turns.en: Yeah, we, what do we, what is the numbers? Yeah, it's in the 90s. Yeah, you did that one, you do that one very small podcast where you just have small like three or four minute clips.
turns.zh: 对，我们，那个数字是多少？嗯，90多集了。对，你做了那个，你做了那个很小的播客，每集只有三四分钟的小片段。

turns.en: Yeah, so what it was: I did a tweet storm called "How to Get Rich Without Getting Lucky," and it got pretty popular on Twitter. It's really about wealth creation. I just used the clickbaity title, and it's trying to basically lay out timeless principles of wealth creation that if you absorb them, you become the kind of person who can create wealth, create business, make money. My theory behind that is there are three things everybody wants—actually more than three, but let's start with three basics: everybody wants to be wealthy, everybody wants to be happy, and everybody wants to be fit. I know there's a lot of virtue signaling, like "we don't want money" or "I don't care about being happy" and "happiness is for stupid people," but let's face it: you want to be rich, happy, and healthy. That's the trifecta. Of course, you also want an internally calm state of mind, a loving household, so there are other things, but those three I think can actually be taught. Fitness I'm not going to teach—there are a lot of people who know a heck of a lot more about fitness and health than I do. But I was born poor and miserable, and now I'm pretty well off and very happy, and I worked at those. So I've learned a few things; there are some principles. I try to lay them out in a timeless manner where you can figure it out yourself, because at the end of the day, I can't really teach anything—I can only inspire you and maybe give you a few hooks so you can remember things when they happen or put a name to them. So this podcast ended up explaining this tweet storm. There's a tweet storm with like 36 or 38 tweets, got very famous, translated into dozens of languages. These were principles I came up with for myself when I was really young, around 13 or 14, and I've been carrying them in my head for 30 years, living them. Over time, I realized that sadly or fortunately, the thing I got really good at was looking at businesses and figuring out the point of maximum leverage to actually create wealth and capture some of that, and do it in a very long-term way—not let the banker crash the economy and get wiped out, but build businesses, help people, and provide value, especially applied to modern technology and leverage in this age of infinite leverage we live in. So the podcast is just explaining each tweet. These are little three, four, five-minute snippets. I don't like to say the same thing twice; I don't like to explain in detail. I feel like if you have something original and interesting to say, you should say it—otherwise it's probably been said better. So that podcast tries to be information-dense, very concise, high-impact, timeless, and has all the information I think you need. The principles: if you absorb these and work hard over 10 years, you get what you want. So I've got the one on wealth creation. I'm going to attempt to do one on happiness—it's a big word, but happiness, inner peace, calm, and all that. Because what you want is not to be the guy who succeeds in life while being high-strung, high-stress, unhappy, and leaving a trail of emotional wreckage with you and your loved ones—which is more common than not.
turns.zh: 嗯，事情是这样的：我发了一条推文风暴，标题是《如何不靠运气致富》，在推特上很火。它其实是关于创造财富的。我只是用了个标题党，试图阐述一些永恒的财富创造原则，如果你吸收了这些原则，你就会成为那种能够创造财富、创办企业、赚钱的人。我背后的理论是，每个人都想要三样东西——实际上不止三样，但我们先从三个基本点开始：每个人都想富有，每个人都想快乐，每个人都想健康。我知道有很多人故作清高，说什么“我们不在乎钱”、“我不在乎快乐”、“快乐是蠢人的事”，但面对现实吧：你想变得富有、快乐和健康。这就是三连胜。当然，你还想要内心平静、家庭和睦，所以还有其他东西，但这三样我认为是可以传授的。健身我就不教了——有很多人比我懂得多得多。但我出身贫寒、生活悲惨，现在却相当富有、非常快乐，而且我是通过努力达到的。所以我学到了一些东西，有一些原则。我试图以一种永恒的方式把它们讲出来，让你自己领悟，因为归根结底，我无法真正教给你什么——我只能激励你，也许给你一些提示，让你在事情发生时能记住它们，或者给它们起个名字。所以这个播客最终解释了那条推文风暴。那条推文风暴有36或38条推文，非常出名，被翻译成几十种语言。这些原则是我在很小的时候，大概13、14岁时为自己总结的，我在脑子里装了30年，一直在践行。随着时间的推移，我意识到，可悲或可喜的是，我真正擅长的是审视企业，找出最大杠杆点，从而创造财富并获取一部分财富，而且是以一种非常长期的方式——不是让银行家搞垮经济然后自己归零，而是创办企业、帮助他人、提供价值，尤其是在现代科技和我们所处的这个无限杠杆时代。所以播客就是逐条解释每条推文。这些是三四五分钟的小片段。我不喜欢重复说同一件事，也不喜欢详细解释。我觉得如果你有原创且有趣的东西要说，你就说出来——否则可能别人已经说得更好了。所以那个播客力求信息密集、非常简洁、高冲击力、永恒，并且包含我认为你需要的一切信息。这些原则：如果你吸收了它们，并努力十年，你就能得到你想要的。所以我已经做了关于财富创造的那一集。我打算尝试做一集关于快乐的——这个词很大，但快乐、内心平静、安宁等等。因为你不希望成为那种在生活中成功却紧张、压力大、不快乐，并给亲人和自己留下一串情感创伤的人——这种情况其实更常见。

turns.en: Yeah, because you got to focus, and it's very hard to be great at everything. You want to be the guy or the gal who gets there calmly, quietly, without struggle. You want to be the person who, when there's a crisis going on, is the calmest, coolest cucumber in the room who still figures out the correct answer. One of the things you were saying is that you feel like happiness is something you can learn, and you can teach yourself to be happy even just by adopting the mindset that you are a happy person and proclaiming that to your friends, so you've sort of developed a social contract: "I'm a happy person and I have to live up to that."
turns.zh: 对，因为你必须专注，而且很难样样精通。你想成为那种冷静、安静、不挣扎就能达到目标的人。你想成为那种在危机发生时，房间里最冷静、最镇定，却仍能找出正确答案的人。你之前说过，你觉得快乐是可以学习的，你甚至可以仅仅通过接受自己是一个快乐的人的心态，并向朋友宣告这一点，来教会自己快乐，这样你就建立了一种社会契约：“我是一个快乐的人，我必须做到这一点。”

turns.en: Yeah, I've got hundreds of techniques. But how did I develop that one? Well, there's social consistency, right? Humans have a need to be highly consistent with their past pronouncements. The way I started my first tech company was: I was working inside a larger organization, and I told everybody that I was going to go start a company. I was like, "I hate this place, I'm going to do my own thing, I'm going to be a successful entrepreneur." Six months pass, nine months pass, then people start: "You're still here? I thought you were going to go start a company. Are you lying?" That was the implication. So we kind of know this, right? Social contracts are very powerful. If you want to give up drinking, and you're not serious about it, you'll say, "I'm going to cut back, I'm going to have only one drink a night, I'm going to only drink on weekends." You tell yourself. But if you're serious, you'll announce it on Facebook, you'll tell all your friends, you'll tell your wife, you'll say, "I'm done drinking, I'm throwing everything out of the house, you'll never see me drink again." When you say that, you know you're serious. So I think a lot of these are choices we make, and happiness is just one of those choices. This is unpopular to say because there are people who are actually depressed, chemically or what have you, and there are people who don't believe it's possible because it creates a responsibility on them: "Oh, now if I'm not happy, that's my fault." I'm not saying that. But I'm saying that just like fitness can be a choice, health can be a choice, nutrition can be a choice, working hard and making money can be a choice, happiness is also a choice. "If you're so smart, how come you aren't happy? How come you haven't figured that out?" That's my challenge to all the people who think they're so smart and so capable. If you're so smart and capable, why can't you change this?
turns.zh: 对，我有几百种技巧。但我是怎么发展出那个的呢？嗯，存在社会一致性，对吧？人类有一种需求，要与自己过去的声明高度一致。我创办第一家科技公司的方式是：我在一家大公司工作，我告诉每个人我要去创办一家公司。我说：“我讨厌这个地方，我要做自己的事，我要成为一名成功的企业家。”六个月过去了，九个月过去了，然后人们开始问：“你还在这儿？我以为你要去创办公司。你在撒谎吗？”这就是潜台词。所以我们都知道这个，对吧？社会契约非常强大。如果你想戒酒，但你不是认真的，你会说：“我要少喝点，每晚只喝一杯，我只在周末喝。”你对自己说。但如果你是认真的，你会在Facebook上宣布，告诉所有朋友，告诉你的妻子，你会说：“我戒酒了，我把家里所有的酒都扔了，你再也不会看到我喝酒了。”当你这么说的时候，你知道你是认真的。所以我认为很多都是我们做出的选择，快乐就是其中之一。这么说不太受欢迎，因为有些人确实抑郁，化学原因或其他什么，还有些人不相信这是可能的，因为这给他们带来了责任：“哦，如果我不快乐，那就是我的错。”我不是那个意思。但我是说，就像健身可以是一种选择，健康可以是一种选择，营养可以是一种选择，努力工作和赚钱可以是一种选择一样，快乐也是一种选择。“如果你这么聪明，为什么你不快乐？为什么你还没想明白？”这是我对所有那些自认为很聪明、很有能力的人的挑战。如果你这么聪明、这么有能力，为什么你改变不了这个？

turns.en: There are a bunch of people though that actually take pleasure in being miserable. There's something about the pursuit of excellence and of success that supersedes all other pursuits, that in their eyes it is the peak, the pinnacle, the most important thing. It's not a trade-off.
turns.zh: 但有一群人实际上以痛苦为乐。追求卓越和成功超越了所有其他追求，在他们眼中，那是巅峰、顶点、最重要的事情。这不是一个权衡。

turns.en: I would argue that if—now when I say happy, happy is one of those words that means a zillion different things, right? It's like love, right? What does that mean? "I love cheese." Yeah. I'm going to define it a little more tightly. So let's go back to desire. This is old, old Buddhist wisdom. I'm not saying anything original. But desire to me is a contract that you make with yourself to be unhappy until you get what you want.
turns.zh: 我会争辩说，如果——现在当我说快乐时，快乐是那种含义无数的词之一，对吧？就像爱，对吧？那是什么意思？“我爱奶酪。”对。我要更严格地定义它。所以让我们回到欲望。这是非常古老的佛教智慧。我没说什么原创的东西。但对我来说，欲望是你与自己签订的一份合同，约定在得到你想要的东西之前，你一直不快乐。

turns.en: You allow yourself to be unhappy until you get what you want. I keep that in front of my mind. So when I'm unhappy about something, I look for what is the underlying desire that I have that's not being fulfilled. It's okay to have desires. You're a biological creature, you're put on this earth, you have to do something, you have to have desires, you have a mission. But don't have too many. Don't pick them up unconsciously, don't pick them up randomly, don't have thousands of them. My coffee is too cold, doesn't taste quite right, I'm not sitting perfectly, oh I wish it was warmer, you know, my dog pooped on the lawn, I didn't like that. Whatever it is, pick your one overwhelming desire. It's okay to suffer over that one, but on all the others you want to let them go, so you can be calm and peaceful and relaxed, and then you'll perform a better job.
turns.zh: 你允许自己不快乐，直到得到想要的东西。我把这句话放在心里。所以当我不开心时，我会寻找背后未被满足的欲望。有欲望是可以的。你是生物，来到地球上，必须做点什么，必须有欲望，有使命。但不要太多。不要无意识地、随意地捡起欲望，不要有成千上万个。我的咖啡太凉了，味道不对，我坐得不舒服，哦我希望它热一点，我的狗在草坪上拉屎了，我不喜欢那样。不管是什么，只选择一个压倒性的欲望。为那个受苦是可以的，但其他的都要放下，这样你才能平静、安宁、放松，然后表现更好。

turns.en: Most people, when you're unhappy like a depressed person, it's not that they have a very clear calm mind. They're too busy in their mind, their sense of self is too strong. They're sitting indoors all the time, their mind working working working, they're thinking too much. Well, if you want to be a high performance athlete, how good of an athlete are you going to be if you're always having epileptic seizures? If you're always like twitching and running around and jumping and your limbs are flailing out of control? The same way, if you want to be effective in business, you need a clear, calm, cool, collected mind. Warren Buffett plays bridge all day long and goes for walks in the sun. He doesn't sit around like constantly loading his brain with non-stop information and getting worked up about every little thing.
turns.zh: 大多数人，当你不快乐时，比如抑郁的人，并不是因为他们头脑非常清晰冷静。他们脑子里太忙了，自我意识太强。他们总是坐在室内，脑子不停地工作，想得太多。如果你想成为高性能运动员，如果你总是癫痫发作，总是抽搐、乱跑、跳跃、四肢失控，你能成为多好的运动员？同样，如果你想在商业中有效，你需要清晰、冷静、镇定、从容的头脑。沃伦·巴菲特整天打桥牌，在阳光下散步。他不会坐在那里不停地往脑子里塞信息，为每件小事激动。

turns.en: We live in an age of infinite leverage. What I mean by that is that your actions can be multiplied a thousand fold, either by broadcasting on a podcast, or by investing capital, or by having people work for you, or by writing code. So because of that, the impacts of good decision making are much higher than they used to be, because now you can influence thousands or millions of people through your decisions or your code. So a clear mind leads to better judgment, leads to a better outcome. So a happy, calm, peaceful person will make better decisions and have better outcomes. So if you want to operate at peak performance, you have to learn how to tame your mind, just like you have to learn how to tame your body.
turns.zh: 我们生活在一个无限杠杆的时代。我的意思是，你的行动可以放大一千倍，无论是通过播客广播、投资资本、让别人为你工作，还是写代码。因此，好的决策的影响比以前大得多，因为现在你可以通过决策或代码影响成千上万的人。所以清晰的头脑带来更好的判断，带来更好的结果。所以一个快乐、平静、安宁的人会做出更好的决策，得到更好的结果。所以如果你想在巅峰状态运作，你必须学会驯服你的头脑，就像你必须学会驯服你的身体一样。

turns.en: I love what you're saying. Warren Buffett might not be the best example because he drinks like I think six Cokes a day and he eats mostly McDonald's and he's still alive somehow. So that shows you that low stress is more... nobody looks like... how old is he? I mean he's a fairly old man. Charlie Munger's I think in his 90s, right? Yeah, he's made it really far. I wonder what Warren's doing, you know? I mean, he's got to know that's bad for him, but it's terrible, he doesn't care. He doesn't care. I think he's just a little stressed. Yeah, stress is right. So he just enjoys that Coca-Cola and that's probably... maybe there is a trade-off, right? Like maybe him enjoying that junk food and that Coke, just that pleasing of the mind, is maybe better than him just eating wheatgrass shots and salads and just being super worked up about everything. It's like if you need your glass of red wine to de-stress and calm down, that's probably better than you flying off the rails, right?
turns.zh: 我喜欢你说的。沃伦·巴菲特可能不是最好的例子，因为他每天大概喝六瓶可乐，主要吃麦当劳，但还活着。这说明低压力更重要……没人看起来……他多大了？他年纪很大了。查理·芒格大概九十多岁了吧？对，他活得很久。我想知道沃伦在做什么？他肯定知道那对他不好，但很糟糕，他不在乎。他不在乎。我觉得他只是有点压力。对，压力就是那样。所以他享受可乐，可能……也许有取舍，对吧？也许他享受垃圾食品和可乐，那种心灵的愉悦，可能比只吃小麦草汁和沙拉、对每件事都超级紧张要好。就像如果你需要一杯红酒来减压和冷静，那可能比你失控要好，对吧？

turns.en: Right, right. And I think that that's applicable not just in business but in probably any pursuit. And I like what you're saying about allow that one thing to be your obsession but everything else just... learn how to let things go, pick your battles.
turns.zh: 对，对。我认为这不仅适用于商业，可能适用于任何追求。我喜欢你说的，允许那一件事成为你的痴迷，但其他一切……学会放手，选择你的战斗。

transcript.sec: Non-linear Output and Lion vs Cow

turns.en: And we like to think that we view the world as linear, which is I'm going to put in eight hours of work, I'm going to get back eight hours of output. Right? It doesn't work that way. The guy running the corner grocery store is working just as hard or harder than you and me. How much output is he getting? What you do, who you do it with, how you do it, way more important than how hard you work. Right? Outputs are non-linear based on the quality of the work that you put in. The right way to work is like a lion. You don't... you and I are not like cows. We're not meant to graze all day, right? We're meant to hunt like lions. We're closer to carnivores in our omnivorous development than we are to herbivores. Um, we don't tell vegans that. Yeah, sorry. I wish all that stuff worked. I don't want to eat meat. This future generation will look back at us as worse than slavers, you know, because the holocaust we're committing with the animals. But they'll have artificial meats that taste and are healthier, is better than the real thing. So allegedly, allegedly.
turns.zh: 我们喜欢认为世界是线性的：我投入八小时工作，就得到八小时产出。对吧？但事实并非如此。经营街角杂货店的人工作和你我一样努力，甚至更努力。他得到多少产出？你做什么、和谁做、怎么做，远比工作努力程度重要。对吧？产出是非线性的，取决于你投入工作的质量。正确的工作方式像狮子。你和我不是牛。我们不是整天吃草的，对吧？我们注定像狮子一样狩猎。在杂食性发展中，我们更接近肉食动物而非草食动物。我们不告诉素食者这个。是的，抱歉。我希望那些都管用。我不想吃肉。未来一代会视我们比奴隶主更坏，因为我们正在对动物进行大屠杀。但他们会有味道更好、更健康的人造肉，比真肉好。据说是这样。

turns.en: But so as a modern knowledge worker, as an intellectual athlete, you want to function like an athlete, which means you train hard, then you sprint, then you rest, then you reassess, you get your feedback loop, then you train some more, then you sprint again, then you rest, then you reassess. This idea that you're going to have linear output just by cranking every day at the same amount of time... that's machines. You know, machines should be working nine to five. Humans are not meant to work nine to five.
turns.zh: 但作为现代知识工作者，作为智力运动员，你要像运动员一样运作：刻苦训练，然后冲刺，然后休息，然后重新评估，获得反馈循环，然后再训练，再冲刺，再休息，再评估。那种认为每天按相同时间工作就能获得线性产出的想法……那是机器。机器应该朝九晚五工作。人类不是为朝九晚五而生的。

turns.en: No, I agree wholeheartedly. But that's it for people that are working for someone, there's not really that option. So that's unfortunately the rub, right? That's kind of where my tweet storm starts, which is first of all, the first thing if you're going to make money is that you're not going to get rich renting out your time. Even lawyers and doctors who are charging three, four, five hundred an hour, they're not getting rich because their lifestyle is slowly ramping up along with their income and they're not saving enough. They just don't have that ability to retire. So the first thing you have to do is you have to own a piece of a business. You need to have equity, either as an owner, an investor, shareholder, or a brand that you're building that accrues to you, to gain your financial freedom.
turns.zh: 不，我完全同意。但对为别人工作的人来说，没有那个选择。所以不幸的是，这就是问题所在，对吧？这基本上是我那串推文的起点：首先，如果你想赚钱，你不会通过出租时间致富。即使律师和医生每小时收费三、四、五百美元，他们也不会变富，因为他们的生活方式随着收入慢慢提升，而且储蓄不够。他们就是没有退休的能力。所以你必须做的第一件事是拥有一部分生意。你需要有股权，无论是作为所有者、投资者、股东，还是建立属于你的品牌，来获得财务自由。

turns.en: Yeah, and I was really fascinated by another thing that you were bringing up about working for yourself, that you feel in the future, whether it's 50 or 100 years from now, virtually everyone is going to be working for themselves. And I believe the way you put it is that the information age is going to reverse the industrial age.
turns.zh: 是的，我对你提到的另一个关于为自己工作的事情非常着迷：你认为在未来，无论是50年还是100年后，几乎每个人都会为自己工作。我相信你的说法是信息时代将逆转工业时代。

turns.en: Yeah. If you go back to hunter-gatherer times, how we evolved, we basically work for ourselves. We communicated and cooperated within tribes, but each hunter, each gatherer stood on their own and then combined their resources with the family unit. But there was no boss hierarchy. Hierarchy hierarchy, or you're like the third middle manager down. In the farming age, we became a little bit more hierarchical as we had to run farms, but even those were still mostly family farms. It's industrial work with factories that sort of created this model of thousands of people working together on one thing and having bosses and schedules and times to show up. The reality is, if you have to... I don't care.
turns.zh: 是的。如果你回到狩猎采集时代，我们是如何进化的，我们基本上为自己工作。我们在部落内沟通合作，但每个猎人、每个采集者独立，然后与家庭单位合并资源。但没有老板等级制度。在农业时代，我们变得更有等级，因为要经营农场，但即使那些也大多是家庭农场。是工业工作，工厂创造了这种模式：成千上万的人一起做一件事，有老板、时间表和上班时间。现实是，如果你必须……我不在乎。

transcript.sec: The nature of true wealth and freedom

turns.en: How rich you are I don't care. Whether you're like a top Wall Street banker, if you have to go—if somebody has to tell you when to be at work and what to wear and how to behave—you're not a free person, you're not actually rich. So we're in this model now where we think it's all about employment and jobs, and intrinsic in that is that I have to work for somebody else. But the information age is breaking that down.
turns.zh: 你有多富有我不在乎。就算你是顶级华尔街银行家，如果有人必须告诉你什么时候上班、穿什么、怎么表现，你就不是自由的人，你并不真正富有。我们现在处于这种模式，认为一切都关乎雇佣和工作，其内在含义是我必须为别人工作。但信息时代正在打破这种模式。

transcript.sec: Coase theorem and the shrinking firm

turns.en: Ronald Coase is an economist who has the Coase theorem, a very famous theorem. But it basically just talks about why is a company the size that it is: why is a company one person instead of 10 people, instead of 100, instead of a thousand? And it has to do with the internal transaction costs versus the external transaction costs. Let's say I want to do something. Let's say I'm building a house and I need someone to come in and provide the lumber. I'm a developer, right? Do I want that to be part of my company or do I want that to be an external provider? A lot of it just depends on how hard it is to do that transaction with someone externally versus internally. If it's too hard to keep doing the contract every time externally, I'll bring that in-house. If it's easy to do externally and it's a one-off kind of thing, I'd rather keep it out of the house. Well, information technology is making it easier and easier to do these transactions externally. It's becoming much easier to communicate with people. Gig economy: I can send you small amounts of money, I can hire you through an app, I can rate you afterwards. So we're seeing an atomization of the firm. We're seeing the optimal size of the firm shrinking. It's most obvious in Silicon Valley: tons and tons of startups constantly coming up and shaving off little pieces of businesses from large companies and turning them into huge markets. So what looked like the small little vacation rental market on Craigslist is now suddenly blown up into Airbnb. One example, that's a great example.
turns.zh: 罗纳德·科斯是一位经济学家，他提出了科斯定理，一个非常著名的定理。但它基本上只是讨论为什么公司会是现在这个规模：为什么公司是一个人而不是十个人，不是一百个人，不是一千个人？这与内部交易成本和外部交易成本有关。假设我想做某事。假设我正在盖房子，需要有人来提供木材。我是开发商，对吧？我希望那是公司的一部分，还是希望那是外部供应商？这在很大程度上取决于与外部进行交易与内部进行交易的难度。如果每次外部签订合同太难，我就会把它内部化。如果外部做起来很容易，而且是一次性的事情，我宁愿把它放在外部。信息技术使这些外部交易变得越来越容易。与人沟通变得容易得多。零工经济：我可以给你汇小额款项，我可以通过应用程序雇佣你，之后我可以给你评分。所以我们看到公司正在原子化。我们看到公司的最佳规模正在缩小。这在硅谷最为明显：大量初创公司不断涌现，从大公司中剥离小块业务，并将其变成巨大的市场。所以克雷格列表上看起来很小的度假租赁市场，现在突然爆发成了爱彼迎。一个例子，这是个很好的例子。

transcript.sec: Future of work: gig economy for high-quality work

turns.en: But what I think we're going to see is, whether it's 10, 20, 50, 100 years from now, high-quality work will be available. We're not talking about driving an Uber; we're talking about super high-quality work will be available in a gig fashion, where you'll wake up in the morning, your phone will buzz, and you'll have five different jobs from people who have worked with you in the past or have been referred to. It's kind of like how Hollywood already works a little bit, with how they're organized for a project. You decide whether to take the project or not. The contract is right there on the spot. You get paid a certain amount. You get rated every day or every week. You get the money delivered. And then when you're done working, you turn it off and you go to Tahiti or wherever you want to spend the next three months. And I think the smart people have already started figuring out that the internet enables this, and they're starting to work more and more remotely on their own schedule, on their own time, on their own place, with their own friends, in their own way. And that's actually how we are the most productive.
turns.zh: 但我认为我们将看到的是，无论是10年、20年、50年还是100年后，高质量的工作将会出现。我们不是在谈论开优步；我们谈论的是超级高质量的工作将以零工形式出现，你早上醒来，手机嗡嗡响，你会有五个不同的工作，来自过去与你合作过的人或经人推荐。这有点像好莱坞已经运作的方式，他们为项目组织的方式。你决定是否接受这个项目。合同当场就在那里。你得到一定数额的报酬。你每天或每周被评分。钱到账。然后当你完成工作，你就关掉它，去塔希提或任何你想度过接下来三个月的地方。我认为聪明的人已经开始意识到互联网实现了这一点，他们开始越来越多地远程工作，按自己的日程、自己的时间、自己的地点、和自己的朋友、以自己的方式工作。而这实际上是我们最高产的方式。

transcript.sec: The information revolution enables working for oneself

turns.en: So the information revolution, by making it easier to communicate, connect, and cooperate, is allowing us to go back to working for ourselves. And that is my ultimate dream. Even when I run a company and I have employees, I always tell those people, 'Hey, I'm going to help you start your company when you're ready,' because I think that's the highest calling. Maybe not everybody will get there, but it would be fine if we were even working a ten-person company or twenty-person company is way better than working in a thousand-person company, a ten-thousand-person company. So this idea that we're all factory-like cogs in a machine who are specialized and have to do things by rote memorization or instruction, it's going to go away. And we're going to go back to being small groups of creative bands of individuals setting out to do missions. And when those missions are done, we collect our money, we get rated, and then we rest and reassess until we're ready for the next sprint.
turns.zh: 所以信息革命通过使沟通、连接和合作更容易，让我们能够回到为自己工作。这是我的终极梦想。即使我经营一家公司，有员工，我总是告诉那些人：‘嘿，当你准备好了，我会帮你创办你自己的公司。’因为我认为这是最高的召唤。也许不是每个人都能达到那个境界，但即使我们在一个十人公司或二十人公司工作，也比在千人公司、万人公司工作要好得多。所以这种我们像工厂机器里的齿轮一样专业化、必须通过死记硬背或指令做事的想法将会消失。我们将回到成为小群体、创造性的个人团队，去执行任务。当这些任务完成时，我们收钱、被评分，然后休息和重新评估，直到准备好下一次冲刺。

turns.en: Has there ever been a study done on happiness and as it regards the size of companies?
turns.zh: 有没有关于幸福感和公司规模的研究？

turns.en: Not that I'm aware. But to me it's obvious, it's just obvious: the smaller the company, the happier you're going to be. The more human your relations are, the less you have rules to operate under, the more flexible, the more creative, the more you behave like a human just because you're able to do multiple things.
turns.zh: 据我所知没有。但对我来说很明显，就是很明显：公司越小，你会越快乐。你的人际关系更人性化，你运作的规则更少，更灵活，更有创造力，你更表现得像一个人，仅仅因为你能做多件事。

turns.en: Yeah, this brings me to what is a subject that keeps getting brought up nowadays: universal basic income, with the oncoming apocalypse of automation. This is how it's being portrayed by Andrew Yang, who's running for president. I sat down and talked to him about it. It's very compelling, and he's a very smart guy, and he's an entrepreneur himself. And when he starts talking about automation and how it's going to just eliminate massive amounts of jobs and leave people stranded, what do you think? I know you're a guy who thinks about the future, and I'm going to take this popular point of view on this.
turns.zh: 是的，这让我想到一个现在经常被提起的话题：全民基本收入，伴随着即将到来的自动化末日。这是安德鲁·杨（正在竞选总统）所描绘的。我坐下来和他谈过。这很有说服力，他是个非常聪明的人，他自己也是企业家。当他开始谈论自动化将如何大规模消除就业岗位、让人们陷入困境时，你怎么看？我知道你是个思考未来的人，我要就这个流行观点发表看法。

turns.en: I don't. I think it's a non-solution to a non-problem. And I mean that in the sense that automation has been happening since the dawn of time. Electricity came along, that put a lot of people out of work, did it? Right. A lot of people carrying buckets of water and lighting lamps and all those kinds of things. And this was the concern with factories as well, apps, everything. Literally every single thing that comes, even the printing press. Absolutely. And what it does is it frees people up for new creative work. So the question is not: is automation going to eliminate jobs? There is no finite number of jobs. We're not like sitting around dividing up the same jobs that we've been around since the Stone Age. So obviously new jobs are being created, and they're usually better jobs, more creative jobs. So the question is: how quickly is this transition going to happen, and what kinds of jobs will be eliminated and what kinds of jobs will be created? It's impossible looking forward to predict what kinds of jobs will be created. If I told you 10 years ago that podcaster was going to be a job, or that playing video games is going to be a job, or commentating on video games is going to be a job, you would have laughed me out of the room. Those are nonsense jobs, but yet here we are. So society will always create new jobs. Civilization creates new jobs, but it's impossible to predict what those jobs are. So the question is: how quickly is that transition happening? Well, the reality is, even though everybody keeps talking about this automation apocalypse, we're at a record low unemployment. Explain that. Where's the transition? Donald Trump? That's hard. All I'm saying is, it's—I don't see it in the numbers. I don't see it actually happening. The question is: how quickly can you retrain people? So it's an education problem.
turns.zh: 我不这么认为。我认为这是对不存在的问题的非解决方案。我的意思是，自动化从远古时代就一直在发生。电的出现让很多人失业了，是吗？没错。很多人挑水、点灯之类的工作。工厂、应用程序、每一样东西都曾引起这种担忧。甚至印刷机也是如此。绝对是的。而它所做的就是解放人们去做新的创造性工作。所以问题不是：自动化会消除工作吗？没有有限数量的工作。我们不是像围着分石器时代以来就有的同一批工作。所以显然新工作正在被创造，而且通常是更好的工作、更有创造性的工作。所以问题是：这个转变会多快发生？哪些工作会被消除，哪些工作会被创造？向前看是不可能预测会创造什么工作的。如果我十年前告诉你播客主持人会是一份工作，或者玩电子游戏会是一份工作，或者解说电子游戏会是一份工作，你会把我笑出房间。那些是荒谬的工作，但我们现在就在这里。所以社会总会创造新工作。文明创造新工作，但不可能预测那些工作是什么。所以问题是：这个转变有多快？现实是，尽管每个人都在谈论这个自动化末日，我们却处于创纪录的低失业率。解释一下。转变在哪里？唐纳德·特朗普？这很难。我只是说，我在数字中看不到。我没有看到它实际发生。问题是：你能多快重新培训人们？所以这是一个教育问题。

turns.en: The problem with UBI—there's a couple of problems with UBI. One is you're creating a straight—you're creating a slippery slide transfer straight into socialism, right?
turns.zh: 全民基本收入的问题——全民基本收入有几个问题。一个是你在创造一个直接的——你在创造一个直接滑向社会主义的滑坡，对吧？

turns.en: The moment people can start voting themselves money combined with a democracy, right? It's just a matter of time before the bottom 51% vote themselves everything from the top 49%. And by the way, the slippery slope fallacy is not a fallacy. I know people like saying that, but they haven't thought it through. The moment you start having a direct transfer mechanism like that in a democracy, you're basically doing it with capitalism, which is the engine of economic growth. You're also forcing the entrepreneurs out or telling them not to come here. The estimate I saw for $15,000 a year basic income for everybody would be three quarters of current GDP. And of course, GDP would shrink in response as all the entrepreneurs fled, so you would essentially bankrupt the country.
turns.zh: 一旦人们可以投票给自己发钱，加上民主制度，对吧？底层51%的人把顶层49%的一切都投票给自己，只是时间问题。而且，顺便说一句，滑坡谬误并不是谬误。我知道人们喜欢这么说，但他们没有想清楚。一旦你在民主制度中开始有这样的直接转移机制，你基本上就是在用资本主义——经济增长的引擎——做这件事。你也在迫使企业家离开，或者告诉他们不要来这里。我看到一个估算，给每人每年1.5万美元的基本收入，将消耗当前GDP的四分之三。当然，随着所有企业家逃离，GDP会相应萎缩，所以你基本上会让国家破产。

turns.en: Another issue with UBI is that people who are down on their luck are not looking for handouts. It's not just about money; it's also about status, it's about meaning. The moment I start giving money to you and put you on the dole, I've lowered your status, I've made you a second-class citizen. So I have to give you meaning, and meaning comes through education and capability. I have to teach a man to fish, not basically throw your rotting leftover carcasses at him and say 'here, eat the scraps.' So it doesn't solve the meaning problem. And lastly, it's nonsense to hand $15,000 out to everybody. You want to means-test people. There's no reason to give it to you and me. So you end up back towards the welfare system, where you do have to figure out who needs it and who doesn't.
turns.zh: UBI的另一个问题是，运气不好的人并不是在寻求施舍。这不仅仅是钱的问题，还关乎地位和意义。一旦我开始给你钱，让你领救济金，我就降低了你的地位，让你成为二等公民。所以我必须给你意义，而意义来自教育和能力。我必须教人钓鱼，而不是把腐烂的残羹剩饭扔给他，说‘来，吃剩饭吧。’所以它没有解决意义问题。最后，给每个人发1.5万美元是荒谬的。你需要进行经济状况调查。没有理由给你和我发钱。所以你最终又回到了福利体系，在那里你必须弄清楚谁需要、谁不需要。

turns.en: I think the better route is that we actually establish a set of basic subsistence services that you have to have, and we provide those in abundance through technology-based automation. So get basic housing, get basic food, get basic transportation, get high-speed internet access, get a phone in your pocket. Those are the kinds of things you want to give people.
turns.zh: 我认为更好的途径是，我们实际上建立一套人们必需的基本生存服务，并通过基于技术的自动化大量提供这些服务。所以提供基本住房、基本食物、基本交通、高速互联网接入、口袋里的手机。这些是你想给人们的东西。

turns.en: And finally, in terms of the rate of automation, I think we can educate people very quickly. One of the myths that we have today is that adults can't be re-educated. We view education as this thing where you go to school, you come out when you're out of college, and you're done, no more education. Well, that's wrong. You have all these great online boot camps and coding schools coming up, ones that will even pay you to go there now. You can educate people en masse, and you can educate them into creative professions.
turns.zh: 最后，关于自动化的速度，我认为我们可以非常快速地教育人们。今天我们有一个误区，认为成年人无法再教育。我们把教育看作是你上学、大学毕业就结束了，不再有教育。但这是错误的。现在有所有这些很棒的在线训练营和编程学校，有些甚至会付钱让你去学习。你可以大规模地教育人们，并将他们教育成创造性职业。

turns.en: People who are talking about AI automating programming have never really written serious code. Coding is thinking; it's automatic structure thinking. An AI that can program as well or better than humans is an AI that just took over the world. That's endgame, that's the end of the human species. And I can give you arguments why I don't think that's coming either.
turns.zh: 那些谈论AI自动化编程的人从未真正写过严肃的代码。编程就是思考；它是自动化的结构性思考。一个能像人类一样好或更好编程的AI，就是一个刚刚接管了世界的AI。那是终局，那是人类物种的终结。我也可以给你理由，为什么我认为这也不会发生。

turns.en: People who are thinking—and I know I take the opposite side from some very famous people in this debate—but we are nowhere near close to general AI, not in our lifetimes. You don't have to worry about it even in our lifetimes, really. It's so overblown. It's a combination of Cassandra complex—you know, it's fun to talk about the end of the world—combined with a god complex, like people who have lost religion so they're looking for meaning in some kind of end of history.
turns.zh: 那些认为——我知道我在这个辩论中与一些非常著名的人持相反立场——但我们离通用AI还差得很远，有生之年都不会。你真的不必担心，甚至在我们有生之年。这被过分夸大了。这是卡珊德拉情结——你知道，谈论世界末日很有趣——加上上帝情结，就像那些失去宗教信仰的人，在某种历史终结中寻找意义。

turns.en: The reason why I don't think AI is coming anytime soon is because a lot of the advances in so-called AI today are what we call narrow AI. They're really pattern recognition, machine learning to figure out like 'what is that object on the screen?' or 'how do you find this signal in all that noise?' There's nothing approaching what we call creative thinking to actually model general intelligence. You run into all kinds of problems. First, we don't know how the brain works at all. Number two, we've never even modeled a paramecium or an amoeba, let alone a human brain. Number three, there's this assumption that all of the computation is going at the cellular level, at the neuron level, whereas nature is very parsimonious; it uses everything at its disposal. There's a lot of machinery inside the cell that is doing calculations, that is intelligent, that isn't accounted for. And the best estimates are it would take 50 years of Moore's law before we can simulate what's going on inside a cell near perfectly, and probably 100 years before we can build a brain that can simulate inside the cells. So putting it at saying 'I'm just going to model neurons on or off' and then use that to build a human brain is overly simplistic.
turns.zh: 我认为AI不会很快到来的原因是，今天所谓的AI的许多进步都是我们所说的狭义AI。它们实际上是模式识别、机器学习，用来弄清楚‘屏幕上的那个物体是什么？’或者‘如何在所有噪声中找到这个信号？’没有任何接近我们所说的创造性思维来真正模拟通用智能。你会遇到各种问题。首先，我们根本不知道大脑是如何工作的。第二，我们甚至从未模拟过草履虫或变形虫，更不用说人脑了。第三，有一种假设认为所有的计算都发生在细胞层面、神经元层面，而自然是非常节俭的；它利用一切可用的东西。细胞内有很多机器在进行计算，是智能的，但没有被考虑进去。最好的估计是，需要50年的摩尔定律才能近乎完美地模拟细胞内发生的事情，大概需要100年才能构建一个能够模拟细胞内的大脑。所以，说‘我只模拟神经元的开或关’，然后用它来构建人脑，是过于简单化的。

turns.en: Furthermore, I would posit there's no such thing as general intelligence. Every intelligence is contextual within the context of the environment that it's in. So you have to evolve an environment around it. So I think a lot of people who are peddling general AI, the burden of proof is on them. I haven't seen anything that would lead me to indicate we're approaching general AI. Instead, we're solving deterministic, closed-set, finite problems using large amounts of data. But it's not sexy to talk about that.
turns.zh: 此外，我认为不存在所谓的通用智能。每种智能都是在其所处环境的背景下具有情境性的。所以你必须围绕它进化出一个环境。所以我认为很多兜售通用AI的人，举证责任在他们身上。我没有看到任何东西让我觉得我们正在接近通用AI。相反，我们是在用大量数据解决确定性的、封闭的、有限的问题。但谈论这个并不性感。

turns.en: If you're talking about mirroring the actual abilities of cells, or are you talking about recreating the actual mechanism? What is going on inside cells and biological organisms?
turns.zh: 你是在谈论镜像细胞的实际能力，还是在谈论重建实际机制？细胞内和生物有机体中发生了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, we just don't know how intelligence works. We literally have no idea. So most of the AI approaches basically say we're going to try and model how the brain works, but the model at the neuron level, which is saying 'this neuron is on, that neuron is off, combining their signal.' But I'm saying the neuron is a cell. Inside the cell, there's all this machinery going on that's operating the neuron that is also part of the intelligence apparatus. You can't just ignore that and abstract that out. You have to model it down to the inside-the-cell level. It's also a part of the biological organism itself.
turns.zh: 是的，我们就是不知道智能是如何工作的。我们完全不知道。所以大多数AI方法基本上是说，我们将尝试模拟大脑的工作方式，但模型是在神经元层面，即‘这个神经元开启，那个神经元关闭，组合它们的信号。’但我说神经元是一个细胞。在细胞内部，有所有这些机器在运行神经元，这也是智能装置的一部分。你不能只是忽略它并抽象掉它。你必须建模到细胞内层面。它也是生物有机体本身的一部分。

turns.en: Exactly, and it has all these needs that the biological organism has to have food and rest, and there's a balance going on. But when you eliminate all that, when there is none of that and it's just calculations, and we get to a point where it's just this thing that we've created—whether you call it a computer, whether it doesn't have to be a moving thing even, but a thing that you've created that stores virtually all the information that's available in the world, stores all the patterns of all the thinking of all the great people that have ever lived, all the writers, all the people that have ever published anything, all the people that have ever spoken any words, stores all of their points, all their counterpoints, all their contradictions, applies logic and reason and some sort of sense of the future, and starts improving upon these patterns, and then starts acting on its own based on the information that it's been provided with—
turns.zh: 正是，它还有所有这些需求，生物有机体必须有食物和休息，并且存在一种平衡。但当你消除所有这些，当没有这些，只有计算，我们到达一个点，它只是我们创造的这个东西——无论你叫它计算机，甚至它不必是一个移动的东西，而是你创造的一个东西，它存储了世界上几乎所有可用的信息，存储了所有曾经活着的伟人的所有思维模式，所有作家，所有发表过任何东西的人，所有说过任何话的人，存储了他们所有的观点、所有的反驳、所有的矛盾，应用逻辑、理性和某种未来感，并开始改进这些模式，然后根据提供给它的信息开始自主行动——

turns.en: Well, first you would have to actually simulate a structure the human brain that can hold all that information. You're basically talking about tens of thousands of brains worth of information, right? We can't even build one brain.
turns.zh: 嗯，首先你必须实际模拟一个能够容纳所有这些信息的人脑结构。你基本上是在谈论相当于数万个大脑的信息量，对吧？我们连一个大脑都造不出来。

transcript.sec: On the limits of AI and the nature of intelligence

turns.en: A decade or two or three? Well, in terms of an actual physical brain, yes. But what about something that recreates the abilities of a brain? Like I said, nature is parsimonious. We've got this three-pound wetware object that can hold all this data. Nature has been very efficient in evolving how we get there. I just don't think computers are anywhere close to that. They can hold that amount of data with that complexity, with the holographic structure of the brain where it can recall in many different ways. And I don't think you can evolve a creature to be intelligent outside of the boundaries of feedback in a real medium. If you raised a human being in a concrete cell with no input from the outside, they wouldn't have any feedback from the real world; they wouldn't evolve properly. So I think just dumping information into a thing isn't enough. It has to have an environment to operate in, to get feedback from. It needs to have context.
turns.zh: 十年、二十年还是三十年？就实际的大脑而言，是的。但如果是能重现大脑能力的东西呢？就像我说的，自然是节俭的。我们拥有这个三磅重的湿件，能容纳所有这些数据。自然在进化我们如何达到这一点上非常高效。我只是认为计算机远未达到那种程度。它们无法以那种复杂性、以大脑的全息结构来存储那么多数据，大脑可以通过多种方式回忆。而且我认为，你无法在真实介质的反馈边界之外进化出一个智能生物。如果你在一个没有外界输入的混凝土牢房里养大一个人，他们不会有来自真实世界的反馈；他们无法正常进化。所以我认为仅仅把信息塞进一个东西是不够的。它必须有一个环境来运作，从中获得反馈。它需要上下文。

turns.en: But isn't that biological? I mean, what if you have all the information that people have accumulated and the lessons that people have learned, and you program that into the computer? Like, if we can take a computer that can beat someone at chess, the real question was: can we make some sort of artificial intelligence that could beat someone at Go, which is far more complex than chess? They figured out how to do that too, and that was a giant shock, right?
turns.zh: 但这不是生物性的吗？我的意思是，如果你把人类积累的所有信息和学到的教训都编程进计算机呢？比如，如果我们有一台能打败人类的计算机，真正的问题是：我们能制造某种人工智能来打败围棋选手吗？围棋比国际象棋复杂得多。他们也做到了，那是一个巨大的震惊，对吧？

turns.en: These are still man-made, very closed, bounded games. They're not on the road to the unbounded game of life. They are completely artificial.
turns.zh: 这些仍然是人为的、非常封闭、有边界的游戏。它们不在通往无限生活游戏的道路上。它们完全是人工的。

turns.en: But didn't that give you a little bit of a pause? A little bit? Go is not Go or League of Legends or Fortnite. They're not completely deterministic, right? But they're still very artificial, very bounded games. Being good at Go doesn't mean that you can then suddenly figure out how to write great poetry, right? The creativity, for sure, is something that's... creativity is the last frontier.
turns.zh: 但这难道没有让你稍微停顿一下吗？一点点？围棋不是围棋或英雄联盟或堡垒之夜。它们不是完全确定性的，对吧？但它们仍然是非常人工、非常有边界的游戏。擅长围棋并不意味着你就能突然写出伟大的诗歌，对吧？创造力，当然，是……创造力是最后的边疆。

turns.en: So I do believe that automation, over a long enough period of time, will replace every non-creative job, or every non-creative work. But that's great news. That means that all of our basic needs are taken care of, and what remains for us is to be creative, which is really what every human wants.
turns.zh: 所以我确实相信，在足够长的时间内，自动化将取代每一个非创造性的工作，或每一个非创造性的劳动。但这是个好消息。这意味着我们所有的基本需求都得到了满足，留给我们的就是去创造，而这正是每个人都想要的。

turns.en: Yeah, what are you doing right now? This is a creative job.
turns.zh: 是啊，你现在在做什么？这是一份创造性的工作。

transcript.sec: Universal basic income and the meaning of life

turns.en: Sure. That brings us back to the idea of meaning and universal basic income. I think the idea of giving someone $15,000 a year doesn't necessarily cause... what everyone would worry about is people being on the dole. You would have a bunch of listless people out there with no meaning in life. But the idea is that $15,000 a year—and I'm not necessarily sure I agree with this, I'm not even endorsing this—but that $15,000 a year would just provide you with the necessities to get by in life. It would give you food, it would give you shelter.
turns.zh: 当然。这让我们回到了意义和全民基本收入的想法。我认为每年给某人1.5万美元不一定会导致……每个人担心的是人们靠救济金生活。你会有一群无精打采的人，生活没有意义。但想法是每年1.5万美元——我不一定同意这个，我甚至不赞同——但每年1.5万美元只会为你提供生活必需品。它会给你食物，给你住所。

turns.en: Well, it's not going to stop at 15, because the moment people are like... I mean, like 15, then people would demand more. Bernie Sanders would be on this: 16, 17, 45 thousand dollars. Yeah, these companies are too big. It doesn't stop. It just goes all the way to bankruptcy. The concern is the slide to socialism. It's obvious. Yeah, I mean, heck, if I was not working and I was getting my $15 a year, I would happily vote for the guy who would give me 20 or 25. It's just common sense.
turns.zh: 嗯，它不会停在1.5万，因为一旦人们……我是说，1.5万，然后人们就会要求更多。伯尼·桑德斯会这样：1.6万、1.7万、4.5万美元。是的，这些公司太大了。它不会停止。它会一路走向破产。担忧是滑向社会主义。这很明显。是的，我的意思是，见鬼，如果我不工作，每年拿1.5万美元，我会很高兴投票给那个给我2万或2.5万的人。这只是常识。

transcript.sec: Critique of capitalism and the case for free markets

turns.en: What do you say to the people that don't believe that there is such a thing as ethical or compassionate capitalism? And there are many people today that are espousing Marxism and espousing some sort of a socialist society. They believe that capitalism has screwed people over and eliminated the middle class. And there are absolute problems with capitalism. I think monopolies are a problem. I think crony capitalism is a problem, with the government getting in bed with them and sort of forcing things. I think the bankers have really raped society and the rest of us are suffering for it. Yeah, they've essentially taken huge risks where they privatize the gains and they socialize the losses. So when it fails, they basically get bailed out and bankrupt everybody else. So capitalism has gotten a really bad name.
turns.zh: 你对那些不相信存在道德或富有同情心的资本主义的人怎么说？今天有很多人在宣扬马克思主义，宣扬某种社会主义社会。他们相信资本主义已经搞砸了人们，消灭了中产阶级。资本主义确实存在绝对的问题。我认为垄断是个问题。我认为裙带资本主义是个问题，政府与它们勾结，强制推行一些事情。我认为银行家真的强奸了社会，我们其余的人都在为此受苦。是的，他们基本上承担了巨大的风险，将收益私有化，将损失社会化。所以当它失败时，他们基本上得到救助，让其他所有人破产。所以资本主义的名声真的很臭。

turns.en: Let's talk about its free exchange. Free markets. Free markets and free exchange are intrinsic to humans. From when the first person started a fire and somebody came along with a deer and said, 'Hey, if I cook my deer on your fire, I'll share some of it with you,' right? So specialization, labor, we trade. That's built into the human species. Basic math comes from accounting, keeping track of debts and credits, and so on. We need to be able to engage in free trade. The correct criticism of capitalism is when it does not provide equal opportunity. And so we should always strive to provide equal opportunity. But people confuse that with equal outcome. When you have equal outcome, that can only be enforced through violence, because different people, free people, make different choices. And when they make different choices, they have different outcomes. If you don't let them suffer the consequences of bad choices or reap the rewards from good choices, then you are forcibly redistributing through violence. It's interesting that there are no working socialist examples that exist without violence. You basically need someone to show up with a gun and say, 'Okay, you're not allowed to do that. You hand this over to that person.'
turns.zh: 让我们谈谈它的自由交换。自由市场。自由市场和自由交换是人类固有的。从第一个人生火，另一个人带着一只鹿来说，‘嘿，如果我在你的火上烤我的鹿，我会分一些给你，’对吧？所以专业化、劳动、我们交易。这植根于人类物种。基础数学来自会计，记录债务和信贷等等。我们需要能够进行自由贸易。对资本主义的正确批评是当它不提供平等机会时。所以我们应该始终努力提供平等机会。但人们将其与平等结果混淆。当你拥有平等结果时，那只能通过暴力来强制执行，因为不同的人，自由的人，做出不同的选择。当他们做出不同的选择时，他们会有不同的结果。如果你不让他们承受坏选择的后果或收获好选择的回报，那么你就是在通过暴力强制重新分配。有趣的是，没有不通过暴力而存在的社会主义例子。你基本上需要有人拿着枪出现，说，‘好吧，你不允许那样做。你把这个交给那个人。’

turns.en: One of the reasons why this podcast exists is because I believe everybody can be wealthy. Everybody. It's not a zero-sum game. It is a positive-sum game. You create something brand new, you exchange it with me for something brand new I've created. This creates higher utility for both of us. The sum of the value created is positive. It's not like status, where it's like you're higher up, I'm lower down. You're president, so I must be vice president. You're a plus one, I'm a minus one, and it has to cancel to zero. We should all be for playing positive-sum ethical games. The problem is because of these looters who have ruined capitalism's name, that then you get socialists coming in and saying, 'Burn the whole system down.' Right? You burn the whole system down, we end up like Venezuela or the former Soviet Union. You know, you don't want to be a failed socialist state with emaciated teens hunting cats in the streets to eat. That's literally what happens in some of these places. So I think it's very important not to destroy the engine of progress that brought us here.
turns.zh: 这个播客存在的原因之一是我相信每个人都可以变得富有。每个人。这不是一个零和游戏。这是一个正和游戏。你创造全新的东西，你用它交换我创造的全新东西。这对我们双方都创造了更高的效用。创造的价值总和是正的。不像地位，你更高，我更低。你是总统，所以我必须是副总统。你是加一，我是减一，必须抵消为零。我们都应该支持玩正和的道德游戏。问题是因为这些掠夺者毁了资本主义的名声，然后社会主义者进来，说‘烧掉整个系统。’对吧？你烧掉整个系统，我们最终会像委内瑞拉或前苏联一样。你知道，你不想成为一个失败的社会主义国家，瘦弱的青少年在街上猎猫吃。这确实发生在一些地方。所以我认为非常重要的是不要摧毁带我们走到这里的进步引擎。

turns.en: Yeah, the idea that socialism just hasn't worked yet, that it needs to... we just need to do it right. If we do it right, we can... if you ever... 100 million dead and yeah, yeah. Well, let's keep trying all over the world. Yeah, and every single time it's been implemented... Have you ever had a conversation with someone who's a socialist? Many times, some of my better friends are socialists. Really? We really get...
turns.zh: 是的，认为社会主义还没有成功，它需要……我们只需要做对。如果我们做对了，我们可以……如果你曾经……1亿人死亡，是的，是的。好吧，让我们继续在世界各地尝试。是的，每次它被实施……你有没有和社会主义者交谈过？很多时候，我的一些好朋友是社会主义者。真的吗？我们真的……

turns.en: Yeah, and what is there? I mean, does anyone have a compelling perspective at all? I think really socialism comes from the heart, right? We all want to be socialist. Capitalism comes from the head because there are always cheaters in any system, and there are incentives in any system. So when you're young, if you're not a socialist, you have no heart; when you're older, if you're not a capitalist, you have no head. You haven't thought it through. So I understand where it comes from. I always liked Nassim Taleb's framing on this, where he said: with my family, I'm a communist; with my close friends, I'm a socialist; at my state-level politics, I'm a Democrat; at higher levels, I'm a Republican; and at the federal level, I'm a libertarian. So basically, the larger the group of people you have massed together who have different interests, the less trust there is, the more cheating there is, the better the incentives have to be aligned, the better the system has to work, the more you go towards capitalism. The smaller the group you're in—you're in a kibbutz, you're in your commune, you're in your house, you're in your tribe—by all means, be a socialist. With my aunts, with my brother, with my cousins, with my uncles, with my mom, with your family, I'm a socialist. That's the right way to live a loving, happy, integrated life. But when you're dealing with strangers, I mean, you want to be a real socialist? Great, open all your doors and windows tomorrow, please, everybody come take what you want. See how that works out.
turns.zh: 是啊，那有什么呢？我是说，有人有令人信服的观点吗？我认为社会主义确实来自内心，对吧？我们都想成为社会主义者。资本主义来自头脑，因为任何系统中总有作弊者，任何系统中都有激励。所以当你年轻时，如果你不是社会主义者，你就没有心；当你年长时，如果你不是资本主义者，你就没有头脑。你没有想清楚。所以我理解它从何而来。我一直喜欢纳西姆·塔勒布对此的框架，他说：和我的家人在一起，我是共产主义者；和我的密友在一起，我是社会主义者；在我的州级政治中，我是民主党人；在更高层面，我是共和党人；在联邦层面，我是自由意志主义者。所以基本上，你聚集的人群越大，他们的利益越不同，信任就越少，作弊就越多，激励就越需要对齐，系统就越需要运作良好，你就越走向资本主义。你所在的群体越小——你在基布兹，你在公社，你在家里，你在部落里——完全可以做个社会主义者。和我的姑姑、兄弟、堂表亲、叔叔、妈妈、家人在一起，我是社会主义者。那是过一种有爱、快乐、完整生活的正确方式。但当你和陌生人打交道时，我是说，你想成为一个真正的社会主义者？很好，明天打开你所有的门窗，请吧，每个人都来拿他们想要的。看看那会怎样。

turns.en: This idea of income inequality always strikes me as a very deceptive term. Income inequality? Well, let's flip it around: it comes from outcome inequality. And outcome inequality is there because you made different choices. Now, again, going back, if it was because you didn't have the same opportunities, that's a problem. Yes, society should always try to give people equal opportunities. So, for example, instead of basic income, what if we had a retraining program built into our basic social fabric which said that every four years or every six years or whatever it is—maybe it's every ten—you can take one year out, and we'll pay for you to go retrain completely, and you can go into any profession you like that has some earning power and output, hopefully a creative, long-term profession, and you can re-educate yourself. That would be much better for society on all levels than basically just saying now you're going to be on the dole for the rest of your life. You'd have to lead that horse to water and then make them drink; it requires people to put in some effort. You know, we can't all just sit around. It's just not... Well, that's my perspective on income inequality. There's always effort inequality and thought inequality. There are just some people that are obsessed, and if those people become successful, it doesn't mean they stole from you; it just means that they put in the amount of energy and effort that it required to reach where they're at. And there's a lot of virtue signaling that goes on now where people say, 'Well, it's because you're privileged.' Well, you know what the greatest privilege is? You're alive. 85% of humanity is dead. So how privileged are you then? You're living in the first world, and you have four limbs, etc. So you can take that argument all the way; it's kind of a nonsense discussion.
turns.zh: 收入不平等这个概念总是让我觉得是个很有欺骗性的术语。收入不平等？好吧，我们反过来看：它来自结果不平等。结果不平等之所以存在，是因为你做出了不同的选择。现在，再说回来，如果是因为你没有同样的机会，那是个问题。是的，社会应该始终努力给人们平等的机会。所以，例如，与其搞基本收入，不如在我们的基本社会结构中内置一个再培训计划，规定每四年或每六年——也许是每十年——你可以休一年假，我们出钱让你完全重新培训，你可以进入任何你喜欢的、有赚钱能力和产出的职业，希望是创造性的、长期的职业，你可以重新教育自己。那对社会各个层面都会好得多，而不是基本上就说你余生都要靠救济金生活。你得把马牵到水边，然后让它喝水；这需要人们付出一些努力。你知道，我们不能都无所事事。那只是……嗯，这就是我对收入不平等的看法。总是存在努力不平等和思想不平等。有些人就是痴迷，如果那些人成功了，并不意味着他们偷了你的东西；只是意味着他们付出了达到那个位置所需的精力和努力。现在有很多道德信号，人们说，‘嗯，那是因为你有特权。’好吧，你知道最大的特权是什么吗？你还活着。85%的人类已经死了。那你有多特权呢？你生活在第一世界，你有四肢等等。所以你可以把这个论点一路推下去；这有点像无意义的讨论。

turns.en: What's a very weird progressive argument, and as it pertains to race, is always a weird one, right? Because white privilege to me—although you could look at what they're saying on paper, like yes, I'm sure there are more black people that are harassed by the police, I'm sure there are more black people who are treated suspiciously by shop owners and the like. But the problem isn't the people who aren't treated poorly; the problem is the people who treat the people poorly. The problem is racism. The problem is not people that didn't ask to be born white or whatever they are and they don't get harassed. So this idea of white privilege or male privilege or whatever it is—that's not the problem. You're just looking at someone who's not a victim of this particular problem that you're highlighting, but you're not looking at the perpetrators of the problem. You're making people perpetrators by simply existing and having less melanin in their skin or having their ancestors come from... Actually, it's a sneaky way of being racist. And then they say you can't be racist, it's not racist because you're white. That's right, that is this hilarious: 'If you can't be racist against white people'—that one, that's a variation of the whole 'stop struggling while I'm hitting you' argument. It's just so silly. You've completely changed what racism means. But what's hilarious is mostly the people who are yelling 'racist' are not the minorities. When I look on my Twitter, my social media, or on my news, it's white-on-white violence, virtue signaling. Yeah, it's white-on-white violence. What's mostly going on is elitist whites, blue-state whites, college-educated whites beating up on high-school-educated whites, blue-collar. It's a white-collar versus blue-collar war that's going on, and the rest of us are just kind of watching like, 'Oh, that's kind of interesting.'
turns.zh: 一个非常奇怪的进步主义论点，而且涉及到种族问题时，总是很奇怪，对吧？因为白人特权对我来说——虽然你可以从纸面上看他们说的，比如是的，我确信有更多黑人被警察骚扰，我确信有更多黑人被店主等怀疑对待。但问题不是那些没有被恶劣对待的人；问题是那些恶劣对待别人的人。问题是种族主义。问题不是那些没有要求生为白人或其他什么、也没有被骚扰的人。所以这种白人特权或男性特权之类的概念——那不是问题。你只是看着一个不是你强调的这个特定问题的受害者的人，但你没有看问题的肇事者。你仅仅因为别人存在、皮肤中黑色素较少或祖先来自……就让他们成为肇事者。实际上，这是一种狡猾的种族主义方式。然后他们说你不能是种族主义者，因为你是白人所以不是种族主义。没错，这很滑稽：‘你不能对白人是种族主义者’——那个，那是整个‘我打你时你别挣扎’论点的变种。太愚蠢了。你完全改变了种族主义的含义。但滑稽的是，大喊‘种族主义者’的人大多不是少数族裔。当我看我的推特、社交媒体或新闻时，那是白人对白人的暴力，道德信号。是的，那是白人对白人的暴力。主要发生的是精英白人、蓝州白人、受过大学教育的白人在打击受过高中教育的白人、蓝领。这是一场白领对蓝领的战争，而我们其他人只是看着，觉得‘哦，那挺有意思的。’

turns.en: Well, it's also a side effect of the ability to broadcast, right? Like everyone with a Twitter handle has the ability to broadcast, everyone with a Facebook page has the ability to pontificate and have these long, rambling, huge statements that people put out. When you read them, it's like, 'How much time did you put into this? What the f***? Do you put that much time into your kids or your job or your life or your future or planning for your...' You know, how much do you work out a day? I mean, are you just... Some people's Facebook posts, I'm like, 'This is a preposterous amount of effort that you put into saying virtually nothing.'
turns.zh: 嗯，这也是广播能力的一个副作用，对吧？就像每个有推特账号的人都有广播能力，每个有脸书页面的人都有说教的能力，发表这些长篇大论、杂乱无章的巨大声明。当你读它们时，你会想，‘你花了多少时间在这上面？搞什么？你花那么多时间在你的孩子、工作、生活、未来或计划上吗？’你知道，你每天锻炼多少？我是说，你是不是只是……有些人的脸书帖子，我就想，‘你花了荒谬的精力说了几乎什么都没有。’

turns.en: Let's see, humans are being creative. And AI can do that well.
turns.zh: 让我们看看，人类很有创意。而人工智能也能做得很好。

turns.en: That's true, it is creative. It's creative in a very odd way, right? Because it's creative in that they're trying to elicit a response from people, and they're trying to raise their social value or raise their position on the social totem pole. It's signaling, and it's easy signaling because it's the kind of thing that everybody has to agree with you on because nobody wants to be seen as a horrible person. And it's very hard to make the nuanced arguments against it than it is to just kind of go along.
turns.zh: 没错，这很有创意。以一种非常奇怪的方式有创意，对吧？因为它的创意在于他们试图引起人们的反应，试图提高他们的社会价值或在社会图腾柱上的地位。这是信号传递，而且是容易的信号传递，因为这是每个人都必须同意你的那种事，因为没人想被视为一个可怕的人。而且做出细微差别的反驳比随大流要难得多。

turns.en: Well, it's also some of it is so cliché that it seems like... I know one guy who poses as a woman on Twitter, but he does it just obviously. Yeah, what is this, the name Tatiana McGregor? Yes, yeah, yeah. Used to be Godfrey Elwick? Yes. Oh, is that the same guy? I think so. Yeah, that's hilarious. I did not... They killed his account. They killed his account for sure, for pretending to be transracial. That's right, they did. Yeah, he basically says...
turns.zh: 嗯，其中一些也如此陈词滥调，以至于看起来……我知道有个人在推特上假扮女人，但他做得太明显了。是的，叫什么名字，塔蒂亚娜·麦格雷戈？对，对，对。以前是戈弗雷·埃尔威克？是的。哦，是同一个人吗？我想是的。是啊，太搞笑了。我不知道……他们封了他的账号。他们肯定封了他的账号，因为假装是跨种族者。没错，他们封了。是啊，他基本上说……

transcript.sec: On Outrage Culture and Social Media

turns.en: All the crazy stuff that people on the left say, but he says the craziest version, and it kind of shows how it's okay. I saw a tweet from someone recently that said it's not okay to be white, and some people agreed. But it's so close to what they say that it's the most artful form of subtle parody. If you replace the word 'white' with 'black' or 'Asian' in half of these things, it's the same.
turns.zh: 左派说的那些疯狂言论，但他说的版本最极端，这反而让人觉得没什么大不了的。我最近看到一条推文说‘身为白人不可以’，居然还有人赞同。但这和他们的说法太接近了，简直是最巧妙的微妙讽刺。如果把一半内容里的‘白人’换成‘黑人’或‘亚裔’，意思完全一样。

turns.en: Yeah, it's a strange time. There's a saying: if you want to see who rules over you, see who you're not allowed to criticize. That's so true.
turns.zh: 是啊，这是个奇怪的时代。有句话说：想知道谁在统治你，看看你不能批评谁。说得太对了。

turns.en: Yeah, that's so true. I wonder where this is going. This newfound ability to broadcast—whether through YouTube, Twitter, or whatever—lets you spread your message to so many people with very little understanding. Overall, I think it's a great thing. It means any human can broadcast to any other human at any time. For example, if a totalitarian dictator came to power and fascists were beating up old women, that would get broadcast instantly, causing outrage and rallying support. So it helps bring attention to anyone's plight. But right now, we're going through a phase where we have this power to assemble mobs, and people don't know how to handle it. It becomes very easy to set up a mob and attack someone, taking everything out of context. Even this conversation—I'm sure people will take snippets and try to get someone outraged. Society has to get over this idea of outrage. Outraged people are the stupidest on social media; I block them instantly. It's low-level thinking. They're the foot soldiers of the mob. Eventually, society must understand that these are snippets taken out of context, doctored clips, just someone trying to get outraged. There will also be anti-mob tactics. For instance, if I go to someone's Twitter feed and it's full of political ranting and conspiracy theories, do I want to work with them? Do I want to be friends? Their mind is cluttered with junk. I don't blame them—the human brain isn't designed to absorb 24/7 breaking news emergencies injected with clickbait headlines. If you pay attention, even if well-meaning and sound of mind, it will drive you insane. It's like 'A Clockwork Orange' where his eyes are forced open to watch the news. That's happening now because these platforms are addictive. Twitter, Facebook, Instagram are weaponized. Social statisticians, scientists, researchers in lab coats—the best minds of our generation—are figuring out how to addict you to the news. If you fall for it, your brain gets destroyed. This is the modern struggle.
turns.zh: 是啊，说得太对了。我真不知道这会走向何方。这种新获得的广播能力——无论是通过YouTube、推特还是其他方式——让你能把信息传播给那么多人，而大多数人却缺乏理解。总的来说，我认为这是件好事。这意味着任何人都能随时向地球上的任何人广播。比如，如果某个极权独裁者上台，法西斯分子殴打老妇人，那会被立刻传播出去，引发愤怒和声援。所以这有助于关注任何人的困境。但现在我们正处于一个阶段，我们有了召集暴民的力量，却不知道如何应对。很容易就能组织暴民攻击某人，断章取义。就连这场对话——我肯定有人会截取片段试图激怒别人。社会必须克服这种愤怒情绪。愤怒的人是社交媒体上最愚蠢的，我直接屏蔽他们。这是低层次思维。他们是暴民的步兵。最终社会必须明白，这些都是断章取义的片段、篡改过的视频、只是有人想找茬愤怒。还会出现反暴民策略。比如，如果我去某人的推特页面，全是政治咆哮和阴谋论，我还想和他共事吗？想和他做朋友吗？他的脑子里塞满了垃圾。我不怪他们——人脑不是设计来24小时吸收突发新闻、用标题党注入紧急事件的。如果你关注这些，即使你心地善良、心智健全，最终也会发疯。就像《发条橙》里他被强行撑开眼睛看新闻。现在就是这样，因为这些平台让人上瘾。推特、脸书、Instagram都被武器化了。社会统计学家、科学家、研究人员、穿白大褂的人——我们这一代最聪明的人——都在研究如何让你对新闻上瘾。如果你上钩了，你的大脑就会被摧毁。这就是现代的斗争。

turns.en: The ancient struggle was tribal—you had your tribe of friends and family, your religion, your country, your loyalty, your nationality. At least you had meaning and support. But you struggled against other tribes. Modern life is so free; everything has become atomized. You stand alone. You live in your apartment alone, your parents don't live nearby, your friends don't live nearby. You don't have tribal meaning. You don't believe in religion or country anymore. It's fine—you have a lot of freedom, it's fantastic. But now when they come to attack you, you're alone and can't resist. How do they attack you? It's all well-meaning. I don't fault capitalism; I love capitalism. But look at how it happens. Social media has massaged all the mechanisms to addict you like a Skinner's pigeon or a rat that just clicks and clicks. Can't put the phone down. Food: they've taken sugar and weaponized it, put it in forms you can't resist eating. Drugs: they've taken pharmaceuticals and plants, synthesized and grown them so you get addicted. Porn: if you're a young male, it saps your libido and you don't go out in real life because you have incredibly stimulating stuff. Video games: another way to addict people. There are entire factories of people working to addict you to these things, and you stand alone. The modern struggle as an individual is learning how to resist these things in the first place, drawing your own boundaries. There's no one there to help you. That's terrifying. It's a new road that needs to be navigated by young people with no map, no guidebook. Our generation is the transition generation. I think our kids will handle it better because they'll grow up with it. But I hope so, because you're seeing ridiculous behavior from people today that's so common.
turns.zh: 古代的挣扎是部落式的——你有你的亲友部落、你的宗教、你的国家、你的忠诚、你的国籍。至少你有意义和支持。但你要与其他部落斗争。现代生活如此自由；一切都原子化了。你独自一人。你独自住在公寓里，父母不在附近，朋友也不在附近。你没有部落意义。你不再相信宗教或国家。这很好——你有很多自由，太棒了。但现在当他们来攻击你时，你独自一人，无法抵抗。他们怎么攻击你？都是出于好意。我不责怪资本主义；我热爱资本主义。但看看它是怎么发生的。社交媒体精心设计了所有机制让你上瘾，就像斯金纳的鸽子或老鼠，只会不停地点击。放不下手机。食物：他们把糖武器化，做成你无法抗拒的形式。毒品：他们把药物和植物合成、培育，让你上瘾。色情：如果你是年轻男性，它会消耗你的性欲，你不再出门社交，因为你有极度刺激的内容。电子游戏：另一种让人上瘾的方式。有整座工厂的人致力于让你对这些东西上瘾，而你独自一人。作为个体，现代的斗争首先是学会如何抵制这些东西，划定自己的界限。没有人帮你。这很可怕。这是一条新路，年轻人没有地图、没有指南需要摸索。我们这一代是过渡的一代。我想我们的孩子会处理得更好，因为他们会从小接触。但我希望如此，因为今天人们的行为荒谬至极，而且非常普遍。

turns.en: I don't know if you've been paying attention, but there was a guy who made a video—it turns out it wasn't even him who made it, at least that's what he said—but it was a video where he doctored Nancy Pelosi talking and made it look like she was drunk. A bunch of people retweeted it like 'Oh my god, look, she's drunk.' An online publication tracked him down and doxed him. Turned out he's just a day laborer, an African-American Trump fan, and thought it would be funny. According to him, he just put it on his Facebook page. What's even more disturbing is that Facebook gave up his information to that website. For what? Because he made something funny that made someone seem drunk? There are a million of those about me. I think Facebook, Twitter, and other social media platforms are committing slow-motion suicide through these kinds of activities. That was a stunning one, though—that they would give up this guy who's a laborer.
turns.zh: 我不知道你有没有注意，有个人做了个视频——结果发现甚至不是他做的，至少他是这么说的——但视频里他篡改了南希·佩洛西的讲话，让她看起来像喝醉了。很多人转发说‘天哪，她醉了’。一家网络媒体追踪到他并人肉了他。结果他只是个日工，一个非裔特朗普粉丝，觉得这样很好玩。据他说，他只是把视频发到了自己的脸书页面上。更令人不安的是，脸书把他的信息交给了那个网站。为什么？就因为他做了个搞笑视频让人看起来醉了？关于我的这种视频有上百万个。我觉得脸书、推特和其他社交媒体平台正在通过这些行为慢性自杀。但这件事很惊人——他们居然出卖了这个劳工。

turns.en: Because he made a parody video or he made someone look foolish with editing. Well, you now have basically the media views it as their job to go after individuals they don't like.
turns.zh: 因为他做了一个恶搞视频，或者通过剪辑让别人看起来很蠢。现在媒体基本上把追捕他们不喜欢的个人当作自己的工作。

turns.en: Yeah, I use media with air quotes in that regard.
