So I don't think this is something that The New York Times would have done anything responsible, but the media is getting more and more desperate, right? Because what happened was, before the internet, you could have two local newspapers in every town and you could have two local news stations, you know, TV stations in every town. And then CNN came along and started commoditizing the news 24/7 broadcast. And then the internet came along, that was the final nail in the coffin. Because what the internet did was, it said, actually, if there's a fact that's news, you can distribute that immediately. It can go on Twitter, it can go on Facebook, it gets reprinted on Google News a thousand times. You know, you go to Google News, you're like, okay, what's the piece of news? Which source? And three thousand other articles too many, right? Yeah. So news has become commoditized. So the entire news media has shifted into peddling opinions and entertainment. Yes. And so now they've become a variation between like cheerleaders, shock troops, enforcers, you know, talking heads. So these are now tribal. These are not propaganda machines signaling for their tribes. It's a right-wing one, this left-wing one, right? There's the alt-right, there's the control left, and the two of them are just fighting it out using their various media organs and memes. So basically, when you see one of these news organizations doxing an individual, that's like a tank running over a soldier, right? That's what's going on. It's just war. And so there's no such thing anymore as a neutral media commentator. The illusion of objectivity that journalism had is lost. There's no longer one guy like a Walter Cronkite that everyone's going to listen to. It's now all just shock troops fighting wars with each other.
turns.zh: 是的，我在这方面给媒体加了引号。所以我不认为《纽约时报》会做任何负责任的事情，但媒体越来越绝望了，对吧？因为发生的事情是，在互联网之前，每个城镇可以有两家地方报纸和两家地方新闻台，你知道的，电视新闻台。然后CNN出现了，开始将新闻商品化，24/7广播。然后互联网来了，这是压垮骆驼的最后一根稻草。因为互联网所做的就是，它说，实际上，如果有一个事实是新闻，你可以立即传播它。它可以上Twitter，可以上Facebook，在Google News上被转载一千次。你知道，你上Google News，你会想，好吧，这条新闻是什么？哪个来源？还有另外三千篇文章，太多了，对吧？是的。所以新闻已经商品化了。因此整个新闻媒体已经转向兜售观点和娱乐。是的。所以现在它们变成了啦啦队、突击部队、执行者、你知道的，脱口秀评论员之类的变体。所以这些现在是部落化的。这些不是为它们的部落传递信号的宣传机器。这是一个右翼的，一个左翼的，对吧？有另类右翼，有控制左翼，它们只是用各自的各种媒体机构和迷因来争斗。所以基本上，当你看到这些新闻机构之一人肉搜索一个个人时，那就像一辆坦克碾过一个士兵，对吧？这就是正在发生的事情。这只是一场战争。所以不再有中立的媒体评论员这种东西了。新闻业曾经拥有的客观性幻觉已经消失了。不再有一个像沃尔特·克朗凯特那样每个人都会听的人了。现在都只是互相争斗的突击部队。

transcript.sec: Internet creates hegemons and atomized individuals

turns.en: How does this play out? Have you thought about it?
turns.zh: 这会如何发展？你想过吗？

turns.en: Yeah, a little bit. So what the internet does, a lot of this is internet driven. What the internet does is, the internet creates one giant aggregator or two for everything: one taxi dispatcher, one e-commerce store, one search engine, one, you know, one social media site for friends and family, one for business, etc. So the internet is this giant aggregator where it creates one big hegemon for everything, and it creates an atomized long tail of millions and millions of individuals. What it gets rid of is the medium-sized ones in the middle. So for example, you might have had like seven Hollywood studios. What's all going to be Netflix? You had, you know, like 10 large e-commerce players, from Walmart to Costco to, you know, Kmart and whatever. Now it's just going to be Amazon and a ton of small individual brands. So that's the world that we're headed towards: one hegemon and millions of individuals. So where it ends up long term is media will be a few gigantic outlets, you know, it could be The New York Times, it could be Facebook, a few like that. And there's going to be just a really long tail of millions of independent people. So this idea of who's a journalist and who's not, you know, is Assange a journalist or not? Everyone's a journalist. That's the world that we're headed towards.
turns.zh: 是的，有一点。所以互联网所做的，很多都是互联网驱动的。互联网所做的是，互联网为每件事创造了一个或两个巨大的聚合器：一个出租车调度员，一个电子商务商店，一个搜索引擎，一个，你知道的，一个面向朋友和家人的社交媒体网站，一个面向商业的，等等。所以互联网是一个巨大的聚合器，它为每件事创造了一个大的霸主，并创造了一个由数百万个体组成的原子化的长尾。它摆脱的是中间的中等规模的东西。例如，你可能曾有七家好莱坞制片厂。什么都会变成Netflix？你有，你知道的，像10个大型电子商务参与者，从沃尔玛到好市多，到，你知道的，凯马特等等。现在只会是亚马逊和一大堆小型独立品牌。所以这就是我们正在走向的世界：一个霸主和数百万个体。所以长期来看，媒体将变成少数几个巨大的媒体机构，你知道的，可能是《纽约时报》，可能是Facebook，几个这样的。然后会有一个非常长的由数百万独立个体组成的尾巴。所以谁是记者谁不是记者的这个想法，你知道的，阿桑奇是不是记者？每个人都是记者。这就是我们正在走向的世界。

transcript.sec: Algorithm writers are the most powerful

turns.en: I do think the extreme power, the most powerful people in the world today, and this is not well known, but the most powerful people in the world today are the people who are writing the algorithms for Twitter and Facebook and Instagram, because they're controlling the spread of information. They're literally rewriting people's brains, they're programming the culture, and they're doing it very subtly. Like Google, I believe that, you know, one of their execs got up in front of Congress and the congressman asked him, you know, do you manipulate search results? And he said no, we do not manipulate search results. Really? That's your job. That is literally all Google does. Google has one job, which is to manipulate search results to pull them out of the noise and rank them properly. And the precise algorithms of how they do that is very hidden, very complex, but influences the hearts and minds of everybody, including all the voters.
turns.zh: 我确实认为极端的权力，当今世界上最有权势的人，这一点并不广为人知，但当今世界上最有权势的人是那些为Twitter、Facebook和Instagram编写算法的人，因为他们控制着信息的传播。他们实际上在重写人们的大脑，他们在编程文化，而且他们做得非常微妙。比如谷歌，我相信，你知道的，他们的一位高管在国会面前，国会议员问他，你知道的，你们操纵搜索结果吗？他说不，我们不操纵搜索结果。真的吗？那是你的工作。那实际上就是谷歌所做的全部。谷歌有一项工作，那就是操纵搜索结果，把它们从噪音中提取出来并正确排序。而他们这样做的精确算法非常隐蔽，非常复杂，但影响着每个人的心灵和思想，包括所有选民。

turns.en: Now, if Google, Facebook, and Twitter had been smart about this, they would not have picked sides. They would have said, we're publishers. Whatever goes through our pipes goes through our pipes. If it's illegal, we'll take it down. Give us a court order. Otherwise, we don't touch it. It's like the phone company. If I call you up and I say something horrible to you on the phone, the phone company doesn't get in trouble. But the moment they started taking stuff down that wasn't illegal because somebody screamed, they basically lost their right to be viewed as a carrier. And now all of a sudden they've taken on liability. So they're sliding down the slippery slope into ruin, sloping to ruin, where the left wants them to take down the right, the right wants them to take down the left, and now they have no more friends, they have no allies. Traditionally, the libertarian-leaning Republicans and Democrats would have stood up in principle for the common carriers, but now they won't. So my guess is, as soon as Congress—this day is coming, if not already here. It might even be here today, actually, because I saw something related in the news. The day is coming when the politicians realize that these social media platforms are picking the next president, the next congressman. They're literally picking, and they have the power to pick. So they will be controlled by the government.
turns.zh: 现在，如果谷歌、Facebook和Twitter对此聪明一点，他们就不会选边站。他们会说，我们是出版商。任何通过我们管道的东西都会通过我们的管道。如果是非法的，我们会删除。给我们法院命令。否则，我们不碰它。就像电话公司一样。如果我打电话给你，在电话里对你说了一些可怕的话，电话公司不会惹上麻烦。但一旦他们开始删除并非非法的内容，因为有人尖叫，他们基本上就失去了被视为运营商的资格。现在突然间他们承担了责任。所以他们正在滑下滑坡走向毁灭，左派希望他们删除右派，右派希望他们删除左派，现在他们没有朋友了，没有盟友了。传统上，倾向自由意志主义的共和党人和民主党人会原则上支持公共运营商，但现在他们不会了。所以我的猜测是，一旦国会——这一天正在到来，如果还没有到来的话。实际上可能今天就已经到来了，因为我在新闻中看到了相关的东西。这一天即将到来，政客们意识到这些社交媒体平台正在挑选下一任总统、下一任国会议员。他们实际上在挑选，而且他们有权力挑选。所以它们将被政府控制。

turns.en: In what way? How do you think they're going to be controlled? You think they're going to have to adhere to strict principles of freedom of speech?
turns.zh: 以什么方式？你认为它们将如何被控制？你认为它们必须遵守严格的言论自由原则吗？

turns.en: No, first, particularly unfortunately, it's headed the opposite direction, right? Absolutely. I wish it was freedom of speech. Much more likely, they're going to be, in the short to medium term, they're going to be hauled in for hearings. They're going to be pressured massively: do this, don't do that. My concern about that is, the hearings that I saw with Zuckerberg, those people were completely incompetent. They don't seem to understand. They don't. But they're just applying pressure. They're just trying to scare him so he'll do what they want. And what do they want him to do? They want him to basically suppress the other side. So if you're right wing, you want to suppress the left wing. If you're left wing, you want to suppress the right wing. And if you just see where these companies are headquartered, in Silicon Valley, all the sensors, and that's really what they are, there are sensors working inside these companies. They're just called by different names, obviously, right? It's double speak. You call it the Department of Defense when it's the Department of War. So in this case, the Department of Safety and Trust when it's really the Department of Censorship. The sensors are inside Silicon Valley. So it's going to be a mess.
turns.zh: 不，首先，特别不幸的是，它正朝着相反的方向发展，对吧？绝对。我希望是言论自由。更可能的是，在短期到中期内，它们会被传唤去听证会。它们会受到巨大压力：做这个，别做那个。我对此的担忧是，我看到的扎克伯格的听证会，那些人完全无能。他们似乎不理解。他们不理解。但他们只是在施加压力。他们只是试图吓唬他，让他做他们想做的事。而他们想让他做什么？他们想让他基本上压制另一方。所以如果你是右翼，你想压制左翼。如果你是左翼，你想压制右翼。而如果你看看这些公司的总部所在地，在硅谷，所有的审查官，实际上就是他们，这些公司内部有审查官在工作。他们只是被叫了不同的名字，显然，对吧？这是双重语言。你把战争部叫做国防部。所以在这种情况下，安全与信任部实际上是审查部。审查官就在硅谷内部。所以这将是一团糟。

turns.en: Silicon Valley's politics are extremely progressive left-wing. And if you're not that, you really have no place there. I mean, try being a conservative, an open conservative at Google. Good luck. Now you get lynched. Yes, that's crazy. I mean, I don't think there was ever a thing like that that was so influential and so politically and ideologically one-sided.
turns.zh: 硅谷的政治氛围极其进步左倾。如果你不是那样，你在那里真的没有立足之地。我的意思是，试着在谷歌做一个公开的保守派。祝你好运。现在你会被围攻。是的，这太疯狂了。我认为从来没有一个如此有影响力、在政治和意识形态上如此一边倒的东西。

turns.en: There's a little saying on the internet, I think it's called Conquest's Law: any organization that's not explicitly right-wing eventually becomes left-wing. And I don't know why that's true, but it does seem to me to be true.
turns.zh: 网上有句俗话，我想叫它康奎斯特定律：任何没有明确右翼的组织最终都会变成左翼。我不知道为什么这是真的，但在我看来确实如此。

turns.en: Well, it's a fascinating battle that's going on right now. I mean, it really is. And conservatives, as far as social media is concerned, they're just getting chopped off at the knees left and right.
turns.zh: 嗯，现在正在进行一场迷人的战斗。我的意思是，确实如此。而保守派，就社交媒体而言，他们正在被左右夹击。

turns.en: What'll eventually happen is that whenever you suppress speech, the organism metastasizes. Then it has to start turning towards other means. If you're unlucky, it goes towards violence. If you're lucky, they find other outlets. I think what will happen is we will start creating decentralized media that's not owned by any single entity, that can't be suppressed or shut down, that will then start spreading these various things, and that will take the place of Twitter or Facebook.
turns.zh: 最终会发生的是，每当你压制言论，这个有机体就会转移。然后它必须开始转向其他手段。如果不幸，它会走向暴力。如果幸运，他们会找到其他出口。我认为会发生的是，我们将开始创建去中心化的媒体，不被任何单一实体拥有，不能被压制或关闭，然后开始传播这些各种东西，并将取代Twitter或Facebook。

turns.en: That's right, but it's going to take 10 years, 20 years. It's not overnight. Well, you know, Twitter took 10 years to get to the point where it's at this mess right now. But it was so interesting to have Jack Dorsey and to talk to him about where it's going, where he thinks it's going in his own principles, which he believes that it's a fundamental right, and he believes that freedom of speech is something that we all should have, and that these platforms should essentially be like utilities, like the electric company.
turns.zh: 没错，但这需要10年、20年。不是一夜之间。嗯，你知道，Twitter花了10年才走到现在这个烂摊子。但能请到杰克·多西并和他谈论它的走向、他认为它基于他自己的原则会走向何方，这很有趣。他相信这是一项基本权利，他相信言论自由是我们所有人都应该拥有的，这些平台本质上应该像公用事业一样，比如电力公司。

turns.en: Jack is correct and he has the right vision. It's just he's in an organization where the other individuals in the organization feel differently, very differently. So the organization itself can get hijacked, and his timeline for changing things, it's like it's decades. I mean, I don't know, I shouldn't say decades, but I mean, I was like, when do you think that something...
turns.zh: 杰克是对的，他有正确的愿景。只是他所在的组织中，其他成员感觉不同，非常不同。所以组织本身可能被劫持，他改变事物的时间表，就像几十年。我的意思是，我不知道，我不应该说几十年，但我的意思是，我当时想，你认为什么时候会...？

turns.en: Well, there was one idea of having an uncensored Twitter, like one Twitter that's the Wild West. Like you could have regular Twitter or you could try Wild West Twitter.
turns.zh: 嗯，有一个想法是拥有一个未经审查的Twitter，就像一个狂野西部的Twitter。比如你可以有常规Twitter，或者你可以尝试狂野西部Twitter。

turns.en: Well, that already exists. There's a network called Gab. Yes, but Gab isn't even Wild West Twitter. When people dox people, they remove things like that. Yeah, I mean, I think there's certainly lines around violence and illegality that you don't want to cross. But yes, Gab is closer to a free speech platform, but it's still not decentralized. It can still get shut down, it can still get taken out. It's also suppressed heavily. Yes, so that... and the people on there right now are extremely right-wing, so it's not a pleasant place for someone like me to hang out with all the people that have been kicked off of something.
turns.zh: 嗯，那已经存在了。有一个叫Gab的网络。是的，但Gab甚至不是狂野西部Twitter。当人们人肉搜索时，他们会删除这类东西。是的，我的意思是，我认为肯定有关于暴力和非法的界限，你不想跨越。但没错，Gab更接近一个言论自由平台，但它仍然不是去中心化的。它仍然可能被关闭，仍然可能被取缔。它也受到严重压制。是的，所以……现在那里的人非常右翼，所以对我来说不是一个愉快的地方，和所有被踢出某个平台的人混在一起。

turns.en: That's right. Try going over there and being moderate. Try going over there, there's no room for you. Yeah, unfortunately, because I don't identify as any party or any creed, you know, it doesn't work for me.
turns.zh: 没错。试着去那里做个温和派。试着去那里，没有你的空间。是的，不幸的是，因为我不认同任何政党或任何信条，你知道，这对我来说行不通。

turns.en: Is that a problem in Silicon Valley when you don't identify as anything? Do you get pressure?
turns.zh: 在硅谷，当你不认同任何东西时，这是个问题吗？你会受到压力吗？

turns.en: It totally... it used to be okay. It's not okay anymore. When was it okay? Like 10 years ago, I would say it was okay. And then you started seeing a shift. Yeah, and now you have to pick sides, otherwise you're automatically the enemy. Really? Yeah, struggle sessions and all that. God, struggle sessions. I'm exaggerating for effect, but it definitely has that oppressive feeling to it. Right, and you also have to be politically outspoken. Right, yeah, it can't be something that you just stay neutral about. Right, it's like when Tim Ferriss, I think at some point, put out a tweet about how you can't just say anything anymore, and you know, people are being suppressed, and a whole bunch of people who love them from Silicon Valley piled on and said, 'What is it that you can't say? What are you afraid to say? You can say whatever you want, Tim. Go ahead, what are you afraid of?' Right, they don't like baiting. Yeah, what was he trying to say? Wow, we have to put him in that box. What is someone who's thinking about saying something he shouldn't have said? Exactly. Now we know. One great tweet I saw was, 'You know, the left won the culture wars. Now they're just driving around shooting the survivors.' Wow, that's hilarious. Yeah.
turns.zh: 完全……以前还好。现在不行了。什么时候还好？大概10年前，我会说还好。然后你开始看到转变。是的，现在你必须选边站，否则你自动成为敌人。真的吗？是的，批判会之类的。天哪，批判会。我夸张了，但它确实有那种压迫感。对，而且你还必须在政治上直言不讳。对，是的，你不能保持中立。对，就像蒂姆·费里斯，我想在某个时候，发了一条推文，说你不能再随便说话了，你知道，人们被压制，然后一大群硅谷喜欢他的人蜂拥而上说：‘你不能说什么？你害怕说什么？你可以说任何你想说的，蒂姆。说吧，你怕什么？’对，他们不喜欢诱饵。是的，他想说什么？哇，我们必须把他放进那个盒子里。一个正在考虑说他不该说的话的人是什么？没错。现在我们知道了。我看到一条很棒的推文：‘你知道，左派赢得了文化战争。现在他们只是开车四处射杀幸存者。’哇，这太搞笑了。是的。

turns.en: I wonder who has won the culture war. There's certainly a battle that's been won in terms of control of social media. Control of social media is absolutely left.
turns.zh: 我想知道谁赢得了文化战争。在社交媒体控制方面，肯定有一场战斗已经赢了。社交媒体的控制绝对是左翼的。

turns.en: Well, this is unfortunate for conservatives, but technology is a force that also pushes left. So if you look all throughout human history, the left essentially grows and grows and grows. Right? Why is that? Why is it inexorably that, as some commentators have said, Leviathan slouches left? Right, Leviathan is the government. Why does it slouch left? And I think a lot of that has been because of technology. Technology has made it so that it makes more like industrial revolution technology. We all band together, we're wards of the state. Right? Contraception is a technology that kind of helps lean leftward. It takes away from the family unit. Abortion is a technology, right? It wasn't possible thousands of years ago. So technology actually empowers the individual. The individual means that you have the breakdown of family structure and religion and all that. And I'm not mostly opposed to that, but it does mean that there's a leftward shift to it. Now we're getting a small set of technologies that actually can take you more rightward. Encryption is an example, because encryption makes it easier to have privacy, it makes it easier to have money that is outside of the state. Guns, 3D printing of guns is an example of a technology that is more of a rightward shift. But generally, technology leads the world left.
turns.zh: 嗯，这对保守派来说是不幸的，但技术也是一种向左推的力量。所以如果你纵观整个人类历史，左派基本上在增长、增长、再增长。对吧？为什么？为什么正如一些评论员所说，利维坦向左倾斜？对，利维坦就是政府。为什么它向左倾斜？我认为很大程度上是因为技术。技术使得它更像工业革命技术。我们团结在一起，我们是国家的受监护人。对吧？避孕是一种有助于向左倾斜的技术。它削弱了家庭单位。堕胎是一种技术，对吧？几千年前是不可能的。所以技术实际上赋予个人权力。个人意味着家庭结构和宗教等的瓦解。我基本上不反对这一点，但这确实意味着向左的转变。现在我们得到了一小部分实际上可以让你更向右的技术。加密是一个例子，因为加密使隐私更容易，使拥有国家之外的货币更容易。枪支，3D打印枪支是一个更向右转的技术例子。但总的来说，技术引领世界向左。

turns.en: Yeah, it's also usually highly educated people that are involved in technology in the first place. And I think when you look at universities in particular, they tend to lean left in this country as well.
turns.zh: 是的，通常也是高学历的人首先参与技术。我认为当你特别看大学时，它们在这个国家也倾向于左倾。

turns.en: Well, universities, what happened with the university is very interesting. Universities first, when you know, became the arbiters of data and intellectualism and what's right and wrong. So there's a time period when it was like, should we be doing that or not? Well, let's look at the university. What do they have to say? What are the smartest people, the professors, the think tanks have to say? And the universities got this credibility from the hard sciences. So they got this from, you know, physics and math and computer science and chemistry, because these deliver real things: the Manhattan Project, the microprocessor, the space vehicles, and so on, the electric car. So they gain this mantle of authority and legitimacy from the hard sciences. So then come the social sciences kind of sneak in. Then you get economics and...
turns.zh: 嗯，大学，大学发生的事情非常有趣。大学首先，你知道，成为数据和理性主义以及是非对错的仲裁者。所以有一段时间，就像，我们该不该做那个？嗯，让我们看看大学。他们怎么说？最聪明的人，教授，智库怎么说？大学从硬科学中获得了这种可信度。所以他们从，你知道，物理、数学、计算机科学和化学中获得了这个，因为这些带来了真实的东西：曼哈顿计划、微处理器、太空飞行器等等，电动汽车。所以他们从硬科学中获得了这种权威和合法性的外衣。然后社会科学偷偷溜进来。然后你得到经济学和……

transcript.sec: Corruption of Science and Social Sciences

turns.en: Microeconomics is a real discipline, real science, real math behind it, logic, reason. And then you get macroeconomics, which can be politicized a little bit more, voodoo. And then you get social studies, and then you get gender studies, and then you get blah blah blah. So what happened is that because we took scientists to be the high priests of our new world, science itself has gotten corrupted. And the social sciences—and you can tell they're fake sciences because the word 'science' tacked on at the end—have come in and hijacked the universities and become the new think tanks. So essentially, what you see going on today in the universities is a war between the social sciences and the physical sciences. The crossover point is biology, where you can see the whole 'gender is a social construct' movement is attacking biology and evolutionary biology. Just like in the social sphere, they're coming after the comedians. But you can see the struggle going on in the universities, and I would say the physical sciences are essentially losing that war.
turns.zh: 微观经济学是一门真正的学科，真正的科学，背后有真正的数学、逻辑和理性。然后你有了宏观经济学，它更容易被政治化，有点巫毒。然后你有了社会科学，再然后你有了性别研究，等等等等。所以发生的事情是，因为我们把科学家当作新世界的大祭司，科学本身已经被腐蚀了。而社会科学——你可以看出它们是伪科学，因为后面硬加了个'科学'——已经入侵并劫持了大学，成为新的智库。所以基本上，你今天在大学里看到的是社会科学和自然科学之间的战争。交叉点是生物学，在那里你可以看到整个'性别是社会建构'的运动正在攻击生物学和进化生物学。就像在社会领域，他们正在攻击喜剧演员。但你可以看到大学里的斗争，我会说自然科学基本上正在输掉这场战争。

turns.en: What can be done? Or is it just something that has to play out? Do we have to realize the consequences of the foolishness?
turns.zh: 能做些什么？还是说这只能任其发展？我们是否必须意识到这种愚蠢的后果？

turns.en: Well, the good news is the physical sciences have reality on their side. But it's not even respected in many ways. But at the end of the day, your aircraft still has to fly, your microprocessor still has to compute. So there's only so far they can take it. But I do see, for example in biology, a lot of biologists are facing this difficult thing where they have to say things that they know are not true to keep their job.
turns.zh: 好消息是，自然科学有现实站在它们一边。但在很多方面甚至不被尊重。但归根结底，你的飞机仍然必须飞起来，你的微处理器仍然必须计算。所以他们只能走到那一步。但我确实看到，例如在生物学中，很多生物学家正面临这样的困境：他们不得不说一些他们知道不是真的东西来保住工作。

turns.en: Like what?
turns.zh: 比如什么？

turns.en: Well, you had Bret Weinstein on here, right? So that's a clear example. So the crossover line of what is acceptable and what's not is entering into biology. And biology will probably suffer the most. Synthetic biology, for example, will end up in China because you won't be able to map facts and reality and actions together. You won't be able to get grants, you won't be able to get the adulation of your peers. I don't know enough here, so now I'm in shaky territory, but it's just my sense that that crossover battleground right now is in evolutionary biology. Economics lost. Well, it's certainly in terms of gender, and that seems to be one of the major battlegrounds.
turns.zh: 嗯，你请过布雷特·温斯坦上节目，对吧？那是一个明显的例子。所以可接受和不可接受之间的分界线正在进入生物学。而生物学可能受害最深。例如，合成生物学最终会跑到中国去，因为你无法将事实、现实和行动联系起来。你拿不到资助，也得不到同行的赞誉。我对此了解不够，所以我现在处于不稳定的领域，但我的感觉是，当前的交叉战场是在进化生物学。经济学已经输了。嗯，当然在性别方面，这似乎是主要战场之一。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, it's also going to happen for example, blank slate theory. You know, are we nature? Are we nurture? It's kind of socially unacceptable to say that a lot of it is nature and not nurture, or vice versa, depending on which side you're on. Right, those kinds of discussions get corrupted. They do get corrupted, and it's really unfortunate because that's an unbelievably important thing to understand: what makes a person a sociopath, what makes a person a super successful person, a winner, what makes a person a drug addict. What are these factors? You can't have a reasonable conversation about climate science anymore, right? It's not a science, it's all politicized. You can't even bring it up; everyone's got their minds made up already.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，这也会发生在比如白板理论上。你知道，我们是天性？还是教养？说很多是天性而不是教养，或者反过来，取决于你站在哪一边，这在社会上是不被接受的。对，这类讨论被腐蚀了。它们确实被腐蚀了，这真的很不幸，因为那是极其重要的事情要理解：是什么让一个人成为反社会者，是什么让一个人成为超级成功者、赢家，是什么让一个人成为吸毒者。这些因素是什么？你不能再对气候科学进行合理的讨论了，对吧？它不再是科学，它完全政治化了。你甚至不能提起它；每个人都已经拿定了主意。

turns.en: Well, what's uncomfortable to me is people have their minds made up and they don't even have the data. Well, on most of these topics, people are talking past each other anyway. They're talking about different things. Like when you get into gun control, for example, right? One side is talking about the right to bear arms in case a tyrannical ruler or king tries to take over the country. The other side is talking about school shootings and protecting people in their homes from crime. So they're just talking about two different things. And it's just not politically acceptable to even talk about the same thing. Or when it comes to immigration, the right is talking about... the left is bundling together illegal immigration and legal immigration into one thing. Whereas on the right, sometimes you've got racists hiding in there, so it doesn't help their cause. They're talking about two different things. If they were talking about the same thing—which is how many immigrants should we let into the country and what are the criteria for that—that would be a very different conversation than 'no immigrants' or 'everybody comes in.' And also on the left, you have this benefit that everybody who's currently coming in illegally is going to vote for the left because of where they're coming from in their socioeconomic circumstances.
turns.zh: 嗯，让我不舒服的是人们已经拿定了主意，但他们甚至没有数据。嗯，在大多数这些话题上，人们反正是在各说各话。他们在谈论不同的事情。比如当你谈到枪支管制时，对吧？一方在谈论携带武器的权利，以防暴虐的统治者或国王试图接管国家。另一方在谈论校园枪击案和保护人们在家免受犯罪侵害。所以他们只是在谈论两件不同的事情。而且甚至在政治上都不允许谈论同一件事。或者当谈到移民时，右派在谈论……左派把非法移民和合法移民混为一谈。而在右派，有时你里面有隐藏的种族主义者，所以这对他们的事业没有帮助。他们在谈论两件不同的事情。如果他们谈论的是同一件事——我们应该让多少移民进入这个国家，以及标准是什么——那将是一个非常不同的对话，而不是'不要移民'或'所有人都进来'。而且在左派，你还有这个好处：所有目前非法进入的人都会投票给左派，因为他们来自的社会经济背景。

turns.en: To me, the test of any good system is: you build a system, hand it over to your enemies to run for the next decade. So for example, if you want censorship on Twitter or Facebook, you should build that system and then hand it over to the other side to run. So if you're a left-winger who's promoting censorship, let somebody else run it. Same with immigration: if you want an immigration system, build the system, then hand it over to the other side to run it. That's how you know it's a good system.
turns.zh: 对我来说，任何好系统的测试是：你建立一个系统，然后把它交给你的敌人来运行十年。所以例如，如果你想要在Twitter或Facebook上审查，你应该建立那个系统，然后把它交给另一方来运行。所以如果你是一个提倡审查的左派，让别人来运行它。移民也一样：如果你想要一个移民系统，建立系统，然后把它交给另一方来运行。这样你才知道它是一个好系统。

turns.en: There's no room for nuance when you're dealing with these political battlegrounds. When you're dealing with right versus left, and one side has a clearly established stance that you're supposed to take, like gun control is a great example of that. There's no room for 'what about mental health?' 'What about the fact that so many of these people are on psych medications?' Why is that not being discussed? We're running one of the greatest mental health experiments in history when we're doping everybody up with SSRIs. And you know, maybe if you give 30 million people SSRIs, maybe 29.9 million are a lot happier, and then you have a fraction that commits suicide or detonate. You're basically trading the mean for the variance. You have blow-up risk.
turns.zh: 当你在处理这些政治战场时，没有细微差别的空间。当你在处理右派与左派时，一方有一个明确确立的立场你应该采取，比如枪支管制就是一个很好的例子。没有空间说'那心理健康呢？''那这么多人在服用精神药物的事实呢？'为什么那没有被讨论？我们正在运行历史上最伟大的心理健康实验之一，当我们给所有人服用SSRIs。而且你知道，也许如果你给3000万人SSRIs，也许2990万人更快乐了，然后你有一小部分人自杀或爆炸。你基本上是用均值换方差。你有爆炸风险。

turns.en: Yeah, there's no room for nuance, which is why I stay out of politics largely.
turns.zh: 是的，没有细微差别的空间，这就是为什么我基本上远离政治。

turns.en: Do they drag you in though? Sometimes they always try.
turns.zh: 但他们把你拖进去吗？有时他们总是尝试。

turns.en: I mean, these conversations, in this conversation you get to get dragged in, sure. But I'm sure there's going to be some people here... Here's the thing about politics: because we have a first-past-the-post system, what that means is that whoever wins 51% of the vote in this country gets a lot of the power. It's not like proportional representation where the Greens have 10% and libertarians have 3% or whatever. It's just like, you're all Democrat in power now, we're all Republican. Because of that, to win, you have to pick one of these two sides. You have to choose. You can't just say, 'I'm going to be nuanced about it.' You can't vote for a third party; that's throwing away your vote. I have a friend who's trying to fix that. He's starting this thing called 'A Good Party' where you kick-start your vote, so you combine all your votes, you hold them in reserve.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，这些对话，在这次对话中你确实被拖进去了，当然。但我肯定这里会有一些人……关于政治的事情是：因为我们有一个简单多数制，这意味着在这个国家谁赢得51%的选票谁就获得大量权力。它不像比例代表制，绿党有10%，自由意志党有3%之类的。它就像，现在你们全是民主党掌权，我们全是共和党。正因为如此，为了获胜，你必须选择这两方之一。你必须选择。你不能只是说，'我要对此保持微妙。'你不能投票给第三方；那是浪费你的选票。我有一个朋友正在试图解决这个问题。他正在创办一个叫做'好党'的东西，在那里你启动你的投票，所以你结合你所有的选票，你把它们保留起来。

turns.en: And then when you have enough to win, then you vote that person in power, right? You don't throw away your vote. But outside of those hacks, we're never gonna get a third party elected. So because of that, all of your beliefs have to neatly fit into the Democrat bundle or the Republican bundle. And so when you get into that tribe, if you signal outside of that bundle, you get attacked. Yeah, so it's literally making you into an unclear thinker. It's making you into a muddled thinker. If all of your beliefs line up into one political party, you're not a clear thinker. If all your beliefs are the same as your neighbors and your friends, you're not a clear thinker. You're literally just your beliefs are socialized; they're taken from other people. So if you want to be a clear thinker, you cannot pay attention to politics. It will destroy your ability to think.
turns.zh: 然后当你有了足够的票数去赢，你就投票让那个人掌权，对吧？你不会浪费你的选票。但除了那些 hack 之外，我们永远不可能让第三方当选。所以正因为如此，你所有的信念都必须整齐地归入民主党或共和党的阵营。当你进入那个部落时，如果你在阵营之外发出信号，你就会受到攻击。是的，这实际上让你成为一个不清晰的思考者，让你成为一个混乱的思考者。如果你所有的信念都归于一个政党，你不是一个清晰的思考者。如果你所有的信念都和你的邻居、朋友一样，你也不是一个清晰的思考者。你的信念只是被社会化了，是从别人那里拿来的。所以如果你想成为一个清晰的思考者，你就不能关注政治。它会摧毁你思考的能力。

turns.en: What I dread most of modern life: all our diseases are diseases of abundance, not diseases of scarcity. Right? Old times, I may have starved. You know, old times, if I got sugar, that was a wonderful thing; I should have eaten all the sugar to get my hands on. If I got a piece of news or gossip, that was interesting data that would have helped my life and moved me forward. If I got some brief amount of entertainment, whether through video games or magazines or whatever, that would have been good. Now it's all disease of abundance. We are overexposed to everything. So the way to survive in modern society is to be an ascetic. It is to retreat from society. There's too much society everywhere you go: society in your phone, society in your pocket, society in your ears. You're being socialized right now by listening to this podcast, right? We're socializing you, we're programming you. Everyone's trying to program everybody. The only solution is turn it off. The only solution is to turn it off and concentrate on your breathing, meditation.
turns.zh: 我最害怕现代生活的是：我们所有的疾病都是富足病，而不是匮乏病。对吧？过去，我可能会挨饿。你知道，过去，如果我得到糖，那是件美妙的事；我应该吃掉所有能到手的糖。如果我得到一条新闻或八卦，那是有趣的数据，能帮助我的生活并推动我前进。如果我得到一点娱乐，无论是通过电子游戏还是杂志之类的，那都是好的。现在一切都是富足病。我们对一切都过度暴露。所以在现代社会中生存的方式就是做一个苦行者。就是从社会中退出来。无论你走到哪里，社会都太多了：你手机里的社会，你口袋里的社会，你耳朵里的社会。你现在听这个播客就是在被社会化，对吧？我们在社会化你，我们在编程你。每个人都在试图编程别人。唯一的解决办法就是关掉它。唯一的解决办法就是关掉它，专注于你的呼吸、冥想。

turns.en: Yeah, yes. I mean, it works. It's been a lifesaver for me. Oh, I do it, and I do it whenever I get like spare time. I was in the doctor's office this morning and I knew it was going to be 20 minutes, so I just sat there with my eyes closed for 20 minutes and I meditated. You know, when I was growing up, there was this statement, I think it was Pascal: he said, you know, all of man's problems arise because he cannot sit by himself in a room for 30 minutes alone. And it's very true. I always needed to be stimulated. And when the iPhone came along, boredom was dead. I would never be bored again, even if I'm standing in line, I'm on my iPhone. And I thought it was great. And when I was a kid, I used to try and overclock my brain, be like, how many thoughts can I think at once? The answer is only one. But I would try to like think multiple thoughts at once, and I was proud of that. I was proud that my brain was always running, this engine was always moving. And it's a disease. It's actually the road to misery. And now that I'm older, I realize like you actually want to again rest your mind. You want to learn how to settle into your mind. Now I look forward to solitary confinement. You leave me alone for a day, it'll be like the happiest day I've had in a while. And that is a superpower that I think everybody can attain: the superpower of learning to be alone and enjoying it.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。我的意思是，它有效。它对我来说是救星。哦，我冥想，而且我一有空就冥想。今天早上我在医生办公室，我知道要等20分钟，所以我就闭着眼睛坐在那里冥想了20分钟。你知道，我小时候有一句话，我想是帕斯卡说的：他说，人类所有的问题都源于他不能独自在房间里坐30分钟。这非常正确。我总是需要刺激。当iPhone出现时，无聊就死了。我再也不会无聊了，即使排队，我也在用iPhone。我当时觉得这很棒。小时候，我试图超频我的大脑，比如，我一次能想多少个想法？答案只有一个。但我会试着同时想多个想法，我为此感到自豪。我自豪我的大脑总是在运转，这个引擎总是在动。这是一种病。实际上是通往痛苦的道路。现在我年纪大了，我意识到你实际上想要再次让大脑休息。你想要学会如何安定在你的大脑里。现在我期待独处。你让我一个人待一天，那将是我一段时间以来最快乐的一天。这是一个超能力，我认为每个人都能获得：学会独处并享受它的超能力。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. Well, I think it's critical. And I do think that these times where you just think about things, just be alone and think about things, are so rare these days. And I think during those rare times is when you really get to understand what you actually believe or don't believe.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。嗯，我认为这很关键。我确实认为，这些你只是思考事情、独自一人思考事情的时间，如今非常罕见。我认为在这些罕见的时间里，你才能真正理解你实际上相信什么或不相信什么。

turns.en: Yeah, it's funny. When I first started meditating, it was really hard, right? Because everybody, I think a lot of people who listen to this broadcast, they've heard of meditation, it has a good rep. So everybody tries it, they struggle, they kind of give it up. It's one of those things that everybody says they do but nobody actually does, right? It's like not eating sugar, right? Everyone talks about how, yeah, I don't eat sugar, but like yeah, then right, then the dessert tray rolls around and everyone's going for the cookies. Yeah, right. So it's become one of those things. And in fact, it's not even become a signaling thing where it's like, oh, how much did you meditate? I meditated this much. Or you know, there are people now wearing headbands saying with tweety birds a chirp, and then when they're in deep meditation, I don't know how they make it work, but they'll be like, I got a lot of trips today. How many trips did you get? Oh my god, oh, your meditation technique is wrong, mine is right. But really, all it is is the art of doing nothing. Okay? And it's important because I think when we grow up, right, it's all this stuff happening to you in your life. And some of it you're processing, some of it you're absorbing, and some of it you should probably think a little bit more about and work through, but you don't, you don't have time. So it gets buried in you. And it's all these preferences and judgments and unresolved situations and issues. And it's like your email inbox, it's just piling up email after email after email that's not answered, going back 10, 20, 30, 40 years. And then when you sit down to meditate, those emails start coming back at you: hey, what about this issue? What about that issue? Have you solved this? Did you think about that? Your regrets there, you have issues there. And that gets scary. People don't want to do that. It's like, it's not working, I can't clear my mind, I better get up and not do this. But really what's happening is it's self-therapy. It's just that instead of paying a therapist to sit there and listen to you, you're listening to yourself. And you just have to sit there as those emails go through one by one. You work through each of them until you get to the magical inbox zero. And there comes a day when you sit down, you realize the only things you're thinking about are things that happened yesterday, because you've processed everything else. Not necessarily even resolved it, but at least listened to yourself. And that's when meditation starts. And I think it's a very powerful thing that everybody should experience. And that's when you arrive upon the art of doing nothing.
turns.zh: 是的，这很有趣。当我刚开始冥想时，真的很难，对吧？因为每个人，我想很多听这个广播的人，他们听说过冥想，它名声很好。所以每个人都尝试，他们挣扎，然后有点放弃。这是那种每个人都说他们做但实际上没人做的事，对吧？就像不吃糖，对吧？每个人都谈论，是啊，我不吃糖，但是，然后甜点车来了，每个人都去拿饼干。是的，对吧。所以它变成了那样的事情。事实上，它甚至没有变成一个信号性的事情，比如，哦，你冥想了多少？我冥想了这么多。或者你知道，现在有人戴着发带，上面有啾啾鸟叫，当他们进入深度冥想时，我不知道他们是怎么做到的，但他们会说，我今天有很多次旅行。你得到了多少次旅行？哦，天哪，哦，你的冥想技巧是错的，我的是对的。但实际上，它只是无为的艺术。好吗？这很重要，因为我认为当我们长大时，对吧，生活中会发生所有这些事情。其中一些你在处理，一些你在吸收，一些你可能应该多想一想并解决，但你没有，你没有时间。所以它被埋在你体内。这些都是偏好、判断和未解决的情况和问题。就像你的电子邮件收件箱，一封又一封未回复的邮件堆积如山，追溯到10年、20年、30年、40年前。然后当你坐下来冥想时，那些邮件开始回击你：嘿，这个问题呢？那个问题呢？你解决了吗？你想过那个吗？你的遗憾在那里，你有问题在那里。这变得可怕。人们不想那样做。就像，它不起作用，我无法清空我的大脑，我最好站起来不做这个。但实际上发生的是自我治疗。只是你没有付费让治疗师坐在那里听你说话，而是你在听自己说话。你只需要坐在那里，让那些邮件一个一个地通过。你逐一处理它们，直到你达到神奇的收件箱为零。有一天你坐下来，你意识到你唯一在想的事情是昨天发生的事情，因为你已经处理了其他所有事情。不一定解决了，但至少听了自己。那时冥想才开始。我认为这是每个人应该体验的非常强大的事情。那时你就达到了无为的艺术。

turns.en: Well, I think it's even a problem that most people are getting their meditation from an app. I will not use an app. It's sneaky. I mean, Sam Harris has a very good meditation app, I should say that. But you should be able to just do it. And many people can. It is literally the art of doing nothing. Yeah, all you need to do for meditation is just sit down, close your eyes, comfortable position, whatever happens happens. If you think, you think. If you don't think, you don't think. Don't put effort into it, don't put effort against it. It's all you need.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为甚至有一个问题，大多数人从应用程序获得冥想。我不会使用应用程序。它很狡猾。我的意思是，Sam Harris 有一个非常好的冥想应用程序，我应该说一下。但你应该能够直接做。而且很多人可以。它实际上就是无为的艺术。是的，你冥想所需要做的就是坐下来，闭上眼睛，舒适的姿势，发生什么就是什么。如果你思考，你就思考。如果你不思考，你就不思考。不要投入努力，也不要对抗努力。这就是你所需要的。

turns.en: Do you concentrate on your breath or do you have a specific technique?
turns.zh: 你是专注于呼吸还是有特定的技巧？

turns.en: Nothing, nothing. No, you just sit. You just sit.
turns.zh: 没有，没有。不，你就坐着。你就坐着。

turns.en: I think about my breath. That's all I do. I just need to try to only concentrate on breathing. I used to do that, but at some level, all the concentration, every meditation technique is...
turns.zh: 我思考我的呼吸。这就是我所做的。我只需要尝试只专注于呼吸。我以前那样做，但在某种程度上，所有的专注，每一种冥想技巧都是……

turns.en: leading you to the same thing which is just witnessing. Yes, and concentration is a technique to still your mind enough that you can then drop the object of concentration. So you could also just try going straight to the end game. The problem with what I'm talking about, which is not focusing on your breath, is you will have to listen to your mind for a long time. It's not going to work unless you do at least an hour a day and preferably at least 60 days before you kind of work through a lot of issues. So it'll be hard for a while, but when you come out the other side, it's great. You get rid of the chatter, or when the chatter comes, it's in the background, it's dimmer, it's smaller. You've heard it before, you see the patterns, it's more recent, it's something you need to resolve anyway. And you will get moments of actual silence.
turns.zh: 这最终也会引导你到同一件事，那就是纯粹的观照。是的，专注是一种技巧，能让你的心足够平静，然后你就可以放下专注的对象。所以你也可以直接尝试进入最终状态。我所说的这种方法——不专注于呼吸——的问题在于，你必须长时间倾听你的思绪。除非你每天至少练习一小时，并且最好连续60天，否则它不会奏效，因为你需要先处理很多问题。所以一开始会很难，但当你走出来时，效果很棒。你会摆脱内心的杂音，或者当杂音出现时，它会在背景中，更暗淡、更微小。你以前听过它，你看清了模式，它更贴近当下，是你需要解决的事情。而且你会体验到真正的寂静时刻。

turns.en: What is your ultimate state when you meditate? Like, is there a state where you've achieved, rarely if ever, where you just... you're in bliss, or you're in harmony, or you're in enlightenment? Like what?
turns.zh: 你冥想时的终极状态是什么？有没有一种状态，你很少达到，甚至从未达到，就是你处于极乐、和谐或开悟之中？那是什么样的？

turns.en: It's kind of indescribable, because when you're really meditating, you're not there. When there's no thoughts, there's no experience, there's nothing, there's just nothing. So it's hard to describe. But I would say that every psychedelic state that people encounter using so-called plant medicines can be arrived just through pure meditation, and I've definitely hit some of those states. You've hit some transcendent psychedelic states where you're vacating the whole deal. I've had trippy visuals, I've had the kind of the lights and colors, I've had the so-called downloads, I've had the realizations, I've had the bliss, I've had the light, I've had the colors. But not every time. No, it's really... and in fact, I would say that that's also like an experience that you can start craving, which will then actually take you out of meditation. Where you really... and I'm not enlightened or anything close to it, so not even in the ballpark. But my own experience, and this is personal experience, is the place where I end up the most, that is really the one that I want to be at, is peace. It's just peace.
turns.zh: 这有点难以描述，因为当你真正冥想时，你并不在那里。当没有念头时，就没有体验，什么都没有，只有空无。所以很难形容。但我想说，人们通过所谓的植物药物体验到的每一种迷幻状态，都可以通过纯粹的冥想达到，我确实达到过其中一些状态。你达到过一些超然的迷幻状态，你完全放空了。我有过迷幻的视觉，有过那种光和色彩，有过所谓的下载，有过领悟，有过极乐，有过光明，有过色彩。但并非每次都是。不，真的……事实上，我想说，那也是一种你会开始渴望的体验，而这反而会让你脱离冥想。你真正……我并没有开悟，甚至离得很远，所以根本不在那个范围。但根据我自己的经验，这是个人经验，我最常达到的状态，也是我真正想要的状态，是平静。只是平静。

turns.en: Peace, happy?
turns.zh: 平静，快乐？

turns.en: Yeah. Peace to me is happiness at rest, and happiness is kind of peace in motion. You can convert peace to happiness anytime you want, but peace is what you want most of the time.
turns.zh: 是的。对我来说，平静是静止的快乐，而快乐是动态的平静。你可以随时把平静转化为快乐，但大多数时候你真正想要的是平静。

turns.en: That's interesting. You can convert peace to happiness anytime you want?
turns.zh: 这很有趣。你可以随时把平静转化为快乐？

turns.en: Yeah. If you're a peaceful person, anything you do will be a happy activity. And by the way, being on social media, engaging politics will not bring you peace. There is nothing less peaceful. Right? And in today's day and age, the way we think, you get peace by resolving all your external problems. But there are unlimited external problems. So the only way to actually get peace is on the inside, by giving up this idea of problems.
turns.zh: 是的。如果你是一个平静的人，你做的任何事都会是快乐的活动。顺便说一句，刷社交媒体、参与政治不会带给你平静。没有什么比这更不平静的了，对吧？在当今时代，我们以为通过解决所有外部问题就能获得平静。但外部问题是无限的。所以真正获得平静的唯一方法是从内心入手，放弃“问题”这个概念。

turns.en: Who thinks you can get peace by resolving external problems? Other than politicians?
turns.zh: 谁认为可以通过解决外部问题获得平静？除了政客？

turns.en: Everybody. That's what everybody's struggling to do. Right? Why are you trying to make money? To solve all your money problems. Why are you trying to win a political argument? Because then you'll be at peace because your people will have won. It's a daunting task to get your external world together. It's easier to change yourself than to change the world. That's true.
turns.zh: 每个人。这就是每个人都在努力做的事情，对吧？你为什么想赚钱？为了解决你所有的金钱问题。你为什么想赢得一场政治辩论？因为那样你就会平静，因为你这边的人赢了。把外部世界整理好是一项艰巨的任务。改变自己比改变世界更容易。这是真的。

turns.en: Yeah, and the best way to change the world is to change yourself. Exactly. It's all these people who are shouting on social media. The best way is just to actually live the life that you want other people to live. Like I went down to New Zealand and there's this guy that I met with. And you know, everyone's on social media shouting about environmentalism and conservation and sustainability. And I go to this guy's house and he was doing, very quietly, very gently, he was doing a two-week-long zero-waste experiment where he was throwing out nothing. So every package that he opened, he would keep and he would like clean it up. So he would keep his Amazon boxes, he would keep the little containers, even a tea bag. If you open the tea bag, he has to figure out how to compost the tea inside, how to make the tea itself useful, how to make the tea bag like a little storage item. So there was no trash. He was literally living with zero trash waste. And he was doing it. And it was really inspirational. Meeting people like him made me far more environmentally conscious than any amount of people yelling at me on social media ever will.
turns.zh: 是的，改变世界的最好方法就是改变自己。没错。就是那些在社交媒体上大喊大叫的人。最好的方法其实就是过你希望别人过的生活。比如我去新西兰，遇到了一个人。你知道，每个人都在社交媒体上大喊环保、保护和可持续发展。我去他家，他非常安静、非常温和地进行了一个为期两周的零浪费实验，什么都不扔。他打开的每一个包裹，他都会保留并清理干净。他会保留他的亚马逊盒子，保留小容器，甚至一个茶包。如果你打开一个茶包，他得想办法把里面的茶叶堆肥，让茶叶本身有用，让茶包变成一个小储物用品。所以没有垃圾。他真的过着零垃圾的生活。他做到了。这真的很鼓舞人心。遇到像他这样的人，让我比任何在社交媒体上对我大喊大叫的人都更环保。

turns.en: How long did he do that for?
turns.zh: 他那样做了多久？

turns.en: I think it was two weeks. It was hard. What the hell are you going to do with tea bags? He had quite the collection. He was filling them with little things. He sounds like a crazy hoarder, like a hoarder person with stacks of tea bags in his house. A very impressive guy.
turns.zh: 我想是两周。很难。你拿茶包怎么办？他收集了不少。他用它们装小东西。听起来像个疯狂的囤积者，像个囤积者，家里堆满了茶包。一个非常令人印象深刻的人。

turns.en: Yeah, that's a strange way to go about things. I appreciate that. I mean, look, it is entirely possible to somehow or another engineer all of our cups and all of our things and all of our stuff to be biodegradable. You know, the struggle with the modern environmental movement is that they identify the correct problem, which is finite Earth, spaceship Earth, this is all we got, don't ruin it. But they don't have the solution. So what they say is no growth, no growth, no growth. Well, the problem is you got three billion Indian and Chinese who aren't going to stay in poverty. They're going to grow whether you like it or not. So you can yell at them, you can scream at them, you can yell at us and scream at us, but that's not going to happen. So the only way out, unfortunately, is again through technology, which is you have to build green technology. And I give Musk a lot of credit for being one of the few people who's out there trying to do that. So you build things that are biodegradable and good for you and healthier. And everybody wants to be healthier. Chinese want to be healthier, Indians want to be healthier, they want to be cleaner. If you say I can clean up your rivers, I can clean up your forests, I can have your children not get sick with cholera and diphtheria and typhoid, I can cure your diseases, I can help make your immune system stronger, I can give you clean drinking water. Like that is what causes people to become environmentalists, not shouting and screaming at them that they shouldn't grow and they should stop pumping things into the sky. And you know, they have no concept of that. They're just trying to get out of poverty. So I think the modern environmental movement identifies the correct problem but then doesn't come up with the right set of solutions that are appealing to people. People are not going to give up economic growth. They're going to have to get rich first.
turns.zh: 是的，这是一种奇怪的做法。我很欣赏。我的意思是，完全有可能以某种方式设计我们所有的杯子、所有物品和所有东西都可生物降解。你知道，现代环保运动的困境在于，他们识别出了正确的问题——地球是有限的，太空船地球，我们只有这个，不要毁了它。但他们没有解决方案。所以他们说的是：不增长，不增长，不增长。但问题是，有30亿印度人和中国人不会一直处于贫困中。不管你喜欢与否，他们都会增长。所以你可以对他们大喊大叫，对我们大喊大叫，但那不会发生。所以不幸的是，唯一的出路还是通过技术，你必须开发绿色技术。我非常感谢马斯克，他是少数几个试图这样做的人之一。所以你要制造可生物降解、对你有益、更健康的东西。每个人都想更健康。中国人想更健康，印度人想更健康，他们想更干净。如果你说我能清理你的河流，我能清理你的森林，我能让你的孩子不得霍乱、白喉和伤寒，我能治愈你的疾病，我能帮助增强你的免疫系统，我能给你干净的饮用水。这才是让人们成为环保主义者的原因，而不是对他们大喊大叫，说他们不应该增长，不应该向天空排放东西。你知道，他们没有那个概念。他们只是想摆脱贫困。所以我认为现代环保运动识别出了正确的问题，但没有提出一套吸引人的正确解决方案。人们不会放弃经济增长。他们必须先富起来。

turns.en: Hmm, that's a very good point. But how do you do both?
turns.zh: 嗯，这是一个很好的观点。但如何两者兼顾呢？

turns.en: You lower the price of clean technologies massively. So you basically make clean technologies cost competitive through subsidy, technology, innovation. Ideally you can subsidize in the short to medium term until the innovation curve is crossed. I mean, like Tesla doesn't have any patents, right? Or they freely give them.
turns.zh: 你大幅降低清洁技术的价格。所以基本上通过补贴、技术、创新让清洁技术具有成本竞争力。理想情况下，你可以在短期到中期内进行补贴，直到跨越创新曲线。我的意思是，就像特斯拉没有任何专利，对吧？或者他们免费提供。

turns.en: Away their patents, that's the example of how you can do it. So you know, if you want to get rid of plastic straws, you can do it here and there, you can get San Francisco to ban plastic straws, but China's not going to ban plastic straws until you build a paper straw that is same cost, good durability, and then you educate the Chinese, like 'Hey, this plastic that you're using is petroleum, this is bad for you, here's the chemical composition, here's the things that are going in your bloodstream.' And they want healthy, happy kids also, so they're going to have their kids use paper straws. Maybe straws aren't the best example, but this is true with fossil fuels, for example, that's probably the best one, or replacing a lot of plastics with glass and paper, and so on. Yeah, there's a new technology that Rhonda Patrick had on her Twitter today about being able to convert plastic waste into fuel, and there are companies that are actively trying to do that now. So in that way, plastic waste will become valuable, right? And it'll become a commodity, it'll become something that people resource. Now, there are certain problems this doesn't solve. This doesn't solve carbon, this doesn't solve deforestation. So there you kind of have to step in with other means. For example, you look at the Amazon, right? Everyone's complaining about the Amazon being deforested. Well, you're not the poor Brazilian farmer, right? So you're sitting here in your comfortable chair, social media hammering away at the evil Brazilians for deforesting the Amazon. But the Amazon has incredible resources. If we really care about it, we should turn it into an incredible tourist park and put your money where your mouth is, start doing ecotourism in the Amazon, start paying for it, and then maybe take the future rights for all the pharmaceuticals that are going to come out of all the incredible plants there and start selling those off, so that maybe give the pharmaceutical companies an incentive to preserve the biodiversity of the Amazon. Say, 'Hey, if you buy this patch of the Amazon, you conserve it, and you can serve whatever plant medicines that come out of there that you can then license, you get the patent for 20 years or 30 years or whatever.' So I think there are solutions where we as the first worlders who have money can put our money where our mouth is and go and rescue these kinds of properties.
turns.zh: 放弃他们的专利，这就是一个例子。所以，如果你想摆脱塑料吸管，你可以这里那里地做，你可以让旧金山禁止塑料吸管，但中国不会禁止塑料吸管，除非你造出一种成本相同、耐用性好的纸吸管，然后你教育中国人，比如‘嘿，你们用的塑料是石油做的，这对你们有害，这是化学成分，这些会进入你们的血液。’他们也想要健康快乐的孩子，所以他们会让孩子用纸吸管。也许吸管不是最好的例子，但化石燃料也是如此，这可能是最好的例子，或者用玻璃和纸代替很多塑料等等。是的，有一项新技术，Rhonda Patrick 今天在她的推特上提到，能够将塑料垃圾转化为燃料，而且有公司正在积极尝试这样做。这样一来，塑料垃圾就会变得有价值，对吧？它会变成一种商品，变成人们可以资源化的东西。但有些问题这解决不了。这解决不了碳排放，解决不了森林砍伐。所以你需要用其他手段介入。例如，看看亚马逊，每个人都在抱怨亚马逊被砍伐。但你又不是贫穷的巴西农民，对吧？所以你坐在舒适的椅子上，在社交媒体上抨击邪恶的巴西人砍伐亚马逊。但亚马逊拥有不可思议的资源。如果我们真的在乎它，我们应该把它变成一个不可思议的旅游公园，把钱花在刀刃上，开始在亚马逊做生态旅游，开始为此付费，然后也许把未来所有来自那里神奇植物的药品权利拿出来出售，这样也许能给制药公司一个保护亚马逊生物多样性的激励。比如说，‘嘿，如果你买下这片亚马逊，你保护它，你可以从那里获得任何植物药物，然后你可以授权，获得20年或30年的专利等等。’所以我认为有一些解决方案，我们这些有钱的第一世界人可以言行一致，去拯救这些类型的财产。

turns.en: That's a very interesting solution, but I could see immediate pushback from people that don't think the pharmaceutical company should have the rights to this natural plant. Okay, or the government does it and then the government gets the patents and the government will auction off the patents later or they'll license them or whatever it is, right?
turns.zh: 这是一个非常有趣的解决方案，但我能立即看到来自那些认为制药公司不应该拥有这种天然植物权利的人的反对。好吧，或者政府来做，然后政府获得专利，政府以后会拍卖专利，或者授权它们，或者别的什么，对吧？

turns.en: Well, that's often the problem. There is no really good solution. There's a bunch of solutions that also have drawbacks. That's life, yeah. That's the trade-off. So it's being human, very messy. Yeah, it's a constrained environment. Obviously I skew more towards private property capitalist type solutions because even though they're not perfect, they have been proven to actually work. Right, once something is your property, you take care of it. You're not going to crap all over your own house. But it should probably be temporary property, not permanent property. You see a lot of countries around the world not doing this, no foreign ownership of land thing, for example. Or Mexico has no private ownership of beaches, right. So you can draw the line at certain points.
turns.zh: 嗯，这往往是问题所在。没有真正好的解决方案。有一堆解决方案也有缺点。这就是生活，是的。这就是权衡。所以作为人类，非常混乱。是的，这是一个受限的环境。显然我更倾向于私有财产资本主义类型的解决方案，因为即使它们不完美，它们已经被证明是有效的。没错，一旦某物是你的财产，你就会照顾它。你不会在自己的房子里到处拉屎。但它应该是临时财产，而不是永久财产。你看到世界上很多国家不这样做，比如不允许外国人拥有土地。或者墨西哥没有私人拥有海滩的权利。所以你可以在某些点上划清界限。

turns.en: Do you enjoy doing this kind of thing where you break things down and give your perspective on things and try to illuminate certain complex subjects?
turns.zh: 你喜欢做这种事吗？把事情分解，给出你的观点，试图阐明某些复杂的主题？

turns.en: I'm not trying to illuminate so much as, you know, talking to you I learn as much as I say, and I learn it for myself because I'm being forced to articulate it. Right, I can sit around and think my thoughts all day long, but a lot of it is going to be nonsense. It's not, I'm going to because there are gaps in thinking where you make leaps because you're kind to yourself that you don't realize you're making. But when you're forced to write it down, and this is why I tweet, or when you have to talk to somebody, you have to complete those gaps and make it a proper logical chain. And the mistake that I made when I was young was, you know, I always wanted to seem like the smartest kid in the room, just like you probably want to seem like the funniest kid in the room or the toughest kid in the room. Right, we're all losers starting out, we want to be winners, so we pick the thing we're good at and we double down on it. So I always wanted to be the smartest kid in the room. So what did I do? I read a lot of books, I memorized a lot of things, and then whatever I hadn't memorized, pre-Google, I made it up so it sounded good. Okay, pre-Google. After Google, fact checking started, and I had to get better. Right, so Google improved me that way. A lot of people exactly. So now what I realize is that the biggest mistake with memorization, right, because when you're actually trying to live your life in congruence with reality, you want to have a deep understanding of what you do and why you do it. And so it's much more important to know the basics really well than to know the advanced. Knowing calculus wouldn't help you today. It doesn't help you in business, doesn't help you in most things. But knowing arithmetic really will help you, really, whether it's at the corner grocery store counting change, to figuring out the value of your podcast business, to figuring out how to do the probability math on some action that you want to take. So understanding basic mathematics cold is way more important than memorizing calculus concepts. And the problem is, and this is true of all reasoning I think, it's much better to know the basics from the ground up, solid foundation of understanding, a steel frame of understanding, than it is to just have a scaffolding of memorizing advanced concepts. This is why there are a lot of people, I'm sure that you listen to, who are really smart, they use a lot of jargon, and you can't quite follow their reasoning, you don't know how they're putting things together, and you have this deep down suspicion they don't even really understand. Right, so if you look at the most powerful thinkers, especially the ones where money or life is on the line, they have to understand the basics really, really well. Richard Feynman, the physicist, was able to, he had this piece in one of his lectures where he takes you from counting numbers on your hand all the way to calculus in four pages of text. Orally, but written down is four pages of text, and it's a complete unbroken logical chain that takes you through geometry, trigonometry, precalculus, analytic geometry, graphs, everything, all the way to calculus.
turns.zh: 与其说我在试图阐明，不如说，和你交谈，我学到的东西和我说的差不多，我为自己学习，因为我被迫把它表达出来。没错，我可以整天坐着想我的想法，但其中很多会是胡说八道。不是，因为思维中有一些空白，你对自己宽容，做了跳跃，却没有意识到。但当你被迫写下来——这就是为什么我发推文——或者当你必须和别人交谈时，你必须填补那些空白，形成一个完整的逻辑链条。我年轻时犯的错误是，我总是想成为房间里最聪明的孩子，就像你可能想成为房间里最有趣的孩子或最强壮的孩子一样。没错，我们一开始都是失败者，我们想成为赢家，所以我们选择自己擅长的东西，加倍努力。所以我总是想成为房间里最聪明的孩子。那我做了什么？我读了很多书，记住了很多东西，然后对于我没记住的，在谷歌出现之前，我就编造，让它听起来不错。好吧，谷歌之前。谷歌之后，事实核查开始了，我必须变得更好。没错，谷歌就这样提升了我。很多人都是这样。所以现在我意识到，记忆的最大错误是，当你真正试图与现实一致地生活时，你想对你所做的事情和为什么做有深刻的理解。所以真正掌握基础比知道高级的东西重要得多。知道微积分今天帮不了你。它在商业中帮不了你，在大多数事情中也帮不了你。但知道算术真的会帮你，无论是在街角杂货店找零，还是计算你的播客业务的价值，还是计算你想采取的某个行动的概率。所以彻底理解基础数学比记住微积分概念重要得多。问题是，我认为所有推理都是如此，从头开始了解基础，一个坚实的理解基础，一个钢铁般的理解框架，比仅仅有一个记住高级概念的脚手架要好得多。这就是为什么有很多人，我相信你听过，他们非常聪明，用了很多行话，但你不太能跟上他们的推理，你不知道他们如何把东西组合在一起，你内心深处怀疑他们甚至并不真正理解。没错，如果你看看最强大的思想家，尤其是那些涉及金钱或生命的人，他们必须把基础理解得非常非常好。物理学家理查德·费曼，他在一次讲座中有一个部分，他带你从用手指数数一直到微积分，只有四页文本。口头上，但写下来是四页文本，这是一个完整不间断的逻辑链条，带你通过几何、三角、微积分预备、解析几何、图形，一切，一直到微积分。

turns.en: He understood numbers at a core level. He didn't have to memorize anything. When you're memorizing, it's an indication that you don't understand. You should be able to rederive anything on the spot, and if you can't, you don't know it.
turns.zh: 他在核心层面理解数字，不需要死记硬背。当你背诵时，说明你没理解。你应该能当场重新推导任何东西，如果不能，你就没掌握。

turns.en: So do you apply that to things other than mathematics?
turns.zh: 那你把这一点应用到数学以外的事情上吗？

turns.en: Everything. Everything. You don't even make an attempt to memorize things, just make an attempt to understand them. You can't help but memorize things. But if you can't—and this is where Twitter is great for me—I try to understand something and then I try to write it down in such a way that I can remember it, just the basic hook that'll point towards the deeper understanding. And I'm forced to explain it to people, and that's how I know I understand something.
turns.zh: 所有事。所有事。你甚至不要试图去记忆，只管去理解。你自然会记住。但如果你做不到——这就是推特对我的好处——我试着理解某件事，然后试着把它写下来，以便我能记住，只记住能指向深层理解的基本钩子。而且我被迫向别人解释，这样我就知道我真的理解了。

turns.en: So this is what I meant originally. We talked about reading a good book: I'll read one page in a night, and then I spend the rest of the night thinking about it, or I'm chasing down references on Wikipedia or weird blog posts trying to understand it.
turns.zh: 这就是我最初的意思。我们聊过读一本好书：我一晚上只读一页，然后整晚思考它，或者去维基百科或奇怪的博客上追查参考文献，试图理解它。

turns.en: You know, for example, there was a—I was dealing with this a few months back—I was dealing with a question of, stupid topic, but the meaning of life, right? What's the meaning?
turns.zh: 比如，几个月前我处理过一个话题，很老套，但就是生命的意义，对吧？意义是什么？

turns.en: How could that be stupid though?
turns.zh: 这怎么会老套呢？

turns.en: Well, it's just trite. It's trite. You're not supposed to think about it. It's something you ask your parents when you're young, they tell you don't worry about it, or they'll get a job.
turns.zh: 嗯，就是陈词滥调。你不该去想它。这是你小时候问父母的问题，他们告诉你别担心，或者让你去找工作。

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. Good job, you freaking hippy. Or here's God: God is the meaning of life, right?
turns.zh: 对，没错。好好工作，你这个嬉皮士。或者上帝：上帝是生命的意义，对吧？

turns.en: And so I was just trying to resolve for myself, like, what could the answer be? Right? Not what is the answer, but what could the answer be? And so at a core level, I was forced to kind of hunt down all these weird little things and really understand for myself. And it's got to be personal, right? But I have to establish for myself what it could and could not be, and that gave me some level of peace.
turns.zh: 所以我只是想为自己解决，答案可能是什么？不是答案是什么，而是答案可能是什么？所以在核心层面，我被迫去追查所有这些奇怪的小东西，真正为自己理解。而且这必须是个人化的，对吧？但我必须为自己确立它可能是什么、不可能是什么，这给了我某种程度的平静。

turns.en: So now I have to keep asking that question: what is the meaning of life? I mean, you—I think the question is more interesting than the answer. Everyone should explore this on their own, but let me just explore a few parts with you, right?
turns.zh: 所以现在我必须继续问这个问题：生命的意义是什么？我是说，我觉得问题比答案更有趣。每个人都应该自己探索，但让我和你一起探索几个部分，好吗？

turns.en: So first is, if I gave you an answer—if I said the meaning of life has to please God—well, which God? Okay, Judeo-Christian God. Well, okay, why that one? Why this thing? The problem is it's a 'why' question. You can keep asking why forever, right? Any answer I give you, you'll just ask why again, why again. That's right. And you end up in a place called Agrippa's Trilemma. Okay, this is a philosophical exercise. But I kind of thought it through, then Googled around, and there's a thing called Agrippa's Trilemma. And Agrippa's Trilemma says that any questioning like this 'why' will always end in one of three places. Okay, first is infinite regress, right? Why? Because of this. Why that? What? And just keep going forever. The second is circular reasoning: well, A why? Because of B. Or why B? Because of A, right? You get trapped in that. Or the third is an axiom. And the most popular axiom is God, but it could be anything: because of math, because of science, because the Big Bang, because the simulation, right? These are all axioms. These are all just stopping points. Saying 'simulation, we're in a simulation' or saying 'it's the Big Bang' is just another way of saying God. It's just God's a dirty word, so we don't use it as much anymore, but same thing. So you end up in one of these three dead ends, essentially, right? So there is no answer.
turns.zh: 首先，如果我给你一个答案——如果我说生命的意义是取悦上帝——那么，哪个上帝？好吧，犹太-基督教上帝。那为什么是那个？为什么是这个？问题在于这是一个“为什么”的问题。你可以一直问为什么，对吧？我给你任何答案，你都会再问为什么，再问为什么。没错。然后你会陷入一个叫“阿格里帕三难困境”的地方。好吧，这是一个哲学练习。但我自己思考过，然后谷歌了一下，有一个叫阿格里帕三难困境的东西。阿格里帕三难困境说，任何这样的“为什么”追问最终都会走向三个结局之一。第一是无限回溯，对吧？为什么？因为这个。为什么那个？什么？然后一直问下去。第二是循环论证：A为什么？因为B。或者B为什么？因为A，对吧？你被困在里面。第三是公理。最流行的公理是上帝，但也可以是任何东西：因为数学，因为科学，因为大爆炸，因为模拟，对吧？这些都是公理。这些都只是停止点。说“模拟，我们在模拟中”或者说“是大爆炸”只是另一种说上帝的方式。只是上帝是个脏词，所以我们不再那么常用，但一回事。所以你最终会陷入这三个死胡同之一，对吧？所以没有答案。

turns.en: The real answer is 'because'. Right? What is the meaning of life? Yeah, you get to make up your own answers. The beauty: if there was a single answer, we would not be free. We would be trapped, because then we would all have to live to that answer. Then we'd be bored like robots, each one competing with each other to fulfill that single meaning more than the others. Back to signaling: 'I'm better at it than you are.' But luckily, there is no answer, so you just do whatever you want.
turns.zh: 真正的答案是“因为”。对吧？生命的意义是什么？是的，你可以自己编造答案。美妙之处在于：如果有一个单一的答案，我们就不会自由。我们会被困住，因为那样我们都必须按照那个答案生活。然后我们会像机器人一样无聊，每个人都互相竞争，比其他人更好地实现那个单一意义。又回到信号传递：“我比你更擅长。”但幸运的是，没有答案，所以你想做什么就做什么。

turns.en: It's funny that that—that's the basis of all existential angst: that you don't know why you're here, and you have this feeling that it could be meaningless. It is. I mean, when you start pondering the multiverse, the universe, the galaxies, the solar system, the planet, the organism, the cells inside the organism, the bacteria, the parasites, right? The symbiotic relationship we have to our environment. And you start going, 'Jesus Christ, what am I? Just a little piece of this thing.' It's like, well, the answers to all the great questions or paradoxes. Yeah. So for example, you're asking like, 'Do I matter?' That's really the question you asked, right? 'Well, how do I matter in this infinite universe?' Well, you know, on the one hand, you're separate. No two points are the same. Every point is—every two points are infinitely different. You're completely separated. No one will have your thoughts, your emotions, your feelings, your experience. So your life is a single-player game. You're trapped inside your head, and you're just aware of a bunch of things going on, and that's it. On the other hand, I cannot say the word 'Joe Rogan' without invoking the entire universe. Joe alien comes along, says, 'What's that? Joe Rogan? What's Joe Rogan? A human. What's a human? Bipedal ape. What's an ape? On the Earth. What's the Earth? Planet. What's a planet? Solar system. Where was the carbon made? Inside stars.' Right? Instead, I have to create the entire universe to just say the words 'Joe Rogan'. So in that sense, you're connected to everything. It's inseparable. So the answer to that question of 'Do I matter?' is 'I am nothing and I am everything.' And you'll find this with all the great questions: the answers are all paradoxes. Which is why, at some level, it's sort of pointless to pursue them to find a trite answer like I'm giving. But the act of pursuing them is actually really useful, because then it gives you certain intrinsic understanding in your life that brings a level of peace.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，这正是所有存在焦虑的基础：你不知道自己为什么在这里，而且你感觉这可能是无意义的。确实如此。我是说，当你开始思考多重宇宙、宇宙、星系、太阳系、行星、有机体、有机体内的细胞、细菌、寄生虫，对吧？我们与环境的共生关系。然后你开始想，“天哪，我是什么？只是这个东西的一小部分。”就像，所有伟大问题的答案都是悖论。是的。比如，你在问，“我重要吗？”这其实就是你问的问题，对吧？“在这个无限的宇宙中，我如何重要？”嗯，一方面，你是独立的。没有两个点是相同的。每个点——每两个点都是无限不同的。你完全独立。没有人会有你的思想、你的情感、你的感受、你的经历。所以你的生活是一场单人游戏。你被困在自己的脑袋里，你只是意识到一堆事情在发生，仅此而已。另一方面，我不能说出“乔·罗根”这个词而不牵涉整个宇宙。外星人乔来了，问：“那是什么？乔·罗根？乔·罗根是什么？人类。人类是什么？双足猿。猿是什么？在地球上。地球是什么？行星。行星是什么？太阳系。碳是在哪里制造的？在恒星内部。”对吧？相反，我必须创造整个宇宙才能说出“乔·罗根”这个词。所以从这个意义上说，你与一切相连。它是不可分割的。所以“我重要吗？”这个问题的答案是“我什么都不是，我是一切。”你会发现所有伟大问题都是这样：答案都是悖论。这就是为什么在某种程度上，追求它们以找到一个像我给出的陈词滥调答案是没意义的。但追求它们的行为实际上非常有用，因为它会给你生活中某种内在的理解，带来一定程度的平静。

turns.en: I feel like with many people, this stress of this question is also accentuated by unhappy lives. It's actually accentuated by unhappy choices, by being trapped. There's a big difference between not knowing what the meaning of life is and 'God, I gotta get out of this job. I have to—I can't live my life this way. What's the meaning of life if this is my life?' Which is why I always start with 'Let's get you rich first,' right? That's why I'm very practical about it. Because look, the Buddha was a prince, okay? He started out really rich, and then he got to go off in the woods. And in the old days, what happened was, if you wanted to be peaceful inside, you would become a monk. You would renounce everything. You would become an ascetic. You would give everything up. You renounce women, men, you'd renounce children, you'd renounce money, you'd renounce politics, science, technology, everything. And you would go out in the woods by yourself. You could give everything up to be free inside. Well, today we have this wonderful invention called money, where you can just store stuff up in a bank account, okay? And you can basically save up. You can work really hard, you can—
turns.zh: 我觉得对很多人来说，这个问题的压力也被不快乐的生活放大了。它实际上是被不快乐的选择、被困住放大的。不知道生命的意义和“天哪，我得离开这份工作。我不得不——我不能这样过我的生活。如果这就是我的生活，生命的意义是什么？”之间有很大区别。这就是为什么我总是从“先让你变富”开始，对吧？这就是为什么我对它非常务实。因为你看，佛陀是个王子，好吗？他一开始非常富有，然后他才能去森林里。在过去，如果你想要内心平静，你会成为僧侣。你会放弃一切。你会成为苦行者。你会放弃一切。你放弃女人、男人，你放弃孩子，你放弃金钱，你放弃政治、科学、技术，一切。然后你独自去森林里。你可以放弃一切以获得内心自由。好吧，今天我们有这个奇妙的发明叫做金钱，你可以把东西存在银行账户里，好吗？而且你基本上可以存钱。你可以非常努力地工作，你可以——

turns.en: You can do great things for society, and society will give you money for giving it things that it wants and doesn't know how to get. Then you can save that up, live well below your means, and find a certain freedom in that. That will give you the time and energy to pursue your own internal peace and happiness. So I believe the solution to making everybody happy is to give them what they want. Let's get them all rich, let's get them all fit and healthy, and then let's get them all happy.
turns.zh: 你可以为社会做伟大的事，社会会给你钱，因为你给了它想要却不知道如何获取的东西。然后你可以存起来，生活远低于你的能力范围，从中找到某种自由。这会给你时间和精力去追求内心的平静和幸福。所以我相信让每个人都快乐的办法就是给他们想要的。让他们都富有，都健康，然后让他们都快乐。

turns.en: Are those things even possible? Everyone can be rich?
turns.zh: 这些事情可能吗？每个人都能富有？

turns.en: Everyone can be rich. Here's my thought exercise for you. Now it seems like we're in an infomercial. Everyone can be rich—I'm not selling my home, this is my Rolls-Royce. So that's a good point. Everything I've ever created on this topic of how to make money, I will never charge a dollar for, because that would ruin it. That would show that I'm just another huckster trying to get rich off you. There are no get-rich-quick schemes; that's just somebody else trying to get rich off you. So to me, it's more of a philosophical contribution. For it to have meaning and be legit, I can't charge you anything for it. But yes, everybody can be rich. Let me give you a thought exercise. Imagine if tomorrow we could wave a wand and everybody was trained as a scientist or an engineer. Everybody, even if you weren't very good, you had enough understanding of computers, you could write some code, you could build some hardware. And don't tell me people can't do it, because they can. That's just the tyranny of soft expectations, that's just you looking down on somebody else. They can't do it? They just have to be educated. Now if they're educated all as hardware and software engineers, scientists, biologists, technicians, hard sciences—not the social sciences—we would all be done within five years. Robots would be doing everything from cleaning toilets to cooking food to flying airplanes to driving Ubers. And what would we be doing? We would be doing all creative jobs to entertain each other and researching science and technology. We would have wonderful lives. So it is really just a question of education, nothing else.
turns.zh: 每个人都能富有。我给你一个思维实验。现在听起来像电视购物广告。每个人都能富有——我不是在卖我的房子，这是我的劳斯莱斯。所以这点说得好。我创作的所有关于如何赚钱的内容，我永远不会收一分钱，因为那会毁了它。那会显得我只是另一个想从你身上赚钱的骗子。没有快速致富的捷径，那只是别人想从你身上赚钱。所以对我来说，这更像是一种哲学贡献。为了让它有意义且合法，我不能向你收费。但没错，每个人都能富有。我给你一个思维实验。想象一下，如果明天我们挥动魔杖，每个人都成为科学家或工程师。每个人，即使你不是很好，你对计算机有足够的了解，能写点代码，能造点硬件。别告诉我人们做不到，因为他们可以。那只是软期望的暴政，只是你看不起别人。他们做不到？他们只需要接受教育。如果他们都接受教育，成为硬件和软件工程师、科学家、生物学家、技术人员、硬科学——不是社会科学——我们五年内就能完成。机器人会做所有事情，从打扫厕所到做饭，到开飞机，到开优步。那我们会做什么？我们会做所有创意工作来娱乐彼此，并研究科学和技术。我们会过上美好的生活。所以这真的只是教育的问题，没有别的。

turns.en: Is this a scale issue though? I mean, you're talking about it as if this would work with 300 million people.
turns.zh: 但这是规模问题吗？我的意思是，你说得好像这对3亿人都有效。

turns.en: It'll work with 10 billion people. It'll work with a spacefaring race with 100 trillion people. We have the resources, we have the ability. The universe is infinite resources. You build it. Have you heard of a Dyson sphere? You pull the Dyson sphere on a star and you gather all its energy. There's so much energy out there. One asteroid has all the minerals we need. One sun, one solar system has all the power we will need for a long, long time. We can extract it out of nuclear fusion. We're not that far from those kinds of technologies working. It's just a question of guts and interest. Like, we should be building nuclear fusion test plants on the moon. The moon should be littered with them. There's no downside.
turns.zh: 它对100亿人也有效。它对一个拥有100万亿人的太空文明也有效。我们有资源，有能力。宇宙资源无限。你建造它。你听说过戴森球吗？你把戴森球套在一颗恒星上，收集它所有的能量。外面有那么多能量。一颗小行星就有我们需要的所有矿物。一个太阳，一个太阳系就有我们很长时间需要的所有能量。我们可以通过核聚变提取它。我们离这些技术实现并不远。这只是胆量和兴趣的问题。比如，我们应该在月球上建造核聚变试验厂。月球上应该到处都是。没有坏处。

turns.en: Yeah, if you could, how would that work? Well, it doesn't send a bunch of people up there to work.
turns.zh: 是的，如果你能做到，那会怎么运作？嗯，不是送一堆人上去工作。

turns.en: Oh, the problem? Robots. The problem with fission, nuclear fission, is that nature creates energy through nuclear energy, like the sun creates energy through nuclear energy. Now for transmission, we use photons because photons don't interact. Photons are great for information transmission, but they're actually not great for energy transmission. For energy creation, you want nuclear to work. And the problem is because nuclear energy—we built it with a bomb, we have dirty nukes, all those kinds of problems with Fukushima, Three Mile Island, Chernobyl. We don't innovate anymore on nukes. Imagine if when the first steam engine blew up, we said, 'Oh, no more steam engines for a while,' or very carefully regulated, billions of dollars of regulation. You can't innovate that way. When the first airplane crashed, we said, 'No more innovation in airplanes.' So we need a way to iterate on nuclear fission and eventually fusion and get them working safely, cleanly, passive fail-safe, etc., if we're going to find our way out of the energy trap. And the best place to do that is somewhere like on the moon or Mars.
turns.zh: 哦，问题？机器人。裂变，核裂变的问题是，大自然通过核能创造能量，比如太阳通过核能创造能量。现在传输时，我们使用光子，因为光子不相互作用。光子很适合信息传输，但实际上不太适合能量传输。对于能量创造，你想要核能工作。问题在于，因为核能——我们用炸弹建造了它，我们有脏弹，所有那些福岛、三哩岛、切尔诺贝利的问题。我们不再在核能上创新了。想象一下，如果第一台蒸汽机爆炸时，我们说‘哦，暂时不要蒸汽机了’，或者非常严格地监管，数十亿美元的监管。你不能那样创新。第一架飞机坠毁时，我们说‘飞机不再创新了’。所以我们需要一种方法来迭代核裂变，最终是核聚变，让它们安全、清洁、被动安全地工作，等等，如果我们想摆脱能源陷阱。而做这件事的最佳地点是像月球或火星这样的地方。

turns.en: Do you think it's actually a possibility that they could get nuclear power to the point where it's not a detriment? Because what everyone's worried about is a meltdown.
turns.zh: 你认为他们有可能让核能发展到没有危害的程度吗？因为每个人都担心熔毁。

turns.en: Yeah, and we do have these old plants that are running on 50-year-old technology. It's crazy because there's no ability to shut them off. Very old technology. They do now have Gen 4 nuclear reactors that are passive fail-safe. So in other words, when they fail, they fail into a safe state. When you pull the plug on them, they fail into a state where there's no leakage, no problem. Their default is a positive outcome, as opposed to the current ones, the old ones, where if you unplug them... And these Gen 4 are just Gen 4, not Gen 5, not Gen 80, not Gen 100, where we are in microprocessors. And that should be something that people are working towards. I hope so. In an ideal world, we would. Now the problem is, if you have nuclear energy on the moon, how do you get it home? So what you actually have to do is you have to use it on the moon, maybe to launch more satellites, more rockets further out into the solar system. That's the initial use case. But then eventually the technology gets so good you can bring it home.
turns.zh: 是的，我们确实有这些老电厂，运行着50年前的技术。这很疯狂，因为无法关闭它们。非常老的技术。他们现在有第四代核反应堆，是被动安全的。换句话说，当它们失效时，它们会进入安全状态。当你拔掉插头时，它们会进入无泄漏、无问题的状态。它们的默认状态是积极结果，而不是现在的那些老式反应堆，如果你拔掉插头……而这些第四代只是第四代，不是第五代，不是第八十代，不是第一百代，就像我们在微处理器领域那样。这应该是人们努力的方向。我希望如此。在理想世界里，我们会。现在的问题是，如果你在月球上有核能，你怎么把它带回家？所以实际上你必须在月球上使用它，也许用来发射更多卫星、更多火箭到更远的太阳系。这是最初的应用场景。但最终技术会变得非常好，你可以把它带回家。

turns.en: I want to go back to this idea of getting people rich. How does that make people happy? How do you stop the natural progression that people have of, 'Oh, I have a nice Chevrolet, but I really want a BMW. I have a nice BMW, but now I want a Mercedes. I have a Mercedes, I want a Ferrari.' How do you stop that material possession trap? Because you can't at some level.
turns.zh: 我想回到这个让人富有的想法。那怎么让人快乐呢？你怎么阻止人们自然的进程，比如‘哦，我有一辆不错的雪佛兰，但我真的想要一辆宝马。我有一辆不错的宝马，但现在我想要一辆奔驰。我有奔驰，我想要法拉利。’你怎么阻止那个物质占有陷阱？因为在某种程度上你无法阻止。

turns.en: But I think most smart people over time realize that possessions don't make them happy. It's just you have to go through that. You have to buy your stupid car to realize that it doesn't attract attractive girls. It actually just attracts other dudes who are like, 'Hey, I like that car, man.' Like, you have some expensive cars out there, some fancy cars. Tell me how much that attracts women versus men. Well, I'm married, those are for me. I just enjoy machines. So for me, they're toys. That's a particular thing where you enjoy machines. But I think as you get older, you just realize that there's no happiness in material possessions. Now, lack of material possessions can make you very unhappy. So being poor can make you unhappy, but being rich is not going to make you happy. And what happens unfortunately is a lot of people struggle through their whole lives to make money, they make some, they're exhausted, and then they're like, 'Well...'
turns.zh: 但我认为大多数聪明人随着时间的推移会意识到，物质财富并不能让他们快乐。只是你必须经历那个过程。你必须买你那辆愚蠢的车，才意识到它并不能吸引有吸引力的女孩。它实际上只会吸引其他男人，他们会说‘嘿，我喜欢那辆车，哥们。’比如，你有一些昂贵的车，一些花哨的车。告诉我那吸引女性和男性的比例是多少。嗯，我结婚了，那些是给我自己的。我只是喜欢机器。所以对我来说，它们是玩具。那是你享受机器的一种特殊方式。但我认为随着你变老，你会意识到物质财富中没有幸福。然而，缺乏物质财富会让你非常不快乐。所以贫穷会让你不快乐，但富有并不会让你快乐。不幸的是，很多人一生都在努力赚钱，他们赚了一些，筋疲力尽，然后他们想，‘嗯……’

turns.en: Now why am I not happy? I guess I'm just not a happy person, and smart people aren't happy. That's like a great little way for people to feel better about it. They say, 'Well, if you're smart, you're not happy, right?' Where's the positive the other way? If you're smart, you should be able to figure out how to be happy. Otherwise, you're not that smart.
turns.zh: 为什么我不快乐？我想我只是个不快乐的人，聪明人都不快乐。这就像是个让人自我安慰的好说法。他们说：‘如果你聪明，你就不快乐，对吧？’那反过来呢？如果你聪明，你应该能想办法让自己快乐。否则，你也没那么聪明。

turns.en: Yeah, that is an offensive statement, that if you're smart you're not happy. I've heard that before, and I just do not understand the logic of that other than self-justifying.
turns.zh: 是啊，说聪明就不快乐，这话挺冒犯的。我以前听过，除了自我辩解，我实在不明白这逻辑。

turns.en: I understand where it comes from. It comes from: if you're smart, it's usually because you think things through and you have a very busy mind. A busy mind can often rob you of peace of mind, because the peace we seek is not peace of mind, it's peace from mind. So if you look at all the crazy activities you do to be happy—whether it's trying to get laid and have an orgasm, or extreme sports, or looking at something beautiful, or taking a psychedelic—you're trying to get out of your own mind. You're trying to get your monkey mind to stop chattering at you for a moment. You're trying to get peace from the mind. There are other, better ways to do that. Most of the ways we try to get peace from mind are indirect, whereas if you understand things, if you see things properly, you will naturally slowly develop peace from mind. Sorry if I went on a tangent there.
turns.zh: 我明白这说法从哪来的。它来自：如果你聪明，通常是因为你深思熟虑，脑子很忙。脑子忙常常会让你失去内心的平静，因为我们寻求的平静不是内心的平静，而是远离内心的平静。所以，看看你为了快乐做的那些疯狂事——不管是想上床达到高潮，还是极限运动，或者看美景，或者嗑药——你都是在试图逃离自己的大脑。你试图让猴子大脑停止对你喋喋不休。你试图获得远离内心的平静。还有其他更好的方法。我们大多数试图远离内心的方法都是间接的，而如果你理解事物，如果你正确看待事物，你会自然而然地慢慢获得远离内心的平静。抱歉我跑题了。

turns.en: No, it's a good tangent. It's a good tangent because I think that oftentimes the pursuit is what's thrilling to people, and the possibility that one day they'll be able to rest and that they'll reach this goal—that's the fundamental delusion: that there is something out there that will make me happy and fulfill forever.
turns.zh: 不，这是个好跑题。这是个好跑题，因为我认为很多时候，追求本身才是让人兴奋的，而有一天能休息、能达到目标的可能——那是个根本的错觉：以为外面有什么东西能让我永远快乐和满足。

turns.en: Yeah, golden years. There is, it's called death. That'll take care of everything. That's the great leveler.
turns.zh: 是啊，黄金岁月。有的，叫死亡。那会解决一切。那是伟大的平等器。

turns.en: But when people look at, particularly social media, bring it back to that: when you see someone posed in front of their mansion with their beautiful car, leaning against it with their designer clothes and expensive watch, you go, 'I want that, right? That's what I want.' What you really want is freedom. You want freedom from your money problems. And I think that's okay.
turns.zh: 但当人们看社交媒体时，回到那个话题：当你看到有人站在豪宅前，靠着豪车，穿着名牌衣服，戴着昂贵手表，你会想：‘我想要那个，对吧？那就是我想要的。’你真正想要的是自由。你想从金钱问题中解脱出来。我觉得这没问题。

turns.en: So once someone can solve their money problems—either by lowering their lifestyle or by making enough money—essentially what you want is to get everybody to retirement. But not retirement in the 'I'm 65 years old sitting in a nursing home collecting a check' sense. Different definition: retirement is when you stop sacrificing today for some imaginary tomorrow. When today is complete in and of itself, you're retired. So how do you get there? One is you can have so much money saved up that just your passive income covers your burn rate without you lifting a finger—keep your burn rate low. A second is you drive your burn rate down to zero; you become a monk. A third is you're doing something you love, you enjoy it so much it's not about the money. There are multiple paths, but the most common is people just say, 'I need to make more money.' The kind of wealth creation I talk about is about creating timeless principles and adapting yourself so that making money won't be an issue, and you can do it by doing what you love.
turns.zh: 所以一旦有人能解决他们的金钱问题——要么降低生活水平，要么赚够钱——本质上你想让每个人都退休。但不是那种‘我65岁坐在养老院领支票’的退休。不同的定义：退休是你停止为了某个想象中的明天而牺牲今天。当今天本身是完整的，你就退休了。那么怎么达到呢？一种是你存够钱，被动收入就能覆盖你的开销，你什么都不用做——保持低开销。第二种是把开销降到零，你成为和尚。第三种是你做自己喜欢的事，你非常享受，跟钱无关。有多条路，但最常见的是人们只说‘我需要赚更多钱’。我谈的那种财富创造是关于创造永恒的原则和调整自己，这样赚钱就不是问题，你可以通过做自己喜欢的事来赚钱。

transcript.sec: The Trap of Working for Things

turns.en: I think people have to be very careful to not get trapped along the way with things that you can afford with your current lifestyle—the way you're living and the way you're earning—but they're also imprisoning you. The fact that you are now going to have to work this 40-hour-a-week job in order to get this thing that you can't afford, but now you're saddled down to this job. You're not saving, you're not putting things in a good place, and you're working for these things. Working for things as rewards is a real trap that a lot of people fall into. It's the biggest one.
turns.zh: 我认为人们必须非常小心，不要被那些你当前生活方式能负担的东西困住——你的生活方式和收入方式——但它们也在囚禁你。事实上，你现在必须每周工作40小时来得到你买不起的东西，但你现在被这份工作拖累。你没有储蓄，没有把东西放在好位置，你在为这些东西工作。把工作当作奖励是一个真正的陷阱，很多人都会掉进去。这是最大的陷阱。

turns.en: You know, Nassim Taleb also says that under two great addictions: heroin and a monthly salary. And that's why you can't get rich renting out your time, because even when you start charging more and more for your time, it's a slow upgrade loop. Then you upgrade your house at the same time, you break your car at the same time, you move in the neighborhood. You really also have to get used to ignoring your peers or upgrading or changing the definition of your peers. A lot of people here who are poor here would be rich if they were living in Thailand and Bali, and if they have the luxury of a remotely doable job, they may want to be living there and saving up money.
turns.zh: 你知道，纳西姆·塔勒布也说过，两大成瘾物：海洛因和月薪。这就是为什么你无法通过出租时间来致富，因为即使你开始为自己的时间收取更多费用，这也是一个缓慢的升级循环。然后你同时升级房子，同时弄坏车，你搬进那个社区。你真的还必须学会忽视你的同龄人，或者升级或改变你对同龄人的定义。这里很多穷人如果住在泰国和巴厘岛，如果他们有远程可行的工作，他们可能会想住在那里存钱。
