turns.en: We get into this model of 'I must work for other people, work my way up the ladder, I must do what that person is doing to make money.' But really, today in society, you get rewarded for creative work, for creating something brand new that society didn't even know it wanted, that it doesn't know how to get other than through you. So the most powerful money makers are actually individual brands—people like yourself, or Elon, or Kanye, or Oprah, or Trump. These are eponymous name brands who themselves are leveraged. Like you're leveraged: you have a podcast media going out to everybody, that's leveraged. The podcast works for you when you sleep. They have knowledge that nobody else has, which is your knowledge—the knowledge of being Joe Rogan. I mean, who else is a UFC fighter and a commentator and a podcaster and a comedian and interested in all these things? All these people can't replace you. So we have to pay you what you're worth.
turns.zh: 我们陷入这种模式：‘我必须为别人工作，一步步往上爬，我必须像那个人一样赚钱。’但实际上，在今天的社会，你因为创造性工作而获得回报，因为创造了社会还不知道自己想要的全新东西，社会除了通过你之外不知道如何得到它。所以最强大的赚钱机器实际上是个体品牌——像你这样的人，或者埃隆、坎耶、奥普拉、特朗普。这些都是以自己名字命名的品牌，他们自己被杠杆化了。就像你被杠杆化了：你有一个播客媒体传播给每个人，这就是杠杆。播客在你睡觉时为你工作。他们拥有别人没有的知识，那就是你的知识——作为乔·罗根的知识。我是说，还有谁是UFC选手、评论员、播客主持人、喜剧演员，并且对所有这些东西感兴趣？所有这些人都无法取代你。所以我们必须付给你你应得的。

turns.en: I never fought in the UFC though.
turns.zh: 但我没在UFC打过啊。

turns.en: Oh, you didn't? Okay, sorry. Or you're involved in that whole scene. You just have a unique set of skill sets. So because of this unique—what I call specific knowledge—because of the accountability that you have with your name, because the leverage that you have through your media, you're a money-making machine. I'm sure at this point I could make you start over tomorrow, wipe out your bank account, you'd be rich again in no time because you have all the skill sets. The beauty is the way you've done it is you don't have any competition. There's no substitution. If Joe Rogan were to disappear off the air tomorrow, it's not like random podcaster number 12 would step in and fill that thing. No, it's just gone. So the way to get out of that competition trap is actually to be authentic. The way to retire is actually to find the thing that you know how to do better than anybody, and you know how to do it better than anybody because you love to do it. No one can compete with you if you love to do it. Be authentic, and then figure out how to map that to what society actually wants. Apply some leverage, put your name on it so you take the risks but you gain the rewards. Have ownership and equity in what you do, and then just crank it out.
turns.zh: 哦，你没打过？好吧，抱歉。或者你参与了整个场景。你只是有一套独特的技能组合。所以，由于这种独特的——我称之为特定知识——由于你名字带来的责任感，由于你通过媒体拥有的杠杆，你是一台赚钱机器。我敢肯定，现在我可以让你明天重新开始，清空你的银行账户，你很快就会再次富有，因为你拥有所有这些技能。美妙之处在于，你做到这一点的方式是你没有任何竞争。没有替代品。如果乔·罗根明天从电波中消失，不会是随便哪个播客主持人第12号来填补那个空缺。不，它就没了。所以摆脱竞争陷阱的方法实际上是做真实的自己。退休的方法实际上是找到你比任何人都擅长的事情，而且你比任何人都擅长是因为你喜欢做。如果你喜欢做，没人能和你竞争。做真实的自己，然后想办法把它映射到社会真正想要的东西上。应用一些杠杆，把你的名字放在上面，这样你承担风险，但获得回报。拥有你所做事情的所有权和股权，然后只管做出来。

transcript.sec: The Trap of Excuses and Victim Mentality

turns.en: But ignoring the peers is an issue because keeping up with the Joneses is a real phenomenon. Yeah, envy makes the world go around, right?
turns.zh: 但忽视同辈是个问题，因为攀比心理是真实存在的。是啊，嫉妒让世界运转，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah, and then there's another thing that people have to avoid: even allowing their mind to think when they're hearing what you're saying and all this logical fantastic advice, there's these six dirty words: 'That's easy for you to say.' Yeah, that is a terrible trap. I mean, look, I grew up as a first-generation immigrant in Jamaica, Queens, with zero money, single mom, two kids, working day and night, going to school. You know, I washed dishes, I was working catering jobs, I was mowing lawns, I was working since the age of 11 on and off. Didn't have two cents to rub together. I had to borrow $400 to go to college. Like literally, I was short $400. I had to find $400, I didn't have it. Got rejected from a job at Dunkin' Donuts. So, okay, it's not to say that it's easy. It's not easy. It's actually really freaking hard. It is the hardest thing you will do, but it's also the most rewarding thing. You know, look at the kids who are born rich: no meaning to their lives. You're in a terrible place. Your real resume is just a cataloging of all your suffering. Right? If I were to ask you to describe your real life to yourself, when you look back on your deathbed, you're going to go back and say, 'What are the interesting things I've done?' And it's all going to be around the sacrifices that you made and the hard things that you did. Anything you're given doesn't matter. You have your four limbs, you have your brain, you have your head, you have your skin. That's all for granted. So you have to do hard things anyway to create your own meaning in life. Making money is a fine one. Yeah, struggle. It is hard. I'm not gonna say it's easy. It's really hard, but the tools are all available. It's all there. There's also these traps that people sort of establish in their own mind of giving themselves excuses or giving themselves insurmountable obstacles, insurmountable paths. Victim mentality. Yes, it's somebody else's fault. It's my skin color's fault. It's the system's fault. Yeah, those people are sinking. I feel bad for them. I want to shake them out of it and say, 'Actually, you can get out of it. You just have to stop thinking it's everybody else. You have to alter the perspective.' Yeah, but it's so difficult for people to do. It's one of the most difficult things for people to do is to change the way they approach reality itself.
turns.zh: 是的，还有另一件事人们必须避免：当他们听到你说的这些逻辑性很强的绝妙建议时，他们甚至允许自己的思维产生这六个脏字：“你说得倒轻松。”没错，那是一个可怕的陷阱。我的意思是，你看，我作为第一代移民在皇后区的牙买加长大，身无分文，单亲妈妈，两个孩子，没日没夜地工作，还要上学。你知道，我洗过盘子，做过餐饮工作，修剪过草坪，我从11岁起就断断续续地工作。口袋里没有一分钱。我不得不借400美元去上大学。真的，我就差400美元。我必须找到400美元，但我没有。还被唐恩都乐的工作拒绝了。所以，好吧，我不是说这很容易。这并不容易。实际上非常非常难。这是你将做的最难的事情，但也是最有回报的事情。你知道，看看那些生来富有的孩子：他们的生活没有意义。你处于一个糟糕的境地。你真正的简历就是你所有苦难的清单。对吧？如果我让你向自己描述你的真实生活，当你躺在临终床上回顾时，你会回想并说：“我做过哪些有趣的事情？”那将全部围绕你做出的牺牲和你做过的艰难事情。任何给予你的东西都不重要。你有四肢，你有大脑，你有头，你有皮肤。这些都是理所当然的。所以无论如何你都必须做艰难的事情来为自己创造生命的意义。赚钱是一个不错的选择。是的，奋斗。这很难。我不会说这很容易。这真的很难，但工具都是可用的。都在那里。还有一些陷阱，人们在脑海中为自己建立这些陷阱，给自己找借口或设置不可逾越的障碍、不可逾越的道路。受害者心态。是的，这是别人的错。是我肤色的错。是体制的错。是的，那些人正在沉沦。我为他们感到难过。我想把他们摇醒，说：“实际上，你可以摆脱它。你只需要停止认为这是别人的错。你必须改变视角。”是的，但这对人们来说非常困难。人们最难做到的事情之一就是改变他们接近现实本身的方式。

turns.en: At the end of the day, I do think even despite what I said earlier, life is really a single-player game. It's all going on in your head. You know, whatever you think you believe will very much shape your reality, both from what risks you take and what actions you perform, but also just your everyday experience of reality. If you're walking down the street and you're judging everyone, you're like, 'I don't like that person because their skin color. I don't like that. Oh, she's not attractive. That guy's fat. This person's a loser. Oh, who put this in my way?' You know, the more you judge, the more you're gonna separate yourself. And you'll feel good for an instant because you'll feel good about yourself: 'I'm better than that.' But then you're gonna feel lonely, and then you're just gonna see negativity everywhere. The world just reflects your own feelings back at you. Reality is neutral. Reality has no judgments. To a tree, there's no concept of right or wrong or good or bad. Right? You're born, you have a whole set of sensory experiences and stimulations and lights and colors and sounds, and then you die. Yeah, and how you choose to interpret that is up to you. You do have that choice. So this is what I meant that happiness is a choice. If you believe it's a choice, then you can start working on it. And I can't tell you how to find it because it's your own conditionings that are making you unhappy, so you have to uncondition yourself. It's just like I can't fix your eating habits for you. I can give you some general guidelines, but you gotta go through the hard habit forming of how to eat right. But you have to believe it's possible, and it is absolutely possible. I was miserable. I'm happy as a clam.
turns.zh: 归根结底，我确实认为，即使我之前说过那些，生活真的是一场单人游戏。一切都发生在你的脑海里。你知道，无论你认为自己相信什么，都会极大地塑造你的现实，不仅是你承担的风险和采取的行动，还有你日常的现实体验。如果你走在街上评判每一个人，你会想：“我不喜欢那个人，因为他们的肤色。我不喜欢那个。哦，她不漂亮。那个家伙很胖。这个人是个失败者。哦，谁把这个挡在我路上了？”你知道，你评判得越多，你就越会孤立自己。你会瞬间感觉良好，因为你会自我感觉良好：“我比他们强。”但随后你会感到孤独，然后你只会看到处处都是消极。世界只是把你的感受反射回给你。现实是中性的。现实没有评判。对一棵树来说，没有对错或好坏的概念。对吧？你出生，你有一整套感官体验、刺激、光线、颜色和声音，然后你死去。是的，你如何选择解读这些取决于你。你确实有这个选择。所以这就是我所说的幸福是一种选择。如果你相信这是一个选择，那么你就可以开始努力。我不能告诉你如何找到它，因为是你自己的条件反射让你不快乐，所以你必须解除这些条件反射。就像我不能替你改变你的饮食习惯一样。我可以给你一些一般性的指导，但你必须经历养成正确饮食习惯的艰难过程。但你必须相信这是可能的，而且绝对可能。我以前很痛苦。我现在快乐得像一只蛤蜊。

turns.en: It's not just the money. I got there before the money. You got happy before the money? Mostly, yeah. How did you get happy before the money?
turns.zh: 不只是钱。我在有钱之前就达到了那种状态。你在有钱之前就快乐了？大部分是的。你怎么在有钱之前就快乐了？

turns.en: I started getting older, you know. I just realized like life is short. I'm going to die. Again, trite, right? Try it in 20 ways. Yeah, well, Confucius had a great saying that every man has two lives, and the second starts when he realizes he has just one. Wow, and I read that. It was one of those book-dropping lines, you know, it's like mic drop. Confucius had a lot of mic drops. That was a great one. Or another one is: next time you get sick, you know, because everybody gets sick every now and then. It's like a happy person wants 10,000 things; a sick person just wants one thing. Right? So it's your unlimited desires that are clouding your peace, your happiness. Have desires. Your biological creature stands up and says, 'I can do something. I move, I resist, I live.' But just be very careful of your desires. This is the oldest, most trite wisdom: desire is suffering. That's what it means. Right? Every desire you have is an excess where you will suffer. So just don't focus on more than one desire at a time. The universe is rigged in such a way that if you just want one thing and you focus on that, you'll get it. But everything else you gotta let go.
turns.zh: 我开始变老了，你知道。我只是意识到生命短暂。我会死。再说一遍，老生常谈，对吧？用20种方式试试。是的，孔子有句名言：每个人都有两条命，第二条始于他意识到自己只有一条命。哇，我读到过。那是那种让人合上书的名言，就像麦克风掉落一样。孔子有很多这样的名言。那是个好句子。或者另一个是：下次你生病的时候，你知道，因为每个人都会时不时生病。快乐的人想要一万样东西；生病的人只想要一样东西。对吧？所以是你无限的欲望遮蔽了你的平静和幸福。要有欲望。你的生物本能站起来说：“我能做点什么。我移动，我抵抗，我活着。”但要对你的欲望非常小心。这是最古老、最陈腐的智慧：欲望即痛苦。就是这个意思。对吧？你的每一个欲望都是一个让你受苦的额外负担。所以一次只专注于一个欲望。宇宙的运作方式是这样的：如果你只想要一件事并专注于它，你就会得到它。但其他一切你都必须放手。

turns.en: Did you make a gradual shift to happiness or was it a radical change?
turns.zh: 你是逐渐转向幸福，还是突然改变的？

turns.en: It's ongoing. It's gradual every day. So you're happier today than you were a month ago? Yeah, allegedly. Yeah, yeah. I'm very happy these days, deliriously so. It's actually hard for me to hang out with normal people. Really? Yeah. So you've made a significant shift over the period of like how many years? About eight years. Eight years. Yeah, wow. And is this something that you've pursued through certain books or is it just like you've made an understanding or gained an understanding in your own mind and then started pursuing it based on that understanding?
turns.zh: 这是持续进行的。每天都在逐渐变化。所以你今天比一个月前更快乐？是的，据说是这样。是的，是的。这些天我非常快乐，快乐得有些飘飘然。实际上我很难和普通人相处。真的吗？是的。那么你在大约多少年的时间里发生了显著的转变？大约八年。八年。是的，哇。这是你通过某些书籍追求的东西，还是你只是在自己的头脑中理解或获得了理解，然后基于那种理解开始追求它？

turns.en: Yeah, it's very, very personal. It's basically you have to decide it's a priority, and then I tried every hack I possibly could. I used to, you know, I tried all the meditation, I tried witnessing. You know, I even tried an SSRI just to see what it would feel like. How did it feel? It turned me from a pessimist to an optimist, but I didn't like the physical side effects, nor did I want to be on a drug for a sustained basis, so I dropped it. And it did turn you into an optimist? Yes, at the time. I used to be a pessimist. Yeah. I started doing things like I would start looking at, you know, in every moment and everything that happens, you can look on the bright side of something, right? And so I used to do that forcibly, and then I trained it until it became second nature.
turns.zh: 是的，这非常非常个人化。基本上你必须决定把它作为优先事项，然后我尝试了所有我能想到的方法。我以前，你知道，我尝试了所有的冥想，我尝试了见证。你知道，我甚至尝试过SSRI，只是想看看它是什么感觉。感觉如何？它把我从一个悲观主义者变成了一个乐观主义者，但我不喜欢身体上的副作用，也不想长期服用药物，所以我放弃了。它确实把你变成了乐观主义者？是的，当时是这样。我以前是个悲观主义者。是的。我开始做一些事情，比如我会开始看，你知道，在每一个时刻和发生的每一件事中，你都可以看到积极的一面，对吧？所以我过去强迫自己这样做，然后我训练它，直到它成为第二天性。

turns.en: For example, a friend of my wife's was over when we were dating, and she took hundreds of photos and sent them all to us. My immediate reaction was, 'Why are you dumping hundreds of photos on my phone? I don't need hundreds of photos. Have some judgment.' That's my immediate reaction. Then I could say, 'Actually, how nice of her. She sent me hundreds of photos so I can pick the one I like.' There are two ways of seeing almost everything. There are a few things that are high suffering, so you can't do that other than just saying, 'Well, this is a teacher.' But I slowly worked through every negative judgment I had until I saw the positive end. Now it's second nature to me.
turns.zh: 比如，我妻子的一位朋友在我们约会时来过，她拍了上百张照片，然后全发给了我们。我的第一反应是：‘你为什么往我手机里倒几百张照片？我不需要几百张照片，有点判断力好吗。’这是我的本能反应。然后我可以说：‘其实她真好，发给我几百张照片，这样我就能挑一张我喜欢的。’几乎所有事情都有两种看法。有些事痛苦程度很高，你没法那样做，只能告诉自己‘这是个教训’。但我慢慢克服了每一个负面判断，直到看到积极的一面。现在这对我来说已经是本能了。

turns.en: I also realized that what you want is to clear your mind. You want to let go of thoughts. Happy thoughts disappear out ahead automatically; it's very easy to let go of them. Negative thoughts linger. So if you interpret everything positively, you let it go much faster. Simple hacks: get more sunlight, learn to smile more, learn to hug more. These things actually release serotonin in reverse; they aren't just outward signals of being happy, they're actually feedback loops to being happy. Spend more time in nature. These are obvious. Watch your mind all day long. Watch what it does, don't judge it, don't try to control it. You can meditate 24/7. Meditation is not a sit-down, close-your-eyes activity. Meditation is just basically watching your own thoughts like you would watch anything else in the outside world, and saying, 'Why am I having that thought? Does that serve me anymore? Is that conditioning from when I was 10 years old?'
turns.zh: 我还意识到，你想要的是清空大脑，放下念头。快乐的念头会自动消散，很容易放下。负面念头则会挥之不去。所以如果你凡事都往积极方面解读，你就能更快地放下。简单的小技巧：多晒太阳，多微笑，多拥抱。这些行为实际上会反向释放血清素；它们不只是快乐的外在信号，更是通往快乐的反馈回路。多接触大自然。这些都是显而易见的。整天观察你的思绪，看它在做什么，不要评判，不要试图控制。你可以全天候冥想。冥想不是一种坐下闭眼的活动。冥想基本上就是像观察外界任何事物一样观察你自己的念头，然后问：‘我为什么会有这个念头？它对我还有用吗？这是不是我十岁时的条件反射？’

turns.en: For example, getting ready for this podcast: you got ready, I didn't. But I did. I couldn't help it. What happened was, in the few days leading up to this, my mind was just running. Normally my mind is pretty calm, and it was just running and running. Every thought I had, I would imagine me saying it to you. My brain couldn't help but rehearse. It's just rehearsing all the time to talk to you. Then I was even rehearsing telling you about the rehearsal. So it was playing all these meta games, and I was just like, 'Shut up, stop it, what is going on?' It took me a while to figure out: oh yeah, when I was a kid in Queens, I had no money and nothing, and I needed to save myself. The way I got out was by sounding smart, not being smart—sounding smart. That was the skill I perfected. So I am hardwired to always rehearse things so I will sound smart. It's a disease; it keeps me from being happy. But when you see that, when you realize that, when you understand something, then it naturally calms you down. So after that, I stopped rehearsing as much. But it's still a trained habit.
turns.zh: 比如，为这期播客做准备：你准备了，我没有。但我确实准备了，我控制不住。事情是这样的：在播客前的几天里，我的思绪一直在运转。通常我的思绪很平静，但那时它不停地转。每一个念头，我都会想象自己说给你听。我的大脑忍不住在排练，一直在排练怎么跟你说话。然后我甚至排练了告诉你我在排练这件事。所以它在玩所有这些元游戏，我就想：‘闭嘴，停下，怎么回事？’我花了一段时间才弄明白：哦，对，我小时候在皇后区，没钱没资源，我需要自救。我摆脱困境的方式是听起来很聪明，而不是真的聪明——听起来聪明。那是我磨练出来的技能。所以我天生就总是排练，以便让自己听起来聪明。这是一种病，它让我无法快乐。但当你看到这一点，意识到这一点，理解它的时候，它就会自然地让你平静下来。在那之后，我就不怎么排练了。但这仍然是一个根深蒂固的习惯。

turns.en: That is a really interesting point, that you want to sound smart. Many people do that, especially young people. When you see someone who is smart or appears smart, they say smart things. 'Oh god, I want to sound smart. I want people to think about me the same way I think about that person.' That is my disease, that is my feeling. It is what clutters my mind. The thing I have to ask myself now is: if I could, would I still be interested in learning this thing if I couldn't ever tell anybody about it? That's how I know it's real, that's how I know it's something I actually want to know.
turns.zh: 这真是个有趣的观点，你想听起来聪明。很多人都会这样，尤其是年轻人。当你看到一个聪明或看起来聪明的人，他们会说些聪明的话。‘天哪，我想听起来聪明。我希望别人对我的看法和我对那个人的看法一样。’这就是我的病，我的感觉。它让我的思绪变得杂乱。我现在必须问自己：如果我可以，但永远不能告诉任何人，我还会对学习这件事感兴趣吗？这样我就知道它是不是真的，是不是我真正想知道的。

turns.en: That's a common thing though. I know I suffered from that when I was young, the desire to sound smart. It's very common. Well, all of us start out as losers. Everything you're a winner at now in your life, you were a loser at some point. If you had gotten all the girls, if you had all the money, if you had everything you wanted, if you were good looking in junior high or high school, you wouldn't have done anything with your life and you would have peaked early. It's like the Bruce Springsteen 'Glory Days' song. You would have married your high school sweetheart, you'd be living in your hometown, you'd be a manager at the local McDonald's, whatever the first dream job you had. Thank god we didn't all get what we wanted when we were young, or we would be trapped in that. So you have to be able to break out of where you came from.
turns.zh: 不过这是很常见的事。我知道我年轻时也为此苦恼，想听起来聪明。这很普遍。我们所有人一开始都是失败者。你现在生活中赢得的每一件事，你在某个时刻都曾是输家。如果你在初中或高中时得到了所有女孩、所有钱、所有你想要的东西，长得又帅，那你的人生就不会有任何成就，你会早早达到顶峰。就像布鲁斯·斯普林斯汀的《光荣岁月》那首歌。你会娶了你的高中恋人，住在你的家乡，在当地麦当劳当经理，或者你最初梦想的工作。谢天谢地，我们年轻时没有都得到我们想要的，否则我们就会被困在那里。所以你必须能够突破你的出身。

turns.en: That is interesting too, about people who peak too early. Maybe those people that peak too early can do the Elon Musk thing and just abandon it and start something new, and then learn the joys of sucking at something.
turns.zh: 关于那些过早达到顶峰的人，这也很有意思。也许那些过早达到顶峰的人可以像埃隆·马斯克那样，直接放弃，开始新的事情，然后学习在某件事上做得很烂的乐趣。

turns.en: Yeah, and actually in our professions, especially when you're high visibility, the problem with peaking is that you then get drowned in a death of a thousand cuts. People have expectations of you. 'Hey Joe, can you come to my event? Hey Joe, can you look at my business plan? Hey Joe, can you give me advice on this? Can you talk to my friend? Can you come on this podcast?' You're just being assaulted all the time with inbound opportunities. So you have no time to start over with anything. So you have to ruthlessly disappoint everybody, eliminate and clear your schedule, drop all the meetings, not even respond to the emails. That's the only way you're going to be able to start over with anything.
turns.zh: 是的，实际上在我们的职业中，尤其是当你知名度很高时，达到顶峰的问题在于你会被千刀万剐地淹没。人们对你有期望。‘嘿，乔，你能来我的活动吗？嘿，乔，你能看看我的商业计划吗？嘿，乔，你能给我点建议吗？你能跟我朋友谈谈吗？你能来这个播客吗？’你一直受到各种机会的冲击。所以你没有时间重新开始任何事情。所以你必须无情地让所有人失望，清空你的日程，取消所有会议，甚至不回邮件。这是你能够重新开始任何事情的唯一方法。

turns.en: Yeah, and I love your approach to meetings. If they're not life or death, I'm the same way. I avoided a good one recently. This was someone that was just tracking me down as a high-profile person in a big organization, and I'm like, 'Can we just talk on the phone?' And then we talk on the phone, there was nothing to say. It was just they wanted to get me in the office.
turns.zh: 是的，我喜欢你对待会议的方式。如果不是生死攸关，我也一样。我最近就避开了一个重要的会议。那是一个在大机构里很有名的人，一直追着我，我就说：‘我们能电话聊吗？’然后我们通了电话，没什么可说的。他们就是想让我去办公室。

turns.en: Yeah, and meetings should really be phone calls, phone calls should be emails, and emails should just be texts. Many of them. With meetings, I despise them. I used to own the domain idonotdocoffee.com. I eventually let it go, but I used to respond from that wallet: 'I don't do coffee.' That was hilarious, but it was a bit of a jerk move. But really, where it comes from is when I was young, one of my principles was I knew I had to make money. It was my overwhelming desire. One of the things I did was I said, 'Okay, I'm never going to be worth more than what I think I'm worth. No one's going to pay me more than what I think I'm worth. So what am I worth?' So I picked an hourly rate for myself that I was worth, and I said I'm never going to squander my time for less than this. So originally it was $500 an hour, then I upgraded to $5000 an hour. It's ludicrous, but pick an aspirational hourly rate. It has to be a little ludicrous. Then what I would do is, if I have to return something and I'm standing in line to return something and it's below my hourly rate, I'll throw it away or give it away.
turns.zh: 是的，会议应该变成电话，电话应该变成邮件，邮件应该变成短信。很多都是这样。对于会议，我深恶痛绝。我以前拥有域名 idonotdocoffee.com。我后来放弃了，但我以前会用那个钱包回复：‘我不喝咖啡。’那很搞笑，但有点混蛋。但真正的原因是我年轻时的一个原则：我知道我必须赚钱。那是我压倒一切的欲望。我做的其中一件事是，我说：‘好吧，我的价值永远不会超过我自认为的价值。没有人会付给我比我自认为的价值更多的钱。那么我值多少钱？’所以我给自己定了一个时薪，我说我永远不会把时间浪费在低于这个时薪的事情上。最初是每小时500美元，然后我升级到每小时5000美元。这很荒谬，但选一个有抱负的时薪。它必须有点荒谬。然后我会这样做：如果我要退货，排队退货的时间低于我的时薪，我就扔掉或送人。

turns.en: If you're going to do anything great, the extreme example is business travel: getting on a plane to fly halfway around the world for one meeting which never amounts to anything, and then wasting your whole life there and then flying back. So about five years ago I resolved: I am never going to travel for business. Wow. And I haven't traveled for business since. I only travel if the travel experience itself will be so entertaining and joyous because I have friends or it's a place I want to see or whatever, that it will be complete in and of itself. Because I know that whatever the business meeting I came from is never worth it. Wow. And actually that principle applies larger than just travel; it applies to life in general.
turns.zh: 如果你想做任何伟大的事，极端的例子就是商务旅行：坐飞机飞到地球另一端参加一个毫无结果的会议，然后在那里浪费你的一生，再飞回来。所以大约五年前我下定决心：我再也不为商务旅行了。哇。从那以后我再也没有为商务旅行过。我只有在旅行体验本身足够有趣和愉快时才会旅行，因为我有朋友在那里，或者那是我想去的地方，等等，这样旅行本身就完整了。因为我知道无论什么商务会议都不值得。哇。实际上，这个原则不仅适用于旅行，也适用于整个生活。

transcript.sec: Hedonic adaptation and doing things for their own sake

turns.en: One of the secrets to happiness is to really embrace what you're doing in that moment. That's trite, but where that comes from is saying: I only want to do actions that are complete in and of themselves. Right? If I'm looking for some ulterior motive down the line, it's not going to materialize. And if you think it is, maybe even if it does, it'll be very short-lived. Anything you wanted in your life—whether it was a car, or a girl, or money—when you got it, a year later you were back to zero. Your brain had hedonically adapted to it, and you were looking for the next thing.
turns.zh: 幸福的秘诀之一就是真正拥抱你此刻正在做的事情。这听起来老套，但它的来源是：我只想做那些本身完整的行为。对吧？如果我期待某种未来的动机，它不会实现。即使你认为它会，即使它真的实现了，也会非常短暂。你生命中想要的任何东西——无论是车、女孩还是钱——当你得到它时，一年后你又回到了零点。你的大脑已经享乐适应了它，你开始寻找下一个东西。

turns.en: That's a great statement: hedonically adapted. That is what happens to people. You get accustomed to whatever it is. I realized that when I first got a new apartment, it was a nice apartment. After a while I got used to it. I was like, 'Oh okay, this is just an apartment. It's just where I live. I'm used to it. It's nice, but I'm used to it.'
turns.zh: 说得好：享乐适应。这就是人们会发生的。你会习惯任何事物。我意识到当我第一次得到新公寓时，那是个不错的公寓。过了一段时间我就习惯了。我想，‘哦好吧，这只是一个公寓。只是我住的地方。我习惯了。它很好，但我习惯了。’

turns.en: Yeah, we all go through this. It's riding the Ferris wheel of life. It's like you get at the bottom, 'I want to get to the top, this is so exciting.' You ride it up, you get a little dopamine rush and a little serotonin, then you ride it back down as that wears off. Then you need another high, then you ride it back up and ride it back down. In fact, the more highs you get, the harder it gets to go around the wheel, and the more bored you get, the harder it goes to go back up.
turns.zh: 是的，我们都经历过。这就像乘坐人生的摩天轮。你在底部，‘我想到达顶部，这太令人兴奋了。’你升上去，得到一点多巴胺冲击和一点血清素，然后随着效果消退，你又降下来。然后你需要另一个高潮，再升上去再降下来。事实上，你得到的高潮越多，转轮就越难，你越无聊，再升上去就越难。

turns.en: So what lights your fire now? What gets you motivated to do things and to act?
turns.zh: 那么现在什么能点燃你的热情？什么能激励你去做事和行动？

turns.en: Art. Art is just creativity. It's just anything that's done for its own sake. So what are the things that are done for their own sake? There's nothing beyond loving somebody, creating something. Playing art. To me, creating business is play. I create businesses early stage because it's fun, because I'm into the product. Even when I invest, it's because I like the people, I like hanging out with them, I learn from them, and I think the product is really cool. So these days I will pass on all kinds of great investments because I'm like, 'I just the product's not interesting, it's boring, I'm not gonna learn anything.'
turns.zh: 艺术。艺术就是创造力。就是任何为了自身而做的事情。那么哪些事情是为了自身而做的呢？除了爱一个人、创造一些东西，没有别的。玩艺术。对我来说，创造企业就是玩。我创建早期企业是因为有趣，因为我喜欢产品。即使我投资，也是因为我喜欢那些人，我喜欢和他们相处，我从他们那里学习，而且我认为产品很酷。所以现在我会放弃各种很好的投资，因为我觉得，‘产品不有趣，很无聊，我学不到任何东西。’

turns.en: That's a beautiful luxury. It is a luxury.
turns.zh: 那是一种美丽的奢侈。确实是奢侈。

turns.en: Art and learning. Yeah, it is a luxury. These are not 100 or zero things, right? You can in your life start moving more and more towards that, right? But that's a goal. When I was younger, I used to be so desperate to make money that I would have done anything. Right? If you'd shown up and said, 'Hey, I got a sewage trucking business and you're going to go into that,' I said, 'Great, let's do it, I'm going to make money.' Thank God no one gave me that opportunity. I'm glad that it went down the road of technology and science, which I genuinely enjoy. And so I got to combine my vocation and my advocation.
turns.zh: 艺术和学习。是的，那是奢侈。这些不是非黑即白的事情，对吧？你可以在生活中逐渐向那个方向靠拢，对吧？但那是一个目标。我年轻的时候，曾经非常渴望赚钱，愿意做任何事。对吧？如果你出现说，‘嘿，我有一个污水卡车运输生意，你要加入，’我会说，‘太好了，我们干吧，我要赚钱。’谢天谢地没人给我那个机会。我很高兴它走上了科技和科学的道路，这是我真正喜欢的。所以我得以将我的职业和爱好结合起来。

turns.en: I mean, what are you doing? You're playing, you're having fun, you're doing art, you're not working.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你在做什么？你在玩，你在享受，你在做艺术，你不是在工作。

turns.en: No, that's what I always say. When people say I work hard, I'm like, 'Sorta, not really.' I'm always working, but it looks like work to them, but it feels like play to me. And that's how I know no one can compete with me on it, because I'm just playing 16 hours a day. And if they want to compete with me and they're going to work, they're going to lose, because they're not going to do it 16 hours a day, seven days a week.
turns.zh: 不，我一直这么说。当人们说我工作努力时，我会说，‘算是吧，也不完全是。’我总是在工作，但对他们来说看起来像工作，对我来说感觉像玩。这就是为什么我知道没有人能在这方面与我竞争，因为我一天只玩16个小时。如果他们想和我竞争，并且他们要去工作，他们会输，因为他们不会一天16小时、一周7天都这样做。

turns.en: Well, listen man, there are some gems of wisdom in this conversation, and I hope people pull things out of this and apply them to their own life. And I'm certainly going to listen to you again and try to apply some of this to my own life, stuff that I'm not already applying. But I really appreciate your time and I really appreciate you coming in here.
turns.zh: 嗯，听着，老兄，这次对话中有一些智慧的瑰宝，我希望人们能从中提取出东西并应用到自己的生活中。我当然会再听你的话，并尝试将其中一些应用到我的生活中，那些我还没有应用的东西。但我真的很感激你的时间，也很感激你来到这里。

turns.en: Thanks for having me. And please tell people—you those small little podcast, it's just the Naval podcast, right? Yeah. Best way to find me is on Twitter, actually. Okay, just @Naval. I have a website at nav.al, I have a YouTube channel, Naval, and I have a podcast in the world. That's it.
turns.zh: 谢谢你邀请我。请告诉大家——你那个小小的播客，就是Naval播客，对吧？是的。找到我的最好方式是在Twitter上。好的，就是@Naval。我有一个网站nav.al，我有一个YouTube频道Naval，我还有一个播客。就这些。

turns.en: Well, thank you very much. Thank you, really appreciate it. Thank you. Bye everybody.
